chapters.summary: Council staff opens the hearing, welcoming attendees to the Executive Budget Hearing for the Committees on Finance, Health, Mental Health, and Hospitals. Attendees are asked to silence electronics and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Hearing called to order and housekeeping announcements

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to the New York City Council Executive Budget Hearing on the Committee on Finance, joined with the Committee on Health, Committee on Mental Health and Substance Use, and the Committee on Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronics and do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Again, please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you need assistance, please contact the Sergeant at Arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you for your cooperation.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, you may begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Linda Lee opens the FY27 Executive Budget Hearing, introducing the DOHMH Public Health portion. She outlines the proposed $1.59B public health budget, noting increases for disease outbreak resilience, mobile food vending, TB management, and sexual health clinics, while flagging concerns about federal funding uncertainty and staffing shortfalls.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opening remarks on FY27 DOHMH public health budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, remember to do that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the eighth day of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and today we'll begin with the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene, Public Health section, followed by Department of Health and Mental Hygiene for Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm pleased to be joined by our speaker, Speaker Menon, and my colleague, Chair of the Committee on Health, Councilmember Lynn Schulman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined today by Council Members Narcisse, Areola, and Felder, who are both on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Marte, Councilmember Wong, Councilmember Wilson, and Councilmember Cabán.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And welcome to our commissioner, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Alistair Martin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, and Carmen De La Rosa.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome, Commissioner, to you and your team for being with us this morning, and thank you all for answering our questions today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So on May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY27-30 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Health and Mental Hygiene's public health proposed FY27 budget of $1.59 billion represents 2.1% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is an increase of $59.7 million or 3.9% from the $1.54 billion originally budgeted in the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, mostly tied to an additional $17.6 million for disease outbreak resilience, $12 million for the mobile food vending expansion, $7.2 million for tuberculosis management, and $4 million for sexual health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, DOHMH public health section headcount was 202 less than their fiscal 2026 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the FY27 preliminary budget hearing, we raised concerns regarding disease prevention and treatment, chronic disease prevention, health equity, and immunization programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We were pleased to see that many of these priorities were reflected in the executive.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At the same time, this budget hearing comes at a period of significant uncertainty for public health agencies across the country.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Federal funding that supported critical disease surveillance, laboratory capacity, and emergency preparedness efforts is beginning to expire, while public health departments are simultaneously being asked to respond to emerging infectious diseases,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: rising chronic disease burdens, and increasing health care access challenges.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That is why I'm encouraged to see investments in disease outbreak resilience, tuberculosis prevention, and childhood asthma initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And healthcare access programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: However, it is also important that these investments are supported by a workforce that can successfully implement them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: With the agency still operating below budgeted headcount and at the same time eliminating long-term vacant positions, we must ensure that staffing capacity keeps pace with the growing responsibilities being assigned to the department.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from the commissioner today about how the department plans to sustain critical public health services, address federal funding uncertainty, advance healthy NYC goals, and continues protecting the health of all New Yorkers in the years ahead.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Chair Schulman, for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Lynn Schulman delivers her opening statement, highlighting the $2.64B DOHMH executive budget and key priorities including Healthy NYC, Medicaid disruptions from essential plan changes, Legionnaires' disease response, TB prevention, mpox, and animal welfare. She thanks finance and committee staff.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman opening remarks on public health priorities

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Lynn Schulman, Chair of the New York City Council's Committee on Health.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us at the Fiscal 2027 Executive Budget Hearing for the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to thank our Finance Chair, Linda-Lee, for co-chairing this hearing, and I also want to thank Speaker...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Julie-Menin for attending as well.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I would also like to thank the Commissioner, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Alistair Martin, his staff, and everyone who is with us today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: DOHMH's fiscal 2027 executive budget totals $2.64 billion, which represents approximately 2.1% of the city's budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Funding for public health services totals $1.6 billion, a net increase of $116.6 million compared to last year's adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Of this amount, $526 million is for personal services and $1.1 billion for other than personal services.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Today's hearing comes at a critical moment for public health in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: While our city has made meaningful progress in improving health outcomes and strengthening disease prevention efforts, we continue to face serious challenges that require sustained investment, thoughtful planning, and strong public trust in our health institutions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This hearing will examine how the department's fiscal 2027 executive budget aligns with the city's broader public health goals, including the Healthy NYC initiative to increase life expectancy, which the council codified into law.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Healthy NYC established ambitious targets to address the longstanding inequities that continue to shape health outcomes across our communities.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These goals cannot exist solely as aspirational benchmarks.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: They must be reflected both in spirit and in funding decisions throughout this budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: At the same time, the city is preparing for the impacts of significant federal health care policy changes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Approximately 233,000 New York City residents are expected to lose health insurance coverage.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: due to upcoming essential plan eligibility changes taking effect on July 1st.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: These Medicaid-related disruptions could increase the number of uninsured New Yorkers and interrupt continuity of care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We will discuss how the department plans to respond through the new Health and Affordability Corps to support vulnerable communities during this transition.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We will also examine investments in programs that directly address food insecurity, environmental health, infectious and chronic disease prevention, sexual health services, public health emergencies, and childhood asthma.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Apart from responding to crises, public health is also about preventing illness before it occurs and ensuring that all New Yorkers have equitable access to the resources they need to live healthy lives.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: This hearing will also focus on the city's response to Legionnaire's disease following recent outbreaks and the implementation of strengthened cooling tower regulations, which I championed last year.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The 2025 Central Harlem Legionnaires Disease Cluster emphasized the devastating consequences of delayed detection and insufficient prevention measures.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: As warmer temperatures increase the risk of Legionella growth, it is essential that the city fully supports inspection capacity, outbreak response, environmental monitoring, and community engagement efforts to protect public health.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We will also discuss investments in tuberculosis prevention through Stop TB NYC, the city's ongoing MPOX response, and the growing importance of combating health misinformation through effective public health communications.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Additionally, we will examine the department's role in animal welfare, zoonotic disease preparedness, and pest mitigation efforts as emerging public health threats continue to evolve.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Before we would begin, I would like to thank the finance staff, Valeria Lazaro-Rodriguez, Florentine Cabor, and Isha Wright for their work on this hearing, and committee staff Chris Pepe, Elizabeth Arts, and Joshua Newman for their support.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Finally, I would also like to thank my staff, Jonathan Boucher, Kevin McAleer, and Sammy New.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: With that, I will now pass it to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee makes announcements about the upcoming public testimony hearing on June 10th at 9:30 AM. Committee counsel Brian Sarfo swears in Commissioner Dr. Alistair Martin and Deputy Commissioner Anderson.
chapters.title: Public testimony announcement and swearing-in of witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So before we get into swearing in, just wanted to make some quick announcements.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we're having our public testimony hearing all day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So instead of doing it on multiple days, we're doing it on one day on Wednesday, June 10th, next week at 930 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you're here from the public and want to sign up to testify, please do so.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It should be a long, fun day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we will make sure to get through all the testimony and hear from everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please again, June 10th, 930 a.m., please sign up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, without further ado, I want to introduce our amazing committee counsel, Brian Sarfo, to swear in our witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Dr.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Alistair Martin?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner Anderson?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin testifies on the DOHMH executive budget, framing public health as an affordability issue. He discusses federal cuts to Medicaid, the department's three priorities—core services, affordability, and making public health visible—and highlights investments in perinatal mental health, asthma, and peer workers.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin opening testimony on DOHMH priorities and federal threats

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Fantastic.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Kavan, and thank you, Madam Speaker, and to all of the council members who've joined us today.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Alistair Martin, Commissioner of Health at the New York City Health Department, and I'm joined today by our Chief Financial Officer, Aaron Anderson, and members of my senior leadership team.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today on our executive budget as it relates to public health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In the midst of this year's budget season, City Hall released a study that found 62% of New Yorkers cannot meet their basic needs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's 5 million people, most of whom are missed by federal poverty measures.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This budget season, the Mamdani administration is putting forward a blueprint for how to care for our city and lift some of the financial weight from New Yorkers'shoulders.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We can't talk about affordability without talking about health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're facing devastating cuts to public health and health care at the federal level, and we're bracing for the impacts of H.R.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Millions of New Yorkers who rely on Medicaid will soon become vulnerable to losing their coverage.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That impacts nearly half of our city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have both a responsibility and an opportunity to intervene.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our mission for the Health Department is centered around three main priorities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The first is to solidify the foundation of our core public and mental health work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As we face an increasingly hostile federal environment, we are dedicated to ensuring our core local services are operating.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The second is to make clear that public health is an affordability issue.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Key components of this work include enrolling New Yorkers in Medicaid and keeping them covered, especially the hundreds of thousands of residents at risk under new federal work requirements.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Connecting families to cash assistance benefit programs like SNAP, WIC, EITC, and other benefits they've already earned but haven't claimed,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: eliminating medical debt both by erasing it once it accrues and preventing it once before it accumulates.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And screening for housing instability at health visits and linking patients to eviction prevention services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The third priority is to make public health's invisible shield visible.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Because that metaphor does us a disservice.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: If something is hidden, it's easy to take for granted.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We cannot afford to be taken for granted.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Especially not right now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are building a healthier, more affordable New York City every single day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In the past three months alone, we've made extraordinary commitments to deliver for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That includes a $20 million investment in perinatal and early childhood mental health services,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: as well as expansion of our nurse family partnership program, a program in which my own mother was a nurse, as a nurse family partnership nurse.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Another $20 million towards community-based asthma programming in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: A $12 million investment in the peer worker.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: at substance use recovery programs across New York City, and a Board of Health resolution condemning federal tax on public health insurance and resolving to fight back.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Each of these is both an accomplishment and also a promise of more to come.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm proud to share them with you today.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin details the Board of Health resolution responding to looming Medicaid cuts and the launch of a $500K campaign to promote free health insurance enrollment services. He notes over 5 million New Yorkers are enrolled in low or no-cost insurance plans.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on health insurance enrollment and Medicaid threats

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Most recently, the Board of Health unanimously approved a resolution responding to looming cuts to Medicaid and changes to public health insurance options more broadly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That resolution also calls on New York City and state government

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: to mitigate the harms of federal actions and educate the public about their options.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That very same day, we launched a $500,000 campaign advertising our free services that help New Yorkers enroll in low and no-cost health insurance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That campaign runs through the end of this month on social media.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In newspapers, on LinkNYC kiosks, it's translated into 13 different languages.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The ads point New Yorkers directly to our health insurance enrollment team.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These services are free of charge and available regardless of someone's immigration status or ability to pay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our enrollment counselors offer guidance on coverage options and help navigating government bureaucracy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They help meet the needs of the whole person by recommending other benefit applications, including SNAP.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They will also enroll people in NYC Care if that's their only health care option.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These are critical services that New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That connect New Yorkers to programs that most of our city relies on.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Right now, more than 5 million New Yorkers are enrolled in low or no-cost insurance plans, including Medicaid and the Essential Plan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Medicaid work requirements will go into effect at the end of this year, January 2027, and additional changes like frequent eligibility redeterminations will make it harder for people to stay covered.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Upcoming federal changes that impact the essential plan are projected to cause an estimated 230,000 New York City residents to lose their coverage as well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the Health Department, we are determined to intervene and keep as many New Yorkers insured as possible.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I've heard some of the conversations unfold around how do we keep people enrolled in these insurance benefit options, and it's those moments that crystallize the impact of our work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are meeting people where they are and helping them navigate some of the most consequential moments of their lives, and we're doing it by the thousands.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin highlights DOHMH's direct services including the Citywide Doula Initiative, Nurse Family Partnership, and newborn home visiting programs. He shares stories of individual New Yorkers served and notes zero pregnancy-related deaths among doula program participants.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on perinatal and early childhood programs

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our direct one-on-one services reach more than 10,000 pregnant or parenting New Yorkers every single year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The Citywide Dual Initiative serves roughly 1,000 people each year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've reached more than 3,900 people since the program launched in 2022, and there have been zero pregnancy-related deaths among participants.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Behind the numbers are New Yorkers like Masada, a doula I joined to visit a young woman who left an abusive partnership during her second pregnancy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: She welcomed

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: her son into this world in January with Masada by her side.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our nurse family partnership program serves more than 2,000 people every year, and that number will go up thanks to a $20 million investment in the Strong Foundations Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I learned of that program's impact by watching my mom, who served as an NFP nurse for nearly 15 years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's her example of public service I seek to emulate at this agency.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our newborn home visiting program serves more than 7,000 families every year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We dispatch community health workers to support families through the early days of parenthood.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: People like Wanda who recounted what it felt like to hold a client's hand the day they came home from the NICU.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In the richest city, in the richest country in the world, no family should lose a loved one to pregnancy-related causes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All of these programs are built on trust.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All of them are free of charge.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And all of them are making pregnancy and parenthood safer and more supported in New York City.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin discusses the $20M childhood asthma investment in the Bronx, funded by MTA congestion pricing revenue. He also covers the Health and Affordability Corps, mobile food vending, Groceries to Go, and Article 6 state matching fund restoration.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on Bronx asthma investment and state funding

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the Health Department, we are committed to driving our resources according to need, no matter the subject area.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The Spring City Hall announced a $20 million investment to improve childhood outcomes, childhood asthma outcomes in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: About half of that money will go towards community-based programming, and the other half will support asthma case management resources in schools.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That investment would not be possible without the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's part of a larger $100 million commitment to New York City government.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The money was generated by congestion pricing revenue and will go to the

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: towards mitigation projects in environmental justice communities, including our asthma work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Every day we are building on a legacy of more than 220 years in public health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are positioned to meet and anticipate the needs of community members, and we will continue to do so.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm encouraged to see increased support for our community health programming in the city's executive budget, and that includes a $3 million investment to establish the Health and Affordability Corps.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That funding will support 46 employees dedicated to working with New Yorkers across our city to facilitate access to benefits, health insurance enrollment, and referral to low- and no-cost services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're grateful for a $12 million investment in our mobile food vending program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I'm also reassured by the dedication of $10 million to continue Groceries to Go, which provides food and secure New Yorkers enrolled in health and hospitals NYC care program with monthly credits to purchase groceries.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the state level, the fiscal year 2027 budget provides crucial support for public health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm very grateful to Governor Kathy Hochul and the New York State Legislature for restoring Article 6 matching funds of 36% to New York City.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This reverses several years of cuts where New York City was the only jurisdiction in the state receiving a lower state reimbursement for core public health services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you to Speaker Menon, to Chair Shulman, and the entire council for their continued advocacy on this issue.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin discusses school-based health centers serving 143,000 students and the state budget's permanent Medicaid carve-out. He addresses federal funding comprising ~20% of DOHMH's budget, mostly for emergency preparedness and infectious disease control, and expresses cautious optimism about the year ahead.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on school-based health centers and federal budget

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm also pleased to see

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: a permanent carve-out of school-based health centers for Medicaid-managed care in this year's budget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our school-based health centers provide free primary care, including mental health and dental services, to approximately 143,000 New York City students.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're grateful to see these services supported in the state budget, and I appreciate this council's advocacy on that issue.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Lastly, let me speak to the federal budget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: About 20% of our budget is federally funded.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That amounts to approximately $500 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As mentioned, the majority of that funding goes towards emergency preparedness and infectious disease control.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: While we expect the federal government to honor its commitment and maintain that funding, the reality is we cannot rely on Washington, D.C.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The writing is on the wall for the year ahead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This will be a uniquely challenging one for public health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But in New York City, I'm approaching that year with a whole lot of hope.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I believe in the leadership we have across city government, including this council.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I believe in the 7,000 people who carry out our work at the health department every day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I believe in our city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: If there's anyone that can turn tribulation into transformation, it is here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're happy to take questions.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee asks about the chief savings officer review process. Deputy Commissioner Aaron Anderson details savings items including Legionella re-estimate, Zadroga re-estimate, early intervention re-evaluation, and space savings, all achieved without service impacts.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DOHMH on chief savings officer and contract savings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I appreciate the citations in your opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Very awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to ask a few questions, and when the speaker comes back, I'll turn it over to her.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to her.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So really quickly for the executive plan, what was the process for your chief savings officer to conduct the required review of spending and operations within the 45-day timeframe, and what are the top line

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: items that were identified and submitted to OMB?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, first of all, let me just start by saying

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are in a very good position with this executive budget, thanks to the work of OMB, this administration, but also thanks to your support in this council.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You put us in a good position to execute with regard to all of the services that we need to keep people safe in the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: With regard to the chief savings officer role, we were managing two very specific things and trying to make sure that we were doing both.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Both complying with and aligning with the mayor's vision on EO12.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And at the same time finding places with savings that did not affect core services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we were able to do that throughout the entire budget, and I'll let Aaron talk a little bit more about the specifics there.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Commissioner, and thanks for the question, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: These savings exercises are never easy.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: They're never fun.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: But I think we were absolutely able to meet both the mission and the charge of the administration to find savings while also minimizing or actually not having any impact at all on New Yorkers'lives.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: In sum, the savings items that touch the public health side of the budget are Legionella.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So this is just a re-estimate for the current year only for spending based on less spending than expected.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So that's a simple one.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Zadroga re-estimate.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: This is the World Trade Center health program.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: This is really a program run by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: The city pays 10%.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So we were able to identify savings in the current year.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: To the extent that the feds spend less, the city's obligated to spend less.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So that was sort of an easy one to recognize as well.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Early intervention re-evaluation, we took a identified savings of $500,000 in FY27 in the out years.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: That one's really about eliminating unnecessary evaluation procedures and aligning our practices with a recent state regulation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: And we found some space savings.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: We have a large real estate footprint across the city, so we're always thinking about ways to consolidate and think about best ways to use space efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So I think we've made some progress there.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So I think all of those taken together on the health side are really an example of finding savings without having any impact on services and certainly nothing that would be reflected.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, and we definitely were very clear about that on the council side, too, that we don't want services to get cut or anything to be sacrificed on that end.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm glad to hear that you were able to do that without.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee asks about consulting and IT contract reevaluations. Anderson explains they looked exhaustively but found little left to cut after prior years of pegs and budget cuts. Lee then asks about the elimination of 76 long-term vacant positions across DOHMH.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions consulting contracts and vacancy reductions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then any consulting contracts, outside contracts that were reevaluated?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: or IT, because I know that a bunch of different agencies have been looking at those costs as well, more on the OTPS side.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, as part of the 45-day process, we certainly looked exhaustively.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Consultants are a natural place to look.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: IT is a natural place to look.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: You know, over the past two or three years of the prior administration, we had rounds and rounds of pegs and budget cuts, and I think...

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: We did a lot of work on that front there, so there wasn't much left to do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: But we're always looking at those areas for potential efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of vacancy reductions, FY27 executive plan includes baseline savings of $6.1 million in city and state funding between FY26 through the elimination of

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 76 long-term vacant positions across DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just again, to clarify, these are positions that were never filled.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so which program areas are impacted by the elimination of the 76 vacant positions?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Were they across multiple programs?

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for the question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So, right, I think that the top line here is certainly that, yeah, this did not target current staff.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: This was all not only vacancies, but long-term vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So we've had challenges filling for years.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: It's really spread across the agency, the same approach we took to

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: The savings initiatives we took to the vacancy, again, not easy, but looked for the things that were the hardest to fill, the longest vacant, and of course the overriding principle was no impact to the public.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: or to services.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So yeah, to answer that, it's really spread across the agency.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: There's not a single area where they're concentrated.

chapters.summary: Anderson confirms the 76 eliminated positions were long-term vacancies spread across the agency, vacant for upwards of two years. Lee asks how responsibilities will be absorbed and whether any eliminated positions were connected to emergency preparedness or federally funded programs; Anderson says generally no.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on impact of 76 eliminated vacant positions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: We were able to meet the target.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What was the average length that the positions had remained vacant prior to being removed?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also what other factors contributed to the department's inability to fill the positions originally?

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, there's no single answer.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, as you know, our agency does so many different things.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So the hiring issues that are in environmental health are very different than the hiring issues in the school health.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: And so it's just not really a one-size-fits-all answer.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: But in general, I mean, we're looking at positions that were vacant for upwards of two years.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Two years or more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And although the administration states that there will be no programmatic impact, how will responsibilities previously associated with the positions be absorbed or redistributed?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how would that look like considering that the positions were never filled?

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, it's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: It's a natural question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I think we're doing our best to absorb as we have.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, we have, as a city, every agency's had a lot of experience with us over the past few years.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: This vacancy reduction was not nearly as painful, at least, as the one several years ago.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So I think we have some experience in how to absorb those.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: And it's always easier to absorb and redirect that work when it's not sort of public-facing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And also not currently, there's a current staff member there.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know the answer to the next question, but...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just for the record.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm assuming that among the eliminated positions, were any of them connected to public health emergency preparedness, data modernization, health equity initiatives, or federally funded programs?

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Generally, no.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, I think we certainly made every effort to focus on things that were more back office functions, if we have to group them together, things like data analysts or program specialists or office support.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin discusses recruitment strategies including social media, community outreach, and partnerships with Workforce One. Lee asks about partnering with CUNY and workforce development programs, and Martin confirms discussions with Commissioner Minaya's Workforce One Healthcare Career Center.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on recruitment strategies and workforce partnerships

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in terms of recruitment and retention, what are some of the strategies that you're implementing to improve some of the more harder to fill positions around public health programming?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll tell you just right off the bat here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is one of the things top priority for me and is something that is top of mind We're applying every single tool we can to make sure we get the right people in this building We are hiring

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: so if council has folks that are individuals that we should be looking at we'd love to

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: see resumes and get people in the door.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're pushing on all fronts, on social media, on comms, and also doing the work that we can do in communities to recruit people as well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So this is a...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Top priority of making sure we get the right people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we also keep them here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then have you been partnering with other provider organizations as well as maybe, you know, the city's own programs, either through CUNY or workforce development type programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have you been plugging into those?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, actually, we recently just met with Commissioner Kenny Minaya and began doing some of our strategic planning around how we might collaborate with their really incredible Workforce One centers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They have a Workforce One Healthcare Career Center, and I think there's a ton that we can do there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, we are not going to recreate the wheel in terms of resume writing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Job searching, but I do think that we have a role to play in helping to attract candidates and then working with Workforce One to get them ready for hiring.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then I'm assuming the positions you have open could be for multiple different types of degrees, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, I mean in terms of levels of degrees.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They span from roles that don't require a college degree to some of the roles that we'll talk about today, which are highly specialized medical economist roles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So the bottom line is we are hiring and we're looking for candidates.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about conversations with OMB for items not in the executive plan. Martin says they have what they need for core services but will continue working with OMB and Council. Anderson confirms daily conversations with OMB about evolving needs.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on OMB conversations and additional funding needs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just really quickly going into some of your conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So how have your conversations actually been going in terms of things that maybe didn't get put in the executive plan that perhaps can be added before the adoption of the budget of the budget?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are there any resources that you're looking at in addition to what was added into the executive plan?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I can say a few words and then pass it over to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, at this time that we're in now, we have what we need to be able to carry out both the core public health and mental health services that New Yorkers count on us to deliver.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But we will continue working with OMB, and we're going to continue our conversations with you all at City Council to figure out how do we push even more and continue to deliver the services the New Yorkers rely on.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll pass it to Aaron and see if he has anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that sums it up well.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, I'm in almost daily conversation with OMB, so they're very well aware of our...

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Needs and are evolving considerations as an agency.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So yeah, we're in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can we add to your list actually?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that there's some things I would love for you guys to take more funding on, especially related to the mental health or health programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wondering, I'm sure Chair Caban would agree with that, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you need, there's no shortage of things that we have on our end of things we would love to see in the budget, but we would love to work with you and OMB on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We'll have our pens ready to write the list down.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOHMH's state budget priorities. Martin highlights Article 6 restoration as a major win and thanks the Council for advocacy. Anderson confirms it's a huge deal for the city and agency.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on state budget priorities and Article 6 restoration

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it seems like most of the requests you submitted were approved.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What funding or policy priorities did DOHMH advocate for during the state budget process, and do you have any?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: confirmation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know the state budget is winding down and finalized, but if you could speak a little bit to that in terms of your state priorities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Well, I'll just say the one that's really important and just to say thank you to this council, to Chair Shulman, to you as well, Chair Lee, for helping to fight for Article 6 restoration.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's a big, big, big deal.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That, you know, is tens of millions, if not hundreds of millions of dollars that we were being

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: blocked from receiving because of the disparity in terms of our match rate.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So thank you for that advocacy and for the commitment to that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So I'm going to hand it to Aaron to see if there's any other pieces that he'd like to lift up.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, not much else to add, just that Article 6 restoration is a huge deal for the city and a huge deal for this agency.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the impact of $706M for hospitals, $480M for nursing homes, and $80M for FQHCs in the state budget. Martin highlights the school-based health center carve-out as a major benefit, and Anderson notes the city supports 34 of 139 sites with $9M annually.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on state healthcare investments and school-based health centers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I think the state budget agreement reportedly includes, I believe, $706 million for hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: $480 million for nursing homes, $20 million for assisted living programs, and $80 million for federally qualified health centers, FQHCs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how will recent state health care investments impact New York City's public health system?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Well, I'll tell you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Another piece that is related to what you've mentioned that we're really excited about is the carve-out that school-based health centers got.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, for many of the students that are involved in getting care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: From school-based health centers, they don't have another primary care doctor or another place to go to get care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so being able to co-locate those services in the school is incredibly important.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Now, the challenge has been that these school-based health centers were facing the prospect of having to do a bunch of administrative work and billing and documentation and things that would have made it more challenging for them to

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: receive funding for the work had they not been carved out and had to enter managed care contracts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So that carve out is a huge, huge benefit to the city in terms of buttressing our public health.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about additional capital needs and new school-based health centers. Martin discusses the program serving 140,000 students across 139 sites, emphasizing its importance for families concerned about immigration status. Anderson notes the carve-out will stabilize resources for city-supported sites.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on school-based health center expansion and siting

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of actually the school-based health centers, are there additional resources, capital needs, things that are needed of that sort that's in the budget as well that is, I guess, a new need?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are there new community health centers that are going to be put online for the school-based health centers?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: For school-based health centers, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So let me say a few words there, and then I'll hand it to Aaron to talk budget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So our school-based health center program helps to serve about 140,000 students across the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's throughout 139 school-based health centers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, again, this is no-cost primary care for students.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And it's something that I think, given the place where we're heading and where we are now with the federal environment that we're in,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: you know, when a family is concerned about going to a hospital or primary care clinic, knowing that their school

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: can offer the services that that child might get somewhere else and also not ask questions about immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And, you know, I think that this is going to serve an even more important role moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we're really excited to lean in to the school-based health center program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Aaron to talk about the budget.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Sure, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So just to underscore the city's commitment to school-based health centers, it's about nine

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: million dollars a year supports 34 sites of the 139 across the city.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: The only thing I would add is just to underscore the importance of the carve-out.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, I think that itself will go a long way towards stabilizing the resources for the many sites that the city does not support.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So I think that goes a long way.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I think there's certainly a larger question about

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: what the right number is, but we're certainly happy to be a part of those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you look at zip codes, areas where there's chronic disease, more prevalence, to figure out where to place them?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, so we are working with partners across the city and across city agencies to make sure that we identify the right places for these school-based health centers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so that data is always...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: A part of the conversation when it comes to deploying these resources.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about DOHMH's collaboration with HRA/DSS on benefit enrollment. Martin describes the interagency task force with Commissioner Dalton and efforts to leverage place-based work to enroll people in SNAP, WIC, and other programs at DOHMH facilities.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on HRA collaboration for SNAP and Medicaid enrollment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just really quickly, going back to your testimony, because I know that you were talking about connecting families to SNAP, WIC, EITC, enrolling New Yorkers in Medicaid, keeping them covered.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yesterday we just had a long hearing with HRA, and so just wondering if you could speak a little bit about what the conversations are or collaboration, partnership with HRA in that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I had a second part to that question once you...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, no, we love our colleagues and our co-agency partners at DSS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I meet regularly with Commissioner Aaron Dalton.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have an interagency task force on this very issue that brings together some of the

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: senior leaders in our agency and in their agency, and we are working this problem together.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're trying to identify what are the ways that we can leverage the place-based nature of our work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, we see hundreds of thousands of people in our clinics, in our vital records shop,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: in our neighborhood action centers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So the question becomes, while people are in those facilities and they're waiting, can't we do more?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Can't we enroll them in some of these programs?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're trying to work on that at the health department to step up and to really lean in there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're grateful to have DSS there to have our back as we develop this new muscle.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about resource navigators and HR1 impacts. Martin details 30 community health workers and 10 specialized insurance enrollers totaling $4.6M, plus plans to leverage volunteers who can satisfy Medicaid work requirements while gaining healthcare career experience.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on resource navigators and HR1 federal funding impacts

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of related to what's happening with HR1 and the potential loss of families with access to benefits,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because one of the big issues I know that we're facing is around the resource navigators and folks that are able to help people to enroll.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wondering if any of your DOHMH contracts incorporate any of those positions that may also need more support.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is a key priority for us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, I think it's not just about pushing people to a website or giving them a handout or a pamphlet or a poster.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Like, we have to sit with people shoulder to shoulder and help them through.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: What in many cases are like really opaque bureaucratic forms.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we take that charge very seriously as part of our new needs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And then in this exact budget, we got dozens and dozens of new staff to do just that, community health workers who can do this work in a place-based way, as well as new,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: they're called certified application counselors, basically people who have the certification to be able to directly enroll you in health insurance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're committed to this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll also highlight the Access Health program, which, as you know, is a really important program that coordinates community-based organizations to do this kind of navigation and certified application counseling.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so the future is bright when it comes to this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have very big challenges because of HR1, but we're not sitting on our hands, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are stepping in and stepping up and trying to make sure that New Yorkers have what they need to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how many new staff were hired for that, and also what's the dollar amount?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yep, it's 30 community health workers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 30, okay, CHV.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 10 specialized insurance enrollers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The total is $4.6 million for that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There's also other funding in that to help support the tech and the IT and stuff like that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But that's...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Those are the people that we're going to be pushing out to our health department.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: facilities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Now we also have the ability to leverage volunteers, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Because for people who are needing to keep their Medicaid, they have to submit work hour requirements.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And one of the ways that you can submit those work hour requirements is through volunteering.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we think that there's an opportunity to basically recruit people, have them volunteer with us, but then also potentially give them the first steps in a healthcare career because this work is a lot like community health worker work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So that core base of 40 staff will help us then build out to a much larger contingency of people.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, I totally, thousand percent agree.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is now the time where we need to really beef up those staffers for that during this time.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $20M investment in perinatal and early childhood mental health. Martin explains the NFPX expansion broadening eligibility and surging resources for perinatal mental health. Deputy Commissioner Lydia Leniak details expanded eligibility criteria and increased clinic capacity.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on $20M perinatal mental health investment

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then my last question related to your testimony is you had mentioned that there's a $20 million investment in perinatal and early childhood mental health services, as well as the expansion of our nurse family partnership program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wanted to know if you go a little bit more into detail of what that $20 million is actually going towards.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to give some high-level points on this, and then I want to have Deputy Commissioner Lydia Leniak join us to get into the details a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So, first of all, this is an expansion of the NFP program, and it's called NFPX.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Essentially, it increases the eligibility.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: window for families who can leverage this service.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We thought that, you know, of course we don't want to mess with the secret sauce of NFP.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: However, we thought it was important to try and increase the aperture of who could be included in the program, so increase eligibility.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And then the second component is increasing resources for perinatal mental health issues.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We know that one of the things that's killing black and brown women in this city who are recently postpartum, it's actually not things like, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: hemorrhage or pulmonary embolism, it's actually mental health issues, substance use issues.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're surging resources in that capacity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So I'm going to hand it to Lydia to share a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before the committee and to respond honestly to the council member question?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So the Nurse Family Partnership Program is a program that pairs pregnant people and new parents with a nurse until the child's second birthday.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So traditionally, the eligibility criteria for that program was first-time pregnant people up to the third trimester.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: With this expansion, we are broadening the eligibility criteria to include any pregnant person, even with a prior birth, up until the birth of the child.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: And we know from research that's been done in this area that parents who have multiple children, right, have additional stressors.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: And so we're really excited about offering this intervention to additional families.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Ultimately, through this expansion, this is an expansion of this eligibility criteria across all of our 15 teams that deliver Nurse Family Partnership Program.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: In addition, as part of the

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: perinatal and early childhood mental health expansion.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: We are going to be increasing capacity at our perinatal mental health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: And also investing in professional development as well as

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: working with academic institutions because we know one of the findings of our maternal mortality review committee is that there's a workforce shortage in this area.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: And so this is another way that we are sort of listening to the recommendations that are being made to us and expanding services in these key areas.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's actually great to hear, because as you know, a lot of immigrant communities, black and brown communities, either are not believed when they say certain things that they're experiencing, or

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: they don't feel that they want to cause trouble and so therefore don't report to the doctors.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, that's how one of my friend's sisters passed away shortly after she gave birth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm glad to hear that it's not just the first pregnancy, because as we know, each pregnancy is very different.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so it's great that you're expanding it to any pregnancy, so I'm happy to hear about that.

chapters.summary: Lee asks whether early childhood mental health services are through Article 31s or community-based models. Leniak confirms it's through existing clinic contracts. Lee asks about age range; Leniak says perinatal and early childhood and will follow up on exact ages.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on early childhood mental health service model

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just lastly, around early childhood and mental health services, is that through the Article 31s, or is that through community-based models, or anything else you could speak to?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: It's through existing clinic contracts that we already have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Wait, which contracts?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Clinic contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Clinic contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what's the age range?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So it's perinatal and early childhood, that period.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Up through.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Probably 13.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: I think.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: I think it's early childhood, so it's like through a...

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Oh, only childhood, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: We can get back to you on the exact age range on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I have more questions, which I will save till later, and on capital projects, but I'll go into more of that later.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I wanted to pass it off to Chair Shulman.

chapters.summary: Chair Schulman asks about the $17.6M disease outbreak resilience investment replacing expiring ELC and COVID-19 grants. Martin says the investment is sufficient, allowing deployment of disease investigation specialists and shoring up technology platforms, with no expected service disruptions.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman questions DOHMH on disease surveillance and federal funding

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to mention we've been joined by Councilmember Gennaro and Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, and sorry, Councilmember Aldabal, as well as Maloney and Deputy Speaker Williams on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, the fiscal 2027 executive plan reflects a $23.8 million reduction in federal funding for DOHMH compared to the fiscal 2026 adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We know that much of this decreased funding is tied to the expiration of the federal epidemiology and laboratory capacity and COVID-19 grants by July 31st, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In anticipation, the executive plan includes a baseline of $17.6 million, of which $11.3, as you mentioned in your testimony, is city funding.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And 70 positions for disease outbreak resilience to help sustain core surveillance and disease control activities previously funded through these temporary federal grants.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: At a time when public health agencies are responding to emerging infectious disease threats and monitoring outbreaks around the world, such as hantavirus and Ebola, it is important to understand whether this investment is sufficient to maintain the city's disease surveillance laboratory.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: and preparedness infrastructure in the face of declining federal support.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Will the $17.6 million investment fully replace the funding previously provided through the expiring ELC and COVID-19 grants?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: If not, what funding gap remains?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I think let me just step back and share a little bit about the environment that we're operating in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The funding that you're referring to was sort of this really large one-time investment from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I think it is sort of the same story of how public health has been funded for decades, which is these boom-bust cycles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The reality is that this administration gets it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This mayor gets it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: OMB gets it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They understand that that's not how you can fund public health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You have to be able to have stable funding to provide the services that people need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And this investment is what we need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have the services now to be able to deliver what New Yorkers require of us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, the $17.6 million is going to allow us to, number one, deploy staff in places, for instance, like hungry settings where we see a lot of outbreaks and clusters form.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Places like shelters, places like nursing homes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So having these disease investigation specialists for that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
