utterances.text: It also gives us the ability to shore up the systems that our staff use, the platforms, the technology.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so I think that, you know, look, there's always more work to do, but at the present moment, we have what we need.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What program services or positions previously supported by ELC and COVID-19 grants will be sustained with this funding, or is it basically how you respond?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We don't really expect any service disruptions or cuts to programming moving forward because of the investment that we have.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about other federal funding concerns. Martin describes the general climate of concern, recalling when ~$100M was cut and later restored through court injunction, and emphasizes the importance of city government stabilizing funding.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on other federal funding stream concerns

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are any public health services, surveillance activities, laboratory functions, or preparedness efforts expected to be reduced as a result of expiring federal funding?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Beyond ELC and COVID-19 grants, are there other federal funding streams that DOHMH is concerned may be reduced or eliminated in future years?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I know, it's a loaded question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, you know, how much time do we have, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Like, I think, you know, this is one of the things that keeps me up at night, you know, the idea that the federal government can just sort of decide on a Tuesday that they want to, you know, remove our funding, and this is what happened.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It happened.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: About a year ago, about $100 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We were able to fight back.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We went to court, got an injunction.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thanks to the advocacy of this council, thanks to the advocacy of Letitia James, we got that money back.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But, you know, there's nothing specific that we're concerned about right now other than the just general climate of concern.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And that's why it's important for, you know, city government to stabilize this funding.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And that's what we're seeing here in this exact budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: No, and I'm very glad to see it.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about the $8.9M Bronx Asthma Program and new Bronx Asthma Center. Martin explains the funding targets community-based interventions and school case management, not a new physical space, building on the existing Tremont Neighborhood Health Action Center.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on Bronx asthma investment and center coordination

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to switch gears because I'm going to ask you about childhood asthma in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Mayor Mamdani announced a $20 million investment from the MTA Congestion Pricing Mitigation Program to improve childhood asthma outcomes in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The investment will support two major initiatives, including $8.9 million for the Bronx Asthma Program, including the establishment of a new Bronx Asthma Center.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And $11.1 million to expand the asthma case management program in Bronx schools.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The school-based expansion will provide intensive asthma support services, including in school medication administration, self-management, education for students and families, and the addition of up to 15 schools to the program.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: While asthma-related emergency department visits among children ages 5 to 8

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: 5 to 18 declined by 38% citywide and 25% in the Bronx between 2009 and 2024.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Rates in parts of the Bronx remain disproportionately high due to longstanding inequities, environmental burdens, and gaps in access to care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How will the $8.9 million allocated for the Bronx Asthma Program and Bronx Asthma Center be?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: utilized.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you, Chair, for this question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, look, as an ER doctor, what I've seen in my own emergency department is that too often asthma presentations are not dictated by biology or genetics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They're dictated by things like structural racism.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They're dictated by, you know, access or no access to health care, dictated by things like being surrounded by mold or pests.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we're extremely grateful for, you know, the opportunity to say that this new $20 million investment will be actualized in a targeted way in some of the very communities that are most impacted by asthma.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So there are two things that we're doing here with this funding.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Number one, we are increasing our footprint when it comes to our targeted asthma case management program in schools.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The reason why we're doing that is because it's incredibly important to meet people where they're at and meet students where they're at.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They're spending hours and hours and hours a day at school, so let's go there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The second is it allows us to surge resources to our, what we call community-based asthma interventions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: What this allows us to do is to go literally to the source of the problem in people's homes, do the home remediation work that needs to happen, the pest mitigation, address the problem.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The mold, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so this is not funding a new physical space because we already have a physical space in the Bronx at our Tremont Neighborhood Health Action Center.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is going a step further in sort of saying that we need to further target our resources and meet people where they are.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so this is an expansion of the Tremont Center, is that what you said?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So it's, so we have our space.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So it's not a new structure, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we have our space in the Tremont Neighborhood Health Action Center.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And now we have more resources to deploy for what we call community-based interventions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As well as...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In the schools.

chapters.summary: Dr. McNatt details coordination through the NICAN asthma network with FQHCs, CBOs, and faith-based organizations. The funding supports technical assistance to primary care practices in the South Bronx, creating an ecosystem across schools, primary care sites, homes, and the action center.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on Bronx Asthma Center coordination with providers

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How will the Bronx Asthma Center coordinate with hospitals, primary care providers, schools, and community-based organizations in the Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm gonna say a few words about this and then gonna turn it over to Lydia to share a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So you asked about asthma centers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Sorry, did you ask about the schools?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I said, how will the Bronx Asthma Center coordinate with hospitals, primary care providers, schools, and CPOs?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to call up Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: McNatt to the table.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, so we are coordinating in this work through a network called NICAN, which is basically the New York City Asthma Network.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is a network of community-based organizations, provider groups, et cetera, who can help us to support individuals with asthma in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: McNatt to share a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear to tell the truth, the whole truth, for the council members today?

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Good morning and thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: We're excited to be able to talk about and center the Bronx Asthma Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And you're correct, there is a lot of collaboration that is and will be taking place across the South Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: The work that we do with federally qualified health centers, community-based organizations, faith-based organizations, as well as the Asthma Network,

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: help us to be able to implement the kinds of programs that the commissioner described, but also create an ecosystem that makes it easier for community members and families and their children to navigate asthma care within this environment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And so in addition to some of the examples you heard from the commissioner, this funding will also be supporting our support and technical assistance to primary care practices in the South Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: So we'll be working with dozens of primary care practices around how they improve the quality of care for children with asthma.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And so this is really an opportunity for a defined ecosystem.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Work is happening in schools.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: happening in your primary care sites, it's happening in your home, and it's also happening in the action center that the commissioner described.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thanks so much for the question.

chapters.summary: Dr. McNatt explains the evaluation approach tracking ED visits and hospitalizations, with population-level impact analysis comparing served neighborhoods to other Bronx areas, and assessment of leveraging a local health information exchange for pre-post analysis.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on asthma program effectiveness metrics

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What metrics will DOH, I have a number of questions about the asthma center, so what metrics will DOHMH use to evaluate the center's effectiveness in reducing asthma-related emergency room visits and hospitalizations?

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thank you so much for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: So the Bronx Asthma Initiative will utilize a comprehensive process and outcome evaluation approach for this work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: The primary outcome metrics that we focus on are reducing asthma-related emergency department visits and reducing asthma-related hospitalizations for children.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: To measure population-level impact, we're also analyzing trends for children residing in the Bronx and in the neighborhoods that we're serving through this work in comparison to children residing in other parts of the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: So this analysis helps us understand over time how the intervention is impacting the kids who are going through the services of the Bronx Asthma Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Additionally, we're also assessing feasibility of leveraging a local health information exchange, which helps us with pre- and post-analysis of the same ED utilization as well as hospital admissions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thanks so much.

chapters.summary: Deputy Commissioner Leniak explains the expansion to 15 additional Bronx schools selected based on K-8 grade level, on-site health staff, poorly controlled asthma populations, and location in MTA-identified environmental justice communities.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on school selection for asthma case management expansion

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, you answered this a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How will the $11.1 million expansion of the asthma case management program be implemented in Bronx schools?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I'm going to ask,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: criteria will be used to select the additional 15 schools participating in the program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Lydia Ledniak for more on that.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So the asthma case management program will be expanding to an additional 15 schools in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So really the overall goal, just like with the current asthma case management program, is to control asthma, which is to prevent asthma exacerbations,

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: to reduce hospitalizations, and to ultimately reduce the number of days missed at school.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So a structured approach was utilized to select the schools, and I have that here, which is one, that

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: we are looking for a general education K through eighth grade school, that there is a office of school health doctor and nurse on site,

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: that there is a large population in that school with poorly controlled asthma,

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: where the population is at risk of poor asthma outcomes, as well as the school is located in the environmental justice communities.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Identified by the MTA in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, that's great.

chapters.summary: Leniak states the current program serves 2,200 children in 14 schools; the expansion aims to reach 750 additional children, with about 6,000 people reached through training and community education. Services include medication administration, distribution, and self-management education.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on students served and asthma case management services

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many students and families are expected to be served annually through the expansion?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Currently, with the current asthma case management program, which operates in 14 schools, we serve over 2,200 children every year.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: With this expansion, we are aiming to reach an additional 750 children.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: However, because of the intensive asthma case management model and the training that is done in those school communities, we are expecting to reach about 6,000

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: people through this asthma case management program expansion.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What specific services will students and families receive through the asthma case management program?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So the asthma case management program, you know, is a partnership through the Office of School Health between the Department of Health and New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: We provide intensive case management services to students, which includes medication administration, medication distribution,

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: education around self-management, and ultimately education to the entire school community about asthma and prevention.

chapters.summary: Leniak explains the current medication administration form process is entirely manual. The initiative will build the form into the citywide immunization registry, allowing seamless digital delivery to the Office of School Health and improving medication access timelines.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on digital medication administration system for asthma

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, how will case managers coordinate with school nurses, pediatricians, hospitals, and community providers to improve medication adherence and asthma control?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So we are planning, as you know, the key to getting school-based medication administration is

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Is through the medication administration form, the MAF form.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Right now, that process is entirely manual.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: People fill out pieces of paper.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's manual?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Oh, yes.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: They have to, and then they have to, the family has to bring it to the nurse in the school.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: So as part of this initiative, we are transforming that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Digital.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: We are going to build in the asthma case management form into the

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: citywide immunization registry, which will allow doctors and families to fill out that form, and it will be seamlessly delivered to our Office of School Health, thereby significantly improving the timeline and ensuring that students are getting

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: the medications that they need.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Leniak confirms schools will receive additional asthma case managers and social workers. On data privacy, she explains the portal will be built into the existing secure, HIPAA-compliant citywide immunization registry system.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on nursing support and data privacy safeguards

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Will schools receive additional nursing, social work, or administrative support?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It may not be a question you can answer.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: As part of the expansion, or yes?

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Yes, they will.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: In addition to the additional asthma case managers, we're also going to be taking on social workers who are going to be working with families.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: To provide additional education and support.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: With the new digital system, what safeguards will be implemented to protect student health information?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Because you know that's a big issue now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And ensure compliance with privacy requirements.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: Yes, excellent and really important issue that we've been thinking a lot about in how we're operationalizing this.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: And that is why we have selected to utilize already existing New York City infrastructure that is, you know, over a decade old.

utterances.speaker: Lidiya Lednyak
utterances.text: The citywide immunization registry to build out.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: this portal and that port and because that system is fully secure and compliant with all confidentiality and HIPAA requirements.

chapters.summary: Martin confirms DOHMH will publish reports on program outcomes including asthma rates, hospitalizations, and school days missed. Dr. McNatt describes air quality monitoring showing congestion pricing tolling is not changing vehicle exhaust in environmental justice neighborhoods, with ongoing evaluation.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on outcome reporting and congestion pricing health impacts

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Will DOHMH publish regular reports or dashboards on program outcomes and asthma trends in participating communities and schools?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We will be publishing reports that, you know, go along with what my colleague, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: McNatt, just talked about, which we're going to be looking at asthma, sort of like the asthma rates in schools, a number of hospitalizations, reductions in days missed in school, etc.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How will the department measure whether congestion pricing mitigation investments are contributing to improved respiratory health outcomes in the Bronx, which is actually a good study to have, as a matter of fact, around congestion pricing?

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: McNabb.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thank you so much for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: The departmental measure respiratory outcomes with air quality monitoring, which has already started.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Early findings from this work suggest that congestion pricing tolling is not changing the level of vehicle

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: exhaust in environmental justice designated neighborhoods, but the monitoring will continue and will evaluate the impact of the programming on, as my colleague shared, asthma-related ED visits, hospitalizations, asthma control, school days missed, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thanks so much.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about DOT sensor collaboration. McNatt confirms close work with DOT and MTA on utilizing their data. Anderson confirms 9 positions in FY26 and 26 positions beginning FY27 for the asthma initiative.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on DOT collaboration and asthma program staffing

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, the Department of Transportation announced yesterday or today that they're going to be using sensors and to determine

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: of traffic and all of that kind of stuff, because truck traffic really causes a lot of the asthma issues.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Is there a way for you guys to work with them and see how that configures with this?

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Yes, thank you so much for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: We work very closely with DOT and MTA, and so looking forward to be able to utilize the data that they identify.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Can the administration confirm that this initiative funds nine positions in FY 2026 and 26 positions beginning in fiscal year 2027 and in the out years?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Aaron to share a little bit more about that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yep, confirmed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Martin lists key positions including program directors, asthma capacity building manager, evaluation manager, community engagement coordinators, and a data analyst. The goal is to bring staff on before the 2027 school year begins.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on asthma program hiring timeline and positions

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What titles and functions will these positions serve within the Bronx Asthma Center and the school-based asthma case management program?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And also give me the hiring timeline.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There's a number of key positions that we're looking to hire for this, including program directors, asthma capacity building manager, and evaluation manager.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Community engagement coordinators, and a data analyst.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're looking to bring folks in in the next few weeks, and the goal is to bring as many of them as we can before the 2027 school year begins.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I presume that with all of this, you're working with the Bronx Borough President and the Bronx electeds on this?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: By the way, I wanted to acknowledge that we've been joined by a majority leader.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Shaun-Abreu.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, I'm going to thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And this looks really promising.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I, in a previous life, worked at Woodhull Hospital, which was considered Asthma Alley because of all the truck traffic and everything else and the commercial traffic around there and the NYCHA housing.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about the $4M for rapid STI testing at Chelsea, Fort Greene, Morrisania, and Corona clinics. Martin describes the sexual health clinics as safety net of safety nets, with a new Quickie Express clinic opening in Morrisania and renovations at Morrisania and Corona.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on sexual health clinic funding and rapid STI testing

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I'm going to talk about sexual health now.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: DOHMH's sexual health clinics provide low to no cost sexually transmitted infection testing, treatment, prevention, and HIV-related services.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The fiscal 2027 executive plan includes $4 million in additional city and state funding beginning in fiscal 2027 to support rapid STI testing at Chelsea, Fort Greene, Marisania, and Corona sexual health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How will the additional $4 million for rapid STI testing be allocated across the four clinic sites, and what services will the funding further support?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for this question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is an integral part of our public health fabric here in the city, the ability to offer these low- to no-cost services across our network of public health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our sexual health clinics in particular provide this sort of safety net.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Safety net of the safety net functions for this city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Folks don't have to worry about immigration status, insurance status.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There's no...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Mine towards payment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are able to, at this point, say that we're operating, we're going to be opening up a third Quickie Express clinic in Morrisania.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's not by accident.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We look at the data, and the data tells us that there are rising STD rates in this area, so we're going to meet the demand and the need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And get the resources that we need in that specific area.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The funding is supporting renovations at Morissania as well as Corona and gives us the ability to really shore up our lab equipment, our testing kits, and the specialized reagents that we need.

chapters.summary: Schulman raises the Crown Heights Clinic. Martin says getting it back online is a top priority but depends on funding and staff, and notes the Fort Greene Clinic is open nearby as an interim option.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on Crown Heights sexual health clinic status

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm going to bring up, I brought it up at the, it was brought up at the preliminary budget hearing, but the Crown Heights Clinic, is there still no plans to do anything there?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We're getting a lot of incoming calls from people that live in that district and others, so it's...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, this is a top priority for us to get that clinic back online at the moment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's really a question of funding and staff, but this is certainly something that we are, we hear, we hear you, Chair, we hear Council, and we hear the community as well, and we're trying to meet that, bless you, that need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Right down the street, the Fort Greene Clinic is open, and so we hope to be able to say that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Both those clinics will be operational soon.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just keeping it in the loop, keeping it current.

chapters.summary: Dr. Bronson shares data showing most reported STIs decreased or remained stable in 2024, but HIV diagnoses increased for the fourth consecutive year with persistent disparities—86% of new HIV diagnoses were Black or Latino, and syphilis rates among Black women were nine times higher than white women.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on STI and HIV rate trends and disparities

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What trends is DOHMH currently observing in STI and HIV rates citywide, including demographic or geographic disparities?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm going to call up Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Sarah Bronstein to join me on this, but just to give you a very sort of high-level overview,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: what we see from our last recorded data set is that most reported STIs either decreased or remained stable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is generally good.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think, again, there's much more work that we can do, but this is encouraging.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So I'm going to hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Bronson.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth for the committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Thank you for this question, Councilmember, Chair Schulman.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: As our Commissioner mentioned, we do see concerning trends in HIV.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: As we've mentioned before, in 2024, we saw the fourth consecutive year in which new HIV diagnoses increased in the city or remained stable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And that really is in contrast to market declines that we observed prior to 2020.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: We'd also mentioned at the preliminary hearing that in 2024,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: We saw an estimated 17% increase in estimated new HIV infections compared with 2023 and really persistent disparities across all the factors we measure, including race and ethnicity and gender.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So, for example, 86% of people newly diagnosed with HIV in 2024 were black or Latino, and 42% of people with new HIV diagnoses lived in high or very high poverty areas of the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: In somewhat in contrast, in some ways, STIs, we see something a bit different.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: As the Commissioner mentioned, in 2024, we did actually see rates of most reported STIs decreased or remain relatively stable compared to 2023.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And that is a promising trend that differs from previous years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: But like for HIV, we do see persistent disparities by race and ethnicity and other factors for STIs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: For example, the primary and secondary syphilis rate among black women in 2024 was nine times higher than the rate among white women.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: This is, of course, concerning in and of itself, and because congenital syphilis is something that we are very actively monitoring and working to prevent.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And chlamydia and gonorrhea rates among people living in very high poverty neighborhoods in the city were three times higher than rates among people living in lower poverty neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, don't go anywhere.

chapters.summary: Dr. Bronson reports 282 people ages 50+ newly diagnosed with HIV in 2024, an 18% increase. He details the SHARP program for clinical provider training, Be Into Health program, Ryan White outpatient services, and housing assistance for older adults with HIV.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on older adult sexual health and outreach

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: During a recent council roundtable run by myself and Chair Lee,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We heard from multiple community-based organizations that older adult sexual health is often overlooked.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Has DOHMH observed increases in STI rates among older adults, and what outreach or education efforts are underway for this population?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Pass it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Bronson.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Thank you for this question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: I was actually quite glad to see this because this is an area that we've been interested in and actively pursuing for a while.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So just in terms of your question, Chair Schulman, about the HIV rates, in 2024, 282 people ages 50 and older were newly diagnosed with HIV in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And that was an increase of 18% from the prior year, 2023.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: However, that was a bit of an aberrant trend in that from 2019 to 2024, we did observe an 8% decrease in the number of HIV diagnoses among this group.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: But nonetheless, we also know that older people constitute a majority of people living with HIV in the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So not just being newly affected by HIV, but in terms of current burden.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: In terms of other STIs, I'll just mention that from 2019 to 2024, case rates of chlamydia, gonorrhea, primary and secondary syphilis, and early latent syphilis among people ages 60 and older remain stable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: The health department does a lot of work to both prevent HIV and STIs in this population and to educate the population about available services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: services, risk, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So for example, we are preparing to release a request for proposals for a new sexual health and aging resources and training for clinical providers, what we're calling SHARP program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And this is to fund an organization that will develop and offer clinical trainings and provider and patient facing resources to enhance primary care providers'capacity to initiate sexual health conversations, take sexual health histories,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: and implement routine opt-out HIV and STI screening among patients 50 and older.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And we anticipate funding that organization who is successfully awarded in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: We also have our Be Into Health program that supports HIV clinics around the city to do evidence-based interventions to promote retention in HIV care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And we expect to fund four clinics in this program to focus specifically on black and Latino people ages 50 and older and address their needs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Our Ryan White program also has an outpatient ambulatory services program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: And within that, there's a focus on the needs of people living with HIV who are older.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Our housing services program, our housing opportunities for people with HIV, also funds three organizations to provide housing assistance and supportive services to income eligible people who are ages 55 and older.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So as you can see, we have a lot of programming in this area and continue to do that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: invest so that our older New Yorkers can be sexually healthy.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What?

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about marketing services for older adults. Martin confirms meetings with DFTA Commissioner McKenzie. Bronson describes outreach through community partners, planning bodies, and advisory groups to ensure providers are aware of programming.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on DFTA collaboration and marketing sexual health services

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I wanted to ask, so my understanding from the commissioner for the Department for the Aging that they want to work with you on this, so I presume that you're going to be doing that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've met several times with Commissioner Lisa Scott McKenzie.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, good.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How are you promoting this?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How are you marketing this, marketing the services that you have?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: Well, we work very, very closely with our wide array of community partners.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: This programming is really going out into the community to support those service providers, clinical, non-clinical providers who know the needs of these communities and work closely with them to provide services directly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Bronson
utterances.text: So we do a lot of work through our planning bodies, our advisory groups, to ensure that all of our community partners are aware of current and upcoming programming so that they can take advantage.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have a lot more questions, but I want to give my colleagues an opportunity to ask questions, so I'll come back on second round.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, we'll start with the Majority Leader, Bray, you followed by Councilmember Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Abreu asks about SNAP-Ed funding expiring in September 2026. Anderson explains it's $1.7M funding 17 staff, with the deadline extended through state conversations. Dr. McNatt notes the hardest-hit neighborhoods include South Bronx, East Harlem, and Central Brooklyn.
chapters.title: CM Abreu questions DOHMH on SNAP-Ed funding expiration

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And thank you, Commissioner, for testifying today.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: The Supplemental Nutritional Assistance Program Education, SNAP-Ed, that program provides nutrition education, healthy eating support, and food budgeting resources to low-income families.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: income individuals and families across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: SNAP-Ed programming has historically supported neighborhoods with experiencing high rates of food insecurity and diet-related illness.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: With minimal remaining SNAP-Ed funding expected to expire in September 2026, it is important to understand how DOHMH plans to sustain these services and prevent disruptions for vulnerable New Yorkers who rely on nutrition education and food access.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: My question is, how is the HMH preparing to prevent disruptions to nutrition education and healthy eating programs serving low-income New Yorkers?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, it's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, we know that food is medicine for some of our most vulnerable patients.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Really the challenge that many of them are having is that they are having to choose between

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: paying for medications or putting more food in their fridge.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so SNAP is one of the key interventions that can help address that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Now, with regard to SNAP-Ed, we're working closely with OMB, with our colleagues at the state, to identify what the funding needs would be.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it over to Aaron to share a little bit more about this.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for the question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, SNAP-Ed is a, you know, we receive a relatively small amount of money.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: It's a little bit less than $2 million.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I think it's $1.7 million.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Funds about 17 staff who are currently on board.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: This is one that we all across the nation learned about with the HR1.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Passage last year that was going to be phased out.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So yes, the natural expiration of this funding is coming up in September.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: And given its relatively small size, do you think this is something that the administration will be able to cover?

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: I mean, this is another good example of, first of all, I think the deadline was already extended through conversations with the state, which was great.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So this is funding that we received through New York State Department of Health.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So we are continuing conversations with the state.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: We're certainly continuing conversations with the city and the admin.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: What population, sorry, you were responding?

chapters.summary: Abreu asks what populations and CBOs would be most impacted. McNatt describes food-related programming at action centers including Get the Good Stuff, Groceries to Go, Health Bucks, and More Veggies programs available regardless of SNAP status.
chapters.title: CM Abreu on SNAP-Ed impacted populations and neighborhoods

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: What populations, neighborhoods, and community-based organizations would be most impacted if SNAP-Ed funding is not renewed or replaced?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to call up Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: McNatt to share a little bit more about the details on the specific neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Sounds great.

utterances.speaker: Shaun Abreu
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thank you so much for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: You know, we are deeply concerned about access to SNAP-Ed and to SNAP benefits generally.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: We know that often the hardest hit neighborhoods include, not solely, but include places like the South Bronx and East Harlem and Central Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: We are investing in other aspects of food-related programming while we wait to understand the landscape.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And so in those three locations, we have action centers that make food-related programming available, both for folks who have SNAP and folks who don't have SNAP.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And so that includes programming like Get the Good Stuff and Groceries to Go and Help Bucks and the More Veggies program and the Food Voucher program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: And so we are heavily focused on food affordability and food security in the city, but understand the concern that you shared.

utterances.speaker: Dr. McNatt
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you all for your service.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Great, and we've been joined by Councilmember Mealy, and then I'll go ahead to Councilmember Narcisse, followed by Councilmember Wong.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Narcisse asks about cancer-related services and the Healthy NYC goal to reduce screenable cancer deaths by 20% by 2030. Martin describes hotspot mapping for targeted resource delivery and a recent advertising campaign on alcohol's impact on cancer risk.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on cancer screening and Healthy NYC goals

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, good morning.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you for being here, Commissioner and the team.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: During the Council Women's Caucus meeting, we had last month, we discussed cancer-related services provided to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: As we know, one of the Healthy New York NYC goals is to reduce deaths.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: due to screenable cancers, including lung, breast, and colon cancer, cervical cancer, and prostate by 20% by 2030.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is any of the funding contracted out to CBOs, and what efforts are you putting in place to reduce cancer-related death, including programming and marketing campaign?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you very much for that question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We, as part of Healthy NYC, as you've stated, have a goal to reduce the deaths of folks who have screenable cancers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: One of the things that we are avidly exploring is trying to identify, first of all,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: not just where the deaths are happening and sort of identifying the, let's call them hotspot areas,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: but now also trying to identify where are the resources that we're deploying to make sure that we're doing more of this targeted resource delivery.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're really excited about this new phase of Healthy NYC, which will be, you know, really a focus in a doubling down on each one of those drivers, but also making sure that the resource that's targeting a driver, like in this case, screenable cancers,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: is really targeted at the areas where we're seeing the most need and the highest number of deaths.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: To your question of the advertising campaigns, yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We actually just ran a very successful advertising campaign.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And campaign over the course of the last month, focusing on the intersection between alcohol and its impact on increasing cancer risk, which many individuals don't know.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so trying to make sure that individuals have the information and the education they need to make the right decisions in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And one of my problems with colon cancer, this is one of the preventable cancers.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We should not be having people dying with colon cancer.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about vital records backlogs. Martin acknowledges the issue and says the backlog is top of mind, with efforts to shorten processing times. Narcisse also asks about the $12M peer workforce investment for substance use recovery.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on vital records backlog and birth certificates

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Constituents come to my office very often to ask about birth and death certificate and other vital records.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What are current processing times and is the department clearing the backlog because we've been having backlog on that?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we are aware of the issue and looking to make an impact on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As you know, the birth certificate process through our vital records shop is something that we are responsible for, and we take pride in our ability to do this vital service for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The backlog is top of mind for us, and we're working to shorten it as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Martin describes the $12M investment in peer specialists—individuals with lived recovery experience who help others start their journeys. He notes it will put 500 more peers across the city and is one of his proudest accomplishments in his first 90 days.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on $12M peer workforce investment for substance use

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I see on the statement here from you a $12 million investment in the peer workforce at substance use recovery program across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We have

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We have an increase in our kids using drugs.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's over the top right now.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I'm sure you have seen it too.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Do you have a breakdown by how you spend, which area are you targeting the most when it comes to drug use?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And if we can collaborate with you to help out, because every time I see a young person under the drug influence, it just hurt my heart.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And they're so young, too.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, yes, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let's work together on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have an incredible team in our mental hygiene division that is eager to sit down and do some problem solving with you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Specifically when it comes to the piece that you mentioned around $12 million, that is, I think, one of the things I'm most proud of in this first 90 days, quite frankly, this investment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It puts money into an intervention that has tons of data for it but is underused throughout the country, and these are peer specialists.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: To put it simply, these are individuals who themselves have struggled with substance use, and they're walking their own journey towards recovery, and they can go and help other individuals start their journeys.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And as an ER doctor, I have to tell you,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is like a dream come true, you know, kind of program because many of the people who I see in my emergency department who are on the verge of maybe thinking about starting their journey to recovery.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Me saying something is not as powerful as someone who's been in their seat.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So this $12 million will help to put 500 more of those people out there in the city, and you're really proud about that.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about DOHMH's climate health budget and initiatives. Martin says climate change is a public health issue with a dedicated team in environmental health, woven throughout the agency. Anderson notes climate impacts are now built into the new needs review process with OMB.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on climate health and preparedness budget

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Chair, one more question on climate, you know, climate, health, and climate change preparedness.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We had a study released by DOHMH

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: on March of this year, outlined the department efforts to address the public health impacts of climate change, including extreme heat, we know that, flooding, air quality, and energy, I mean, energy and security.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: As climate-related health risks continue to grow, understanding the department priorities and preparedness efforts remain critical.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What is DOHMH budget for climate health initiatives and how is it allocated across key priorities?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What are the department primary climate health initiatives and how does it measure success?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Climate change is a public health issue, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: If it's the heat-related events or the air quality challenges, we know that the climate affects people's health in a very powerful way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have a whole team dedicated to this intersection of climate change and public health as part of our environmental health division.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, the work that we do on this is...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: is not sort of easily sort of contained to just that division, though.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I mean, this is really woven in throughout the whole agency.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we can get back to you with regard to the specific budgetary items.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron and see if he has anything to add.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Commissioner, and thanks, Senator Seas.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, the one thing I would add is that there is a process, actually, for new needs, whether it's on the expense side or the capital side, to take into consideration climate impacts.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So that's actually been built in as part of the review process with OMB and with City Hall.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So that is a consideration that comes up regularly and whenever we're thinking about new resources.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong, followed by Wilson.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Wong asks about the 2,200 new supervisory licenses for street vendors starting July 1st and inspection capacity. Deputy Commissioner Corinne Schiff explains the rollout process and that food safety is DOHMH's focus while sanitation handles other vendor concerns.
chapters.title: CM Wong on mobile food vending licenses and inspection capacity

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Martin, for coming today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I have three questions regarding supervisory licenses on street vendors.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Starting July 1st, DOHMH begins issuing 2,200 new supervisory licenses, the first wave of 11,000 new permits over five years.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My constituents, especially those in Elmhurst and Rico Park, already live with congested sidewalks, blocked storefronts, and sanitation problems from vendors.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I support legal pathways for vendors, but the number of new licenses is dramatically outpacing inspection capacity.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And this budget...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Funds 133.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: New inspection positions by FY30.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My questions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: One, how many are coming online on FY27 specifically and how many will be assigned to Queens?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And second, before July 1st, what is the current number of DOHMH inspectors covering Queens community, boards four, five, and six?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, as a Queens kid and as a Regal Park resident, this is an issue that hits close to home.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're trying to balance a lot of different priorities here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Deputy Commissioner Crenshaw to share more on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But the idea is we're trying to focus on food safety compliance, on vendor education, and also building the inspection enforcement capacity so we can do this right and really get this balance right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So I'm going to hand it over to Deputy Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Corinne Schiff, I'm the Deputy Commissioner for Environmental Health, and our mobile food vending program is in my division.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we are preparing to implement the council's significant, as you say, expansion of mobile food vending in the city.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We will be issuing, starting in July, starting each fiscal year beginning this July, 2,200 supervisory license applications.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Those applications would then be turned in, if they are turned into us, would be turned into licenses, supervisory licenses, and that gives the licensee the opportunity to apply for a supervisory license permit.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So just to let you know how this will roll out, they won't immediately become permits for vendors to be operating out on the street.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: These are not designated for specific areas.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There is a permit available to work anywhere in the city, and there is a permit available to work in the boroughs outside of Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We were very pleased, as the commissioner said in his testimony, to receive resources that we will need to be able to monitor the food safety aspects of these newly permitted units.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The issues that you have expressed concerns about and that you're

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: constituents are concerned about are handled not only by us when we really focus on the food safety aspect of mobile food vending

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: sanitation very much focuses on some of these other issues that you are describing okay thank you um thank you I'll have questions for round two thank you great okay council member Wilson followed by come on

chapters.summary: Councilmember Cabán asks about PrEP/PEP access and gender-affirming care. Martin confirms all six sexual health clinics provide low-to-no-cost medications regardless of immigration status. On gender-affirming care, he describes the TGNCNB advisory committee and 20 CBOs funded through the Empowerment Fund.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on LGBTQ healthcare, PrEP/PEP, and gender-affirming care

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Good morning, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I have a couple of questions related to LGBTQ health care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, you know, PrEP and PEP have been amazing developments in preventing the spread of HIV AIDS.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How does the budget ensure affordable PrEP and PEP access, as well as public information on the effectiveness of the drugs and how to use them effectively and safely?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So as I mentioned at the top, you know, our ability to provide medications for sexual health and STDs is a core priority of ours at the Public Health Department.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have six sexual health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All of them are.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, low cost to no cost.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So individuals can always walk in and get the medications that they need from us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That will remain true.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There also is, you know, no thought to someone's immigration status or ability to pay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we look forward to continuing to provide any and all medications that are needed for both.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Prep and PEP.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then pivoting to gender-affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So it's a particular concern to this body.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We passed a package of bills, the Protect Trans Future package last year on this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How is the department addressing health care disparities between TGNC, NBI, and gender-conforming patients, reminding them of their rights and helping to make gender-affirming care affordable and private?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So a couple things.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The first is we have a trans, nonconforming, and nonbinary advisory committee.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I actually was just there two weeks ago where we had an honest conversation that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: this federal government is trying to erase their existence from public record.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so they are counting, federal government's counting on cities like New York to not say anything and to step back from its role in protecting health for this population.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we will not stop protecting trans, nonconforming, nonbinary people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So from our perspective, that advisory committee is what helps us keep ourselves honest and make sure that the services that we're delivering.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: keep them top of mind.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We also select and give funding to 20 CBOs

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: throughout the TGNCB Empowerment Fund, which gives us the ability to not just do the work internally, but also to identify the community-based organizations that are really good at this and to fund them to be able to do what they do.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That commitment is so important now more than ever.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about LGBTQ senior services. Martin says H&H clinics serve all ages and expresses eagerness to partner on targeted interventions for LGBTQ seniors, particularly through SAGE and similar organizations.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on LGBTQ senior services and outreach

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then finally, in my district, organizations like SAGE ensure the health and well-being of LGBTQ seniors.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How does your budget ensure that LGBTQ seniors receive the services they need, including health screenings, vaccinations, and medications?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And how is the department ensuring isolated LGBTQ seniors especially are aware of the services for them?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, it's a really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think there's certainly a lot we can do with you on this, and we're looking forward to partnering with you to make sure we get this right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, we have, as I mentioned, at all of our sexual health clinics, you know, we are in a position to help anybody who walks in those doors, whether it's youth or seniors.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But targeted interventions, I think we can do a lot more.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're looking forward to working with you on that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Look forward to that, too.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right, thank you, Councilmember.

chapters.summary: Chair Cabán (stepping in for Finance Chair) discusses the historical context of trans erasure, referencing Nazi Germany's targeting of LGBT organizations. She asks about the trans rights campaign budget and measurement of effectiveness.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on trans rights campaign and historical context of erasure

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Stepping in for the Finance Chair, before I start my own questioning, I just wanted to welcome and thank the WIN NYC advocates who are up in the balcony today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Appreciate you all being here.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, I want to pick up, I think, where...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The council member just left off in terms of support for our trans, gender nonconforming, gender expansive community, gender affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, Commissioner, you said it really well when you said that there is this concerted effort to erase.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Folks from existence.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I just want to put this in the context of the past because this has happened before and it is also the reason why our community struggled to this day.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And, you know, so for folks who don't know this, before 1933, Germany was a center of LGBT plus community and culture.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And they had a bunch of renowned organizations serving and supporting trans and gender nonconforming people.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And it was under Hitler's Nazi government that they were targeted.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And it's the same argument of like, we have to protect youth and raising healthy families.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And they use that as a mechanism for genocide, right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And that included just decimating the Institute for Sexual Research, which was founded by a Jewish doctor.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And that was where like the first modern gender affirmation surgeries were done.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And they swarmed the Institute.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: They burnt over 20.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: 20,000 books, and it was irreversible, right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Like it set back the progress of the work, not just medically, but socially along ways.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And so here we find ourselves today.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And so I just, I want to root it in how important it is and how it's not different.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's not that different.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So you said that you guys have launched the trans rights or human rights public awareness campaign in partnership with the Office of LGBTQIA plus affairs and DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I just want to know what the total budget allocation is for the campaign and what funding source is being used and how the city is going to measure whether the campaign leads to increased awareness of legal protections, increased reporting of discrimination, mental health.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What are the things that you're looking at?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: In that.

chapters.summary: Martin announces gender-affirming hormone therapy for adults will soon be offered at the Corona clinic. Cabán presses on youth care, noting few providers serve children under 13. Martin acknowledges the balance needed to avoid federal clawbacks while expanding services.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on gender-affirming care expansion at Corona clinic

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Well, first, I'm going to hand it to Aaron in a second.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First, let me share that it's incredibly important that we get the messaging right here and that we lean in on the comms and the campaign here, but it's also important to deliver for people, you know, and to provide the services they need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're excited to say that pretty soon we're going to be able to offer gender-affirming care directly at our clinics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have a clinic that will be opening up in Corona, which will offer gender-affirming hormone therapy for adults.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's like one of the first times a public health department has ever taken that step, and we're proud to not just stop there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We'll continue moving forward with this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Well, can I ask a follow-up on that?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Particularly, because that's a really big deal, but also it's, you know, we're seeing this devastating decrease in services for youth.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And especially youth under 13, 12, like there are almost no providers who provide that care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And the one or two to do is obviously under attack from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So is there going to, are you thinking about an expansion in that youth care?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Because

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I'm talking to parents all the time and they don't know where to take their children.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, as you can appreciate, the
