utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: balance that we have to strike is we are committed to this issue and want to make sure that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: we provide the services and resources for youth, as well as making sure that we don't expose ourselves to clawbacks from the federal government, which disrupt the rest of the care that we can give.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so there's much more to come on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm trying to sort of...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Figure out that right balance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're eager to work with you on this, but rest assured we are working on this and we're trying to figure out how to do this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes, yeah, the numbers.

chapters.summary: Anderson provides numbers: $2M for the TGNCNB Empowerment Fund across 20 CBOs, and $2.75M in FY26 council discretionary contracts across five vendors for trans-equity programming including primary, surgical, and mental health care.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on trans-equity program funding levels

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks Chair Cabana, thanks Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So just to put some numbers on a couple of things we've talked about.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: One is, as the Commissioner mentioned, this 20 community-based organizations that are receiving funding.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So that's through the

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: TGNC NB Empowerment Fund, and that's a $2 million investment.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: And then there's also the trans-equity programming.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: So there are seven FY26 city council discretionary contracts across five vendors totaling $2.75 million.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: That supports primary care, surgical care, mental health care, education programs, and a whole host of other resources.

chapters.summary: Cabán notes the LGBTQIA+ caucus secured $13.25M last year and advocates want more. Martin acknowledges the gap between the mayor's campaign commitment and current exec budget, saying more is to come. Cabán also asks about mental health services for anti-trans violence impacts.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on gender-affirming care investment gap and mental health

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I will say that...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The investments for just on the trans-equity front, we want to see them higher, right?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Last year, the LGBTQIA plus caucus, along with community advocates, like shout out to the Trans-Equity Coalition, who did incredible work, were able to secure in initiative funding

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: a historic investment, almost $13.25 million for gender-affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And there is a greater need today than there was even last year.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I know that the advocates are asking for a bigger investment from the administration.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I know it's something that the mayor ran on when he was running for office.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And so, like, you know, while we see that investment there, it is there's a lot of room between.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That commitment that was made and what we see right now in the exec budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So do you anticipate us seeing differences when it comes to the adopted or what are we looking at there?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we see you, we hear you, we're on the exact same page and we can't get in front of

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: City Hall or OMB on this, but there's much more to come on this exact issue.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We want to be partners in this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So whatever we can do to help on this.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That front is a big deal.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And then before I pass it,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Before I pass it over to Chair Shulman, I have one more question for you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Gender identity discrimination complaints, five-year high, right, while transgender New Yorkers are obviously continuing to face harassment and violence and barriers to care.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What investments particularly...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Are being made to address mental health impacts of discrimination and anti-trans violence?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Like how much is being specifically earmarked to culturally competent mental health services for transgender, gender non-conforming, and non-binary New Yorkers?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I realize that question, like it could be in public health, it could be in the DOHMH portion, but I'm just going to ask it now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, well, I'll start the conversation and then we can continue with some of our...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: experts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, look, you know, from our standpoint on our mental health side, our services are available to all New Yorkers, including LGBTQIA plus New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Now, that being said, there are specific allotments and carve outs that help to allow us to do our work even more focused in that community.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so I want to not

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: you know, jump out ahead of our mental hygiene team who can give you a little bit more specifics in that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But yeah, absolutely, we do see an opportunity, not just for the LGBTQIA community writ large with regard to mental health, but also the youth.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: component is incredibly important that we get that right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Pass it to you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Restler raises 465 vacant supportive housing units under DOHMH with no referral in process. Martin acknowledges the issue, noting a 5-6% vacancy rate across 13,300 units, and says some details will be addressed by the mental hygiene team later.
chapters.title: CM Restler on vacant supportive housing units

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Congratulations on your appointment, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, it's when people are new to city government, one of the quickest ways to judge them is based on the people you hire and put around you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I was very pleased by your chief of staff appointment, somebody who I worked with for a long time at ACS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Shout out to Jill.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Shout out to Jill Krause.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, a few things from me.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll try to go fast because I'd like to cover a fair bit.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So according to the report we received earlier this year based on legislation we passed last year, there are currently 465 vacant

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: supportive housing units that are contracted by the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They are offline with no referral in process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So that's nearly 500 units of housing offline, no referral in process, supportive housing, the most urgently needed kind of single type of housing in all of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have a breakdown of why those units are vacant and do they need significant repairs?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And if, as the City Council's Progressive Caucus has advocated for with our Rentals Within Reach campaign, we were to provide additional funding to fix those units up, would you take it to help to connect more residents who need supportive housing to it?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, let me say, first of all, you're exactly right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We see the same picture.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As you do, at the end of the day, sometimes home is the most important intervention for individuals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're proud to contract over 1,300.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 13,300 units in total.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our vacancy rate is somewhere in the 5% to 6% range.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There are some details here that I want to hold off until our mental hygiene team.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll come back to them this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But I'll just say that it's a little complicated when we're trying to, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: move folks through the process, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There are natural things that happen.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: People pass away or there are other complications that make it challenging to quickly get people in and out.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's also important to make sure you get the right person in the right home.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Without a doubt, all of that's true.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There's a separate conversation we have to have about how we do a better job of getting somebody through the application process and into housing more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But the thing that we have focused on for both NYCHA and supportive housing under DOHMH and HRA's purviews on the supportive housing side is that we have 1,000 city units that we fund that are vacant and that have no referral in process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And that, in my opinion, is offensive.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's disgraceful.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have thousands of people sleeping on the streets and in our subways who are suffering from chronic mental illness, who have no place to go.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The number one solution, the evidence-based, cost-effective solution is supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And the fact that we have so many vacant units with no referral in process is...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think mismanagement that we cannot defend or accept.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so we want to put the resources in place to get those units fixed up and work together.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: on it, so we'll follow up with the team this afternoon further on that.

chapters.summary: Restler emphasizes the urgency of vacant units with thousands sleeping on streets. He then asks about community health workers and the 233,000 people losing essential plan coverage, pressing on what insurance options exist and how navigators can help with Medicaid recertification.
chapters.title: CM Restler on health insurance loss and community health workers

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I was really pleased by the article in Cranes this morning about the additional community health workers, and so I just wanted to run through this a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So our current estimates are that 233,000 people are going to lose access to health care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They'll no longer be eligible for the essential plan after the changes that were made in Albany.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so just to give a sense, to have this on the record and for everyone to understand, that's a single person who's earning between $31,920 and $39,900.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So somebody making $30-something thousand a year, living in poverty, right, in New York City, cannot get by, between 200 and 250% of the poverty line, no longer has lost their health care as a result of the changes that...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That have been made.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So could you, I think there's two things I'd like you to speak to because I'm out of time and I don't want to, Chair Lee will kill me because she's got 16 more hearings today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Firstly, how are these community health workers, what are the health care options, the health insurance options that these community health workers are going to be able to connect these very low income New Yorkers to now that they're no longer eligible for the essential plan?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then secondly, I'm profoundly concerned about the 500,000 people that are going to lose access to Medicaid as a result of the new bureaucracy that is being imposed around recertification every six months.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know that we don't have the terms yet from Albany, from the State Department of Health, on exactly what that process is going to look like, but how can those community health workers do more?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: to proactively engage New Yorkers and try to make that process as easy as possible.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that part of the purview of, I believe, the 46 individuals that you're hiring with those $3 million?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, look, we could talk for hours on this.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I tried to go fast, but big questions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We'll follow up with you, and it's clear that you understand this issue and really are committed to helping to address it, and so we're eager to work with you on this.

chapters.summary: Martin details the Board of Health resolution, the $500K enrollment campaign, and NYC Care as a safety net option. On Medicaid, he describes 26 new FTEs as a floor, with navigators, volunteers, and CBOs through Access Health building additional capacity.
chapters.title: CM Restler on essential plan and Medicaid enrollment strategies

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let's talk one at a time.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So first, with the essential plan, your data is exactly right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 230-Something thousand individuals are going to end up losing coverage.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So what do we do about it?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So first, we pulled the Board of Health together just about two weeks ago to pass a resolution that calls this what it is.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Number one, these are attacks on public health insurance by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Number two, it commits us to creating a plan to address these coverage loss scenarios, and we immediately put money behind it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We launched a campaign to help individuals who may lose their central plan,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: help them enroll in other coverage options, including NYC Care, which is another type of coverage, as you know, safety net of the safety net type of coverage options.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But third, helping these individuals also recognize that they can always come to us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have 11 clinics across the city, and folks can come to us to get their care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: With regard to Medicaid, yeah, you know, so we have 40.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 26 new FTEs that are going to be coming in to help us do this work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's the baseline.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's sort of a floor.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: On top of that, we can build in navigators and people who are looking for either volunteer work or looking to do workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we can add many, many, many more layers on top of that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we have a huge community of community-based organizations as part of the Access Health program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So the bottom line is this is us as a health department putting our hand up and saying we are committed to this fight.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're going to work with other organizations.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're going to work with other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're going to work to get this done on behalf of New Yorkers.

chapters.summary: Restler underscores that 9% of NYC residents will lose health insurance. Martin, as an ER doctor, anticipates increased volume and emphasizes the health department's role in helping ER docs by getting people insured. Restler appreciates the creativity in approaching the crisis.
chapters.title: CM Restler on devastating impact of federal health policy changes

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, I really appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think, you know, we can't just underscore how devastating the federal policies have been that are going to lead to one in 11 New Yorkers losing their health care, right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Nine percent of New York City residents are going to lose their health insurance as a result of changes to Medicaid and eligibility changes to the essential plan.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And when we're shifting.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, what is billions of dollars, I mean, extraordinary sums onto the health and hospitals that, you know, as a result of so many newly uninsured people going in for care there, you know, NYC Care is a good model.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I helped to launch it, but it's not insurance.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, frankly, the wait times on primary care and secondary care are not what we want them to be.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So, you know, we have limited options here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Appreciate your

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: creativity and trying to step up and saying we want to do our best to connect people to care and preventative care as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think we all need to be much more focused on this issue and immediately and I appreciate your leadership and pushing this conversation.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you and we're looking forward to working with you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, as I can say as an ER doctor who is part of the health and hospital system, I think we are going to see a much larger volume of folks coming through.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: By our ERs and the bottom line is we as a health department need to do our job to help ER docs like myself so that we can take care of people but also not have to worry about helping them get insured.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just want to recognize we've been joined by Councilmember Murano on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to, in the interest of time, there were a couple questions that the deputy speaker had wanted to ask about the racial equity plan, which we'll forward you guys, and hopefully you can get back to us on those.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will pass it off to Chair Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And apologies, I had to step out and take an important phone call, so I apologize.

chapters.summary: Chair Schulman asks Speaker Menin's questions about the Office of Healthcare Accountability. Martin describes the price comparison tool launched in December, with 15,000 users, and the annual report due by August 1st per agreement with the Speaker.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman (for Speaker Menin) on Office of Healthcare Accountability

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, so the speaker had to be called away.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: She asked me to ask the questions that she wanted to ask, so I'm going to ask on her behalf.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The Office of Healthcare Accountability was established in June 2024 through the Healthcare Accountability and Consumer Protection Act, which the speaker spearheaded while serving as chair of the Committee on Consumer and Worker Protection.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The office was created to examine healthcare costs across New York City, improve transparency in healthcare pricing and spending, and identify strategies.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: To reduce costs for patients and the healthcare system overall.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The fiscal 2025 adopted budget added $2 million and 15 positions to DOHMH's budget for the Office of Healthcare Accountability, but no additional funding was added to support the office in the preliminary and executive plans.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Pursuant to Local Law 78, the office is required to publish an annual report detailing its findings, analyses, and recommendations, which is required to be released by January 1st.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: What progress has the Office of Healthcare Accountability made since it was established?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, first of all, just thank you to the speaker for her leadership on this issue.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We don't think you can have a conversation about affordability in this city without talking about health care, and we don't think you can hold health care systems accountable without a body like this, the Office of Health Care Accountability.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, the thing that I would say right up front is the tool that we were able to release last December, the price comparison tool, offers New Yorkers an ability to check

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: the price of 30-something, you know, common procedures and services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And this gives us, gives New Yorkers the insight that they need to make decisions about where they should go for their care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Secondly, yes, we're hard at work at the annual report.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have an agreed-upon deadline with the speaker to make sure that we get that out the door.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: by August 1st.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we will be working with her, her team, and with council to make sure that we get the information that we need to do with regard to the annual report.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me pause there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Much more I could say on the office, but I know you may have other questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And the portal that you were talking about, do we know how many people, I know we asked this during the preliminary budget hearings, how many people have used it?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How are we marketing it?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That kind of stuff.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, 15,000 people have used it.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And yes, we are absolutely singing praises of this site from the rooftop.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think it's an incredibly effective tool, and folks should get online and should use it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You can just Google search price transparency tool, New York City Health Department.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You should put it out in one of your wonderful tweets that you always put out.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Honestly, that's not a bad idea.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Has the office identified opportunities to reduce health care costs or improve affordability for New Yorkers?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we're hard at work trying to identify the intersection between the four key areas that we're trying to address with affordability and this office.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So area one is Medicaid coverage and helping individuals who are at risk of losing their Medicaid stay covered.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Number two is benefit enrollment, so cash assistance benefits like SNAP, WIC, EITC, helping individuals get those benefits.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The third is medical debt.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: trying to erase or prevent as much of it as possible.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And the third is eviction prevention.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're looking right now at this sort of intersection between what the office has done and what the office can do to address these four areas.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I'm personally very excited about the ability to leverage this entity to accomplish some of our goals for affordability.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How many of the 15 budgeted positions are currently filled?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Currently, we have 12 of the 15 filled.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have three remaining slots.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These are some of the slots that I mentioned earlier, highly technical medical economist slots.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're looking not to just fill the roles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We want to have the right person in each of those roles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So if council has quality candidates, we'd be looking to interview them and potentially bring them on board.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about the office's coordination with other agencies. Martin cites work with OLR on the NYCHA PPO plan and healthcare economists analyzing reimbursement rate negotiations between NYU Presbyterian and Emblem Health, though no final analysis is available yet.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on healthcare accountability office coordination and reimbursement

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How is the office coordinating with other city agencies and health care stakeholders?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we're working across City Hall to do the work of holding health care systems and payers accountable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll give you just one concrete example.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're working closely with OLR on an upcoming report looking at the NYCHA PPO plan and making sure that that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: shift to this new PPO is done in a way that is not limiting access to care and is also decreasing costs.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Given ongoing reimbursement rate negotiations between New York Presbyterian and Emblem Health, what role has the Office of Healthcare Accountability played in assessing the impact of provider reimbursement rates on city healthcare costs?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, another really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we have our team of healthcare economists and policy analysts beginning to ingest that data and to analyze it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: To assess what the impacts are going to be for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the present moment, we don't have a final analysis to share on that, but we can circle back.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: When is the, I know you said you're working on the report.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Do you have an estimate of when the report will be ready?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we're aiming to get that by August 1st.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And, you know, we can, I'm sure some of these questions here, because I want to skip to something else, will be answered in the report.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to ask you about...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Hold on one second.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Sorry, it's...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Just a couple of quick questions.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about baselining the $10M Groceries to Go program. Martin calls it a huge program serving 4,200 people in 2025, with ongoing OMB conversations. Schulman also asks about TNR and spay-neuter services, to which Martin responds as a proud cat dad.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on Groceries to Go baselining and TNR services

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is about groceries to go.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So groceries to go every year, it's financed at $10 million, and I want to know if there's any move by you guys to push OMB to baseline it because...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Every year it's $10 million, and then we have to keep pushing and pushing and pushing.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And, you know, it should just be baselined at this point.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, huge fan of this program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is an incredibly important program for individuals throughout the city who need access to fresh fruit, produce, and...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Food that keeps them healthy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've been able to help about 4,200 people over the course of calendar year 2025.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're constantly in communication with OMB to figure out how to meet the needs of our people that we serve and are looking to continue those as we build it out.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to, in the interest of time, I think we're going to send you a bunch of questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So we're going to ask you to answer those.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: But one question I do want to ask, you know, that last year City Council provided $500,000.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: discretionary award to Flatbush Cats to support TNR services.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So we wanted to see if the administration would consider baselining or expanding city support for TNR and low-cost spay-neuter services in future fiscal years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As a proud cat dad.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's good to know.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm just laughing because I have four cats now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I have my two normal cats.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That explains it now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: My fiance surprised me with two new foster kittens this weekend.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we will...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We will circle back.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Circle, yeah.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about the Brooklyn Animal Care Center renovation timeline. Martin says the Bronx shelter opens early 2027 and Brooklyn renovations are expected by late 2027.
chapters.title: Chair Schulman on animal care center construction timelines

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: It's an important, because it goes back to, we had questions, too, about the ACC and when is the, you know, when are they all going to be finished?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: When is the Brooklyn Animal Care Center project going to be done?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, there's an, because the other thing, too, is that the overcapacity is based on the TNR.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, everything goes hand in hand.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I can just speak to some, sorry, I regained composure here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, it's true.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We can say that the Bronx shelter is going to be targeted for opening in early 2027.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have renovations that are going on in the Brooklyn shelter.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: When is that?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: How is that going?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thinking about late 2027 for that one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: We will ask you, like I said, we'll send you a...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: bunch of questions and I'm going to hand it over to Charlie to end our session.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One more question though from Councilmember Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Narcisse asks about the $5M baseline for abortion access services. Martin describes the abortion access hub answering nearly 10,000 calls and referring 8,000 patients, with 25% of callers from outside NYC. Anderson details $1M for the hub and $4M for clinic medication abortion services.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on abortion access hub and reproductive health services

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you again, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Recent federal court activities surrounding access to my first on medication has brought back national attention on reproductive health and access to abortion services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: DOHMH operate the abortion access hub, which connects individuals to abortion providers and reproductive health resources.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And medication abortion services are currently available at four sexual health clinics.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The executive plan includes $5 million baseline starting in FYI 27 for abortion access services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How will the $5 million baseline funding be used to support abortion access and reproductive health services?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you very much for this question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We take seriously our responsibility to provide access to safe abortion services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And that means not just providing those services ourselves, as you've said, but we also make sure to provide a network.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: of providers that patients can call.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is funded through our abortion access hub.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've been able to answer nearly 10,000 calls and refer over 8,000 patients to a whole network of providers who can help them if they need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're not going to stop providing these services no matter what we hear from the federal administration.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it over to Aaron to share more.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Councilmember Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: Right, so just to reiterate, yeah, the $5 million baseline funding actually came a couple years ago, so this helped us stand up the abortion hub work.

utterances.speaker: Aaron Anderson
utterances.text: And of the $5 million, it includes $1 million for the abortion access hub itself, and then about $4 million of that is for our public health clinics to provide medication abortion services.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks why medication abortion is only at four of six sexual health clinics. Martin says a fifth is being worked on, with challenges mostly around space and logistics. He also notes utilization trends including out-of-state callers.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on medication abortion expansion plans

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Why is abortion medication currently available at only four of the city's six sexual health clinics?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And are they plans to expand access citywide?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You're right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Four out of the six currently have the capabilities to do this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are working on a fifth.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I will say that the challenges are mostly with space.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And some of these services need more than just a simple room.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, we need to be able to do the care right by the patient.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're working through some of the challenges with regard to space and logistics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But it's our hope that we'll be able to expand these services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What trends has DOHMH seen in utilization of the abortion access hub?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So far, what we know from our data is that we've been able to answer about almost 10,000, but 9,400 calls.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've been able to...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: refer about 8,000 patients.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And interestingly, what we see is that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: a pretty sizable portion are people who are not even from New York City.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: About 25% of the folks who are calling into the hub are from places like Georgia and Texas and others.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so the service that we're providing, of course, is primarily focused for New York City residents, but we're identifying that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Others across the country are using it as well.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But somewhere I heard that some of the folks from here traveling to other states to get abortion medication or care.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How that...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: When I heard it, I was wondering, how come we're leaving the city of New York to go other states?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: To get abortion supports, whatever.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I haven't heard that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We will circle back on that and have a conversation with you if it seems like that's true.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Has demand for reproductive health services changed in recent years?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I know you knew, but you can make from the data.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we'll have to get back to you on exactly what the trend looks like.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I will say that we expect it to because of some of the data that I've just suggested that, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 25% of the people who are calling our lines are not even from New York City.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so as we see the abortion access landscape across the country,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: change, I think we're going to end up seeing more intake from our own city.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How does DOHMH ensure timely reimbursement for community-based providers delivering those services?

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about maternal mortality disparities for Black and Latina women. Martin highlights the Citywide Doula Initiative with zero maternal deaths among participants, and notes mental health and substance use as leading causes of postpartum death.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on maternal mortality disparities and doula program

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And quickly, black and Latino mothers continue to experience higher rates of maternal mobility and mortality than other groups.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What is DOHMH doing to reduce the disparities?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're going to take those one at a time.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to kick it to Aaron on the reimbursement, and then I'll take the second part of the question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for the question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So we certainly aim to ensure timely reimbursement for all of our providers, including these folks.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: If there are issues, we're always happy to work with them directly.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And actually, our chief nonprofit officer position is there for exactly that type of work.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So we're happy to have that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You can see that the timing things, even though we want to continue with the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Should I continue?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: No, finish.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Finish the answer.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So, yeah, you're right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So black and Latina women face up to four to five times the likelihood of dying from pregnancy in the city, and that's unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have a number of concrete initiatives that we leverage in a targeted way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The first one that I'm most proud of is the Citywide Doula Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is a program that pairs at-risk pregnant people with doulas.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: to help them basically walk through the whole process of pregnancy with someone by their side.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've had thousands and thousands and thousands of people who have been served by the CDI, the Citywide Dual Initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've had zero maternal deaths as part of this program.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I love that improvement.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: An astounding.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Due to time in the chair, it seemed like we want to keep the time, so I'm going to pass it on back to you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, we'll definitely follow up for the rest of the questions that were not asked.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then, Phil, Councilmember, sorry, Phil, Councilmember Wong.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about the Queens Animal Care Center opening and Brooklyn animals being moved there, displacing Queens animals. Deputy Commissioner Schiff explains the alignment of projects and states no animals were euthanized due to the move. Wong urges better adoption promotion.
chapters.title: CM Wong on animal care center displacement and adoption promotion

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Final question.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: In the budget over here, it says that highlights of the 27-34 year plan, you're going to have a new animal care center in the Bronx, upgrades to the animal care center in Brooklyn, and other animal welfare investments.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Now, the Queens Animal Center opened on August 1st.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Looks great, 50,000 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Beautiful.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The problem was the Brooklyn Center was closed within the same week and all the Brooklyn animals moved into Queens.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I know because the Queens Animal Care Center is within walking distance from my district office.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And what happened, the Queens animal got displaced.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And they became the victims, and many of them were sent away or even euthanized.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So what I would like to know is, do you have a plan?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Because if you're going to build, say, the Browns one, the Brooklyn animals,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: is going to, the Bronx animals will move to Brooklyn when that's finished.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And then all this is going to happen again.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: All right, so my question is, do you have a plan B on what to do with the animals while these animal centers are being renovated, rather than just push it into the other boroughs?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's my question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's a really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So first of all, we have had an increase in our budget specifically for animal control.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Number two, as you've talked about, we have the Bronx shelter that's going to be opening in 27 and the Brooklyn renovation, which is becoming in the end of 2027.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are certainly aware of the increased surge in the capacity here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it over to Deputy Commissioner Corinne Schiff to share a little bit more here.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And then the Queens Animal Care Center is, as you say, a beautiful state-of-the-art facility.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: When we opened that facility, we were able to actually align the...

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Full renovation of the Brooklyn site at the same time as we were opening up the Queens site.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And what that let us do is move more rapidly to finish the Brooklyn site.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And what we had been concerned about is the alternative was to be building the, doing the renovation at the Brooklyn site while staging those renovations.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So the animals and the staff would have to be on site.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: That was going to slow us down, but it was also going to leave the animals and

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: and the staff in a, essentially in a construction zone, which would have been very stressful for the animals.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So we managed to align the two projects and we moved the animals that were in Brooklyn to the Queens Animal Care Center before it opened.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: That let us keep status quo, the number of full-service shelters that we had operating in New York City, as the commissioner just said, and as

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: the chair had mentioned, we will be opening a full-service shelter for the first time in the Bronx, and we will be opening a full-service shelter in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: That's going to expand our capacity across the city.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My final comment is that there's room for improvement on animal adoption because what I see is that if we can do better on that, then far fewer animals will get euthanized.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I get calls every other day, you know, animal XYZ is going to get euthanized.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So what are you doing to promote adoption?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I cannot answer them because we do not, I'm not the one that's planning them.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: And let me just say that.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: The animal care centers, there were no animals euthanized with respect to the move from Brooklyn to Queens.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: Animals are not euthanized for space.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We do have animals available for adoption.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We also have a facility in Manhattan that is solely dedicated to animal adoption.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: There are wonderful animals available for adoption from animal care centers.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: We welcome your helping us to get out the word about adopting from ACC, fostering from ACC.

utterances.speaker: Corinne Schiff
utterances.text: So those are New York City's animals, and we welcome your support in getting the word out to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces a quick break before the mental health portion of the hearing, targeting a 12:30 restart time.
chapters.title: Break announced before mental health portion of hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to take a really quick break.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to say five minutes, knowing it's probably going to be a little longer, but definitely for sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I want to make sure that we are starting at 1230 at the latest.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a good question because the council not the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, it's not his name.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: This is actually restoring, putting baseline funding in place before the restaurant.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Temporary one-time federal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's not.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's not.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, yep.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you look a little bit?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, I will.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin

chapters.summary: Chair Lee opens the DOHMH Mental Hygiene portion of the FY27 Executive Budget Hearing, joined by Chair Cabán. She outlines the $923.1M mental health budget, noting increases for mobile treatment teams, clubhouses, the Office of Community Mental Health, and savings measures. Headcount was 24 below budgeted.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opening remarks on FY27 DOHMH mental health budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I think we're ready to begin.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, so good afternoon and welcome to the second FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for the day, focusing on Department of Mental Health and Mental Hygiene on the mental health portion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague and chair, Councilmember Caban, Chair of the Committee on Mental Health and Addiction.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by the deputy speaker on Zoom, Councilmember Narcisse, Wong, Wilson, Felder and Areola on Zoom, and Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think that's it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And Councilmember Shulman, oh yeah, because you were the chair of the other one.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry about that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so welcome again to Commissioner Dr.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Alistair Martin and your team, and thank you so much for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 26, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY27-30 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Department of Health and Mental Hygiene's mental health proposed fiscal 27 budget of $923.1 million represents 0.7% of the...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is an increase of $67.8 million, or 7.9%, from the $855.4 million originally budgeted in the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, mostly involving mobile treatment teams, mental health clubhouses, the Office of Community Mental Health, and an array of savings measures.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, April 2026, DOHMH's headcount for mental health was 24 less than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to add $18 million to DOHMH's mental health portfolio to invest in intensive mobile treatment, assertive community treatment, and crisis respite centers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We also raised concerns regarding mental health programs, one-shot funding, mental health continuum, New York City 988 to ensure free suicide prevention counseling is available to all New Yorkers in crisis, peer specialists, and to ensure we are adequately funding clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Although our request was not included in the plan, I look forward to hearing from the Commissioner today.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: About their plan to sustain critical mental health programs and what steps are being taken to ensure these vital programs remain available to New Yorkers who rely on them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Chair Caban, for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Cabán delivers her opening statement, noting the $923.1M mental hygiene budget with 563 budgeted positions and 377 contracts. She highlights baseline funding cliffs for mobile treatment teams and clubhouses, and outlines focus areas including mobile treatment, opioid settlement funding, supportive housing, JISH, relay programs, and headcount.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán opening remarks on mental health budget priorities

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Tiffany Caban, Chair of the New York City Council's Committee on Mental Health.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Health and Substance Use.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you for attending today's hearing on the city's fiscal 2027 executive budget for the mental hygiene portion of the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene, or DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I would like to thank Finance Chair Lee for holding this executive budget hearing with my committee and also Dr.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Martin and your team for testifying today.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: In the fiscal 2027 exec budget, DOHMH mental hygiene's proposed operating budget is $923.1 million, of which $63.2 million is spent on

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: personnel services for 563 budgeted positions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The budget also includes nearly $860 million for other than personnel services, most of which covers 377 mental hygiene contracts.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So the net change of $67.7 million in the DOHMH mental hygiene fiscal 2027 exec budget compared to the fiscal 2027 prelim budget,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think, is mainly due to a baseline funding cliff for mobile treatment teams and DPHMH-funded clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, and I think that's important to point out and be clear about.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And the changes also include a one-time funding to support the Office of Community Mental Health and the newly created Office of Community Safety, as well as additional opioid settlement funding that the department received.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So the executive budget, it also includes an array of new savings measures focused on contract renegotiations, vacancy reductions, and anticipated underspending.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm interested in...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: hearing, you know, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Martin and your team, your testimonies on how these savings were achieved and whether they would impact operations.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: One thing is clear, New Yorkers with mental health and substance use issues rely on all of us to advocate for them and allow them with adequate resources to continue receiving services and care that they need.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I do want to ensure that the city doesn't cut funding that will impact critical services.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so the committee is going to focus on the following topics for questions today.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And that's going to be the DOHMH mobile treatment teams, the opioid settlement funding, the city supportive housing program, as well as JISH, the Justice Involved Supportive Housing, DOHMH relay programs, and headcount changes.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I know that our chair, who's well-versed in this area as well, is going to focus on some other related areas.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But before we begin, I would like to thank the financial analyst, Aman.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Mahadevan, we gotta put respect on our names, you know?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And Assistant Director Florentine Caboret and Deputy Director is right for preparing this hearing and the committee staff, Sarah, Sarah Sucher and Justin Campos for their support.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The committee is just incredible.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They are the best.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I would also like to thank my staff, Jonah Birch.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I'm gonna turn it over to Chair Lee now.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces the June 10th public testimony hearing. Committee counsel Brian Sarfo swears in Commissioner Martin, Deputy Commissioner Anderson, Deputy Commissioner Dr. Petit, Assistant Commissioner Knuckles, and Assistant Commissioner Dr. Lynn Walton.
chapters.title: Public testimony announcement and swearing-in of mental health witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm going to make the same announcement as I did before, which is that we are having public testimony hearings all day, Wednesday, June 10th.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I feel like I'm promoting a party, but I hope you guys are going to join us and listen in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But yes, June 10th, starting at 930, is going to be an all-day public testimony hearing day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please join us for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And without further ado, I'll pass it off to Brian Sarfo, our committee counsel, to swear in their witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Commissioner Dr.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Alistair Martin.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Anderson.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Dr.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Pettit.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Assistant Commissioner Knuckles.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: And Assistant Commissioner Dr.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: Lynn Walton.

utterances.speaker: Brian Sarfo
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Commissioner Martin testifies on the mental health budget, highlighting the $12M peer workforce investment, $20M perinatal mental health investment, and naloxone distribution. He emphasizes harm reduction, accessibility, and honoring every person's humanity across services.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin opening testimony on mental health priorities

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, sorry, one thing really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Aldebell.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And sorry, go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Fantastic.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair Lee, Chair Kavan, Chair Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Council members who are here with us today.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Alistair Martin.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm the Commissioner of Health at the New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm joined today by our Chief Financial Officer, Aaron Anderson, Executive Deputy Commissioner of Mental Hygiene, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: George Petit, and Assistant Commissioner Jamie Nichols, and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Rebecca Lynn Walton.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today on our executive budget as it relates to mental health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our mental health work is a core component of our larger vision for the health department and the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We offer a wide range of free services for justice-involved populations and New Yorkers struggling with mental health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: mental illness, or substance use.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Some of those services are delivered directly by the health department employees.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In addition, we work with more than 200 community providers to deliver more than 500 programs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These programs offer crisis intervention, housing, clinical support, and many other mental health programming.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We build on that work every day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: For example, in my first 90 days at the agency, I was proud to announce a $12 million investment in the peer workforce at substance use recovery programs across New York City,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: a $20 million investment in perinatal and early childhood mental health services, as well as the expansion of our nurse-family partnership program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And a new campaign that encourages New Yorkers to order and carry our free naloxone kits.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Last year alone, we distributed more than 285,000 naloxone kits and more than 100,000 fentanyl test strips.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Across every service, we are focused on harm reduction, accessibility, and honoring every person's humanity.

chapters.summary: Martin details the $12M opioid settlement fund investment for peer workforce development, aiming to employ 500 more peers over four years. He describes the peer model as honoring lived experience in recovery.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on peer workforce and opioid settlement investment

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That is perhaps best exemplified by our peer workforce programs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Last month, the Momdani administration announced a $12 million investment of opioid settlement funds.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That money will go towards peer workforce development programs for substance use recovery, including things like internships,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: scholarships, and direct hiring opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Over the next four years, this money will ultimately help employ 500 more peers across New York.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The peer model honors the fact that people in recovery have a story no one else can tell.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There is no substitute for lived experience.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The recent investment in this workforce will help equip 500 more people to retrace their steps on the road to recovery and bring someone back with them.

chapters.summary: Martin discusses supportive housing, noting the 15 over 15 program has surpassed 13,000 units. He highlights the average length of stay of 7.6 years and the syringe redemption program collecting 1.7 million syringes in its first year, now baselined.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on supportive housing and syringe redemption program

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Whether New Yorkers are struggling with substance use, mental illness, or transitioning out of incarceration, they need the same things we do to know that they are not alone and to have a safe place to come home to.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We recognize that affordable, stable housing is a starting point for recovery.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we're determined to get as many New Yorkers as possible housed and connected to resources.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We remain committed to the goal of the 15 over 15 program to develop 15,000 units of supportive housing, which provide affordable, independent, and permanent homes to New Yorkers who are unhoused.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And either have or are at risk of having a serious mental illness or a substance use disorder.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This year, we surpassed 13,000 units of both 15 over 15 units and state-city partnership program units.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are well on our way to meeting or exceeding our goal.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Approximately 13% of our supportive housing units serve families with children.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: About 5% serve young adults.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In aggregate, that's more than 2,000 units that are providing New Yorkers with stability early in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All 13,000 of these apartments provide long-term solutions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our data shows that for people moving out of supportive housing, the average length of stay is 7.6 years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These units are a lifeline for thousands of our neighbors, especially in one of the most expensive cities in the world.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In this year's executive budget, I'm particularly proud to see the necessary funding to turn our community syringe redemption pilot program into a mainstay initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That program offers payment for the collection and safe disposal of used syringes in public spaces.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Participants receive guidance on how to safely dispose of syringes and are paid 20 cents a syringe for up to $10 a day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In the first year alone, that program was responsible for the collection of more than 1.7 million syringes and enrolled over 1,800 participants.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's 1.7 million syringes safely discarded away from public reach.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: By every metric, it was a remarkable success.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's reflected in the testimonials of participants.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: One of our earliest adopters said this program gave them a reason to not reuse needles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: While removing litter is one small piece of a larger ecosystem for harm reduction and recovery, it's making a tangible difference in our communities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The syringe redemption program is complemented by the ongoing work of our outreach and syringe litter programs, who also offer naloxone,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Referrals to New Yorkers who frequent the high-use areas our teams prioritize.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Every outreach and syringe litter team partners with a syringe service program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: These SSPs provide overdose prevention education, HIV and hepatitis C testing, and referrals to a range of health and social services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There are 14 SSPs across the city, and they provide services at both brick and mortar locations and through mobile vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Their work reaches approximately 22,000 people a year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm grateful to see the value of the Serene Redemption Program and the larger harm reduction work it's part of be recognized by our city budget.
