chapters.summary: Martin describes the mental hygiene division's 600 employees and $900M operating budget. He highlights baseline funding solutions for mobile treatment teams (IMT and AOT), clubhouses, and notes federal funding is concentrated in disease control, not mental hygiene.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on mental hygiene division structure and funding stability

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: While mental health care is integral to programming across the health department, our mental hygiene division leads that work and employs about 600 people with an operating budget of over $900 million for fiscal year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There are several components of this year's city budget that support our mental health programs.

chapters.summary: Martin details IMT teams serving over 1,000 people annually and AOT teams supporting over 2,000. He describes clubhouses as community strongholds with 13 sites across all five boroughs, where participants are members, not patients. He closes with a story about a public servant named Bev who called the work a calling.
chapters.title: Commissioner Martin on mobile treatment, clubhouses, and federal funding

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In particular, there are long-standing funding cliffs that have been structurally solved in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're grateful to see our work receive the long-term stability it deserves and break out of the pattern of year-to-year funding.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That includes our mobile treatment programming, which encompasses intensive mobile treatment and assisted outpatient treatment teams.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: IMT teams serve New Yorkers whose needs are not being met in traditional health care settings.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They serve some of our hardest-to-reach neighbors and meet them wherever they are, at home, in the showroom,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: shelter, street corner.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Many of these teams include both clinicians and peers who are equipped to support New Yorkers through mental health and substance use disorder treatment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Services are individualized and courses of treatment are expected to last several years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That program reached more than 1,000 people a year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In parallel, our assisted outpatient treatment teams support adults with a history of multiple hospitalizations or harm caused by non-adherence to mental health treatment plans.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: AOT reinforces recovery by monitoring the progress of a court-ordered treatment plan and supporting New Yorkers through care coordination across multiple service providers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That work supports more than 2,000 people a year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are also encouraged to see funding stabilized for clubhouses, which provide New Yorkers living with serious mental illness with help accessing benefits, employment opportunities, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There are 13 clubhouses across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Each of these physical sites houses a community stronghold for New Yorkers with mental illness.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 40% of members have been enrolled for more than five years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Here, participants are not clients, they're not patients, they're members.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And each clubhouse is designed by and for the members it serves.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: New Yorkers deserve reliable, stable access to these services, and we're reassured this council and this administration are dedicating resources to sustain them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Finally, at the federal level, we are watching this administration's actions very closely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Closely.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: National mental health infrastructure has consistently been devalued as far as we've seen under this administration.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Harm reduction work in particular has come under unwarranted scrutiny.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the New York City Health Department, our federal funding is concentrated in our disease control and our emergency preparedness divisions.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our mental hygiene division is not heavily reliant on federal funding, but every part of our agency is impacted by the rapidly changing public health landscape we live in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We continue to monitor changes in federal funding, infrastructure, and policy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so in many ways, this year ahead is sure to be a challenging one, but I have every confidence that we will meet the moment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That confidence is not derived from any one leader, but it's derived from the many public servants committed to showing up for our city every day.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Early in my tenure as health commissioner, I spoke with one of our public servants working in substance use recovery.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Her name was Bev.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: When I asked Bev what keeps her going every day, she told me something I'll never forget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: She told me this wasn't a job, it was a calling.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm grateful to lead an agency of people who took a job and made it a calling.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I have every confidence my agency will continue to answer the call for our city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thanks for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're happy to answer your questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about state funding for mental health. Martin describes the state ACT program expansion and strong working relationships with OMH and OASAS. Lee asks about the dollar amount; Martin says they'll follow up.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on state mental health funding and ACT expansion

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm gonna quickly try to go through these so that I can pass it off to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in terms of, similarly to the last port question about state funding, the executive plan includes state funding of 464.5 million in FY26 and 414 million for FY27.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And because the executive plan was released before the enactment of the state fiscal 27 budget, what was added in the executive budget that was previously proposed in the state's executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is there new mental health and substance use funding in the state's adopted budget flowing to DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, we're happy to partner with our state colleagues on this work, the Office of Mental Health and OASIS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're always in conversation with our partners at the state to define how we can better intervene as co-partners in this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: State Department of Mental Health is spearheading a couple of expansions that we're aware of.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The first is an expansion of the ACT program statewide.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we're going to be closely monitoring this expansion so that we can see in what ways does this impact our city's program with regard to ACT.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Ultimately, from our perspective, we continue to advocate strongly for sustained

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: state investment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We need the state to be part of this work and be our partners here in the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so far we've had really, really great working relationships with the state.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, especially, you know, thanks to, of course, also on the substance use side, the opioid settlement funds.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm glad that that's now finally being

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: realized and spent down as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, and good to hear about the ACT programs being expanded.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know how much it was, how much that included for the expansion?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: For the state Department of Mental Health?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, we're gonna have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how often DOHMH assesses CBO program effectiveness. Martin and Anderson explain all CBOs are evaluated at least annually, with ongoing metric tracking. Lee asks whether any services would function better delivered in-house; Dr. Petit argues community-based providers are best for community-embedded services.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on CBO contract assessment and service delivery models

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In terms of contracts or savings, DOHMH has one of the agencies that has a really large number of contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just wanted to know how often does DOHMH assess the effectiveness of its programs run by CBOs, nonprofits?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me ask, Chair, is this related to EO12?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Or just broadly speaking, our contract re-evaluations?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think in general, but yes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I mean, the savings portion, yes, it's related to that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But also in general, I think for best practices.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, how often we do it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So, yeah, let me start on the savings piece, the EO12 piece, and then I'll get to Aaron to show a little bit more here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So similar to our mandate with regard to public health, you know, we're trying to balance these two priorities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The first is really L.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: aligning with the mayor's vision on the chief savings officer initiative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At the same time, we're making sure to not cut any services with regard to our mental hygiene portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And I'm proud to say that we've come through this process with EO12, and I can stand firmly here and say that no programs

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: were cut and no services were degraded as part of that experience.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron to share a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks Commissioner and thanks Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean as a general matter all CBOs are evaluated once a year at least.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, for the contract renewal?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Even if it's a three to five year contract that they still sort of have the annual?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And I mean, aside from that, and I would certainly defer to my programmatic colleagues beyond this, but I mean, I think we're always thinking about metrics and how to make programs better and how they're doing and all of that good stuff.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And are there service or program areas based on what you've done with the assessment that would function better if they were delivered by DOHMH directly?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And this is not just specifically related to the CBO contracts, but in general.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I guess you're asking the question of whether or not it's better for us to bring the services internal versus in-house, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, it's a really good question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit in a second.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But yeah, I think this is the core tension, not just on the mental health side, but also on the public health side, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: What do we keep in-house?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And what do we, you know, sort of contract out and find a service delivery partner for?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So getting that right is something that we're constantly trying to improve on.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so let me share it, hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit to share a little more.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: You know, as a public sector psychiatrist doing this for 30 years, it is an interesting question to be thinking about where the role of, you know, the division is vis-a-vis the providers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: But I do have to say, you know, the way that the entire system has been sort of built up over the last, you know, many decades is really relying on contracted providers who are deeply embedded in their communities, who know their communities

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: in ways that it's harder for us centrally to be as attuned to some of those needs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Even though we're aware of them, it really is important for these niche providers to be able to deliver an array of services in their communities to their constituents a little bit differently than we could.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: There are things that we could do citywide that are more overarching that really speak to the system as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: But I am a big believer that a lot of our programs that exist in these communities are best served by community-based providers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a former provider, I fully subscribe to that, especially in communities that have very specific language needs and cultural needs, so yes.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $26M telepsychiatry contract with Somethings for teens, set to expire. Dr. Petit explains the TeenSpace program has had 45,000 registrations, and the contract will be extended while a new RFP is developed, hopefully before end of year.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on teen telepsychiatry contract and TeenSpace program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so DOHMH has a three-year contract accounting for $26 million with a CBO called Somethings for a telepsychiatry service program for teens.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the contract is set to expire at the end of FY26.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wondering what is the update you can provide for this specific contract, and can you elaborate more about the service?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, so first of all, the service that we are providing here is one that we take very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is teen mental health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And for teens, you have to be able to meet them wherever they are.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And in this case, we are trying to meet them digitally.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so that's what you're seeing with the work that we're doing on Teen Space.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The contract that we're talking about here is one that we are trying to

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: re-procure and do it in a fair way, in a transparent way, and also we're making sure that we can continue to deliver the service so that there is no service disruption for teens that are involved.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit to share a little bit more on the details here.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you again.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So this contract was actually a demonstration, a three-year demonstration project, so it's about to end in October, and we are going through the process of a concept paper and ultimately a reprocurement of it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: I think what's really important is that there is an evaluation tied to this so that we can actually determine not just lessons learned.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: over the last three years, but where things are at currently, like we talked about a little bit earlier, where we are today is very different than where we were three years ago, especially with a lot of this technology.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So, you know, TeamSpace is a free, regardless of zip code, you know, location or wherever, an individual, a kid 13 to 17 can actually access, you know, talk, text, or chat in a free way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So it's been incredibly helpful and impactful.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We've had over 45,000 registrations since inception, and I think we can do more with this, and that's why we're looking at a procurement process that will help us shape this going forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and it's kind of crazy to think that in three years the technology has advanced so much in that short time as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so just to confirm, because I think we had heard that DOHMH was not considering renewing the grant starting in FY27, but you're saying that you are going to look at issuing out a new RFP and then putting that out?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, so we will extend the contract until a procurement is out on the street.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and you're saying that should be around October 27, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Or 27?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So the contract is up now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Or it's terminating now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We will be extending the contract as we're going through the process of the concept paper and the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So the extension will be...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Until the next contract is able, until we're able to procure the next contract.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: When do you think that would be, the new RFP to be released?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Hopefully before the end of the year we'll have an RFP out.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just wanted to check.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, let's see.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $2.2M contract renegotiation savings. Anderson confirms it's part of EO12 savings, with conversations ongoing across several vendors, and no service impacts expected. Lee asks about the $1.2M naloxone contract renegotiation; Anderson explains naloxone costs have decreased considerably.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on contract renegotiations and naloxone cost savings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In terms of contract renegotiations, really quickly, the executive plan includes baselined reduction of $2.2 million starting in FY27 as part of contract renegotiations.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanted to know, were these contract reductions associated with the chief savings officer findings, or was it something separate?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, let me hand it over to Aaron to share more.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So, yes, that line item was part of the EO-12 savings reduction initiative.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how many contracts and what type of contracts are being renegotiated?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, so we're still in the process of looking at that.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I think we, you know, this was based on a pretty...

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: reasonable, even maybe conservative assumption.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So we're in conversations right now with several different contracted vendors.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how will the services provided through these contracts be impacted by the renegotiations?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No impact or?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, our guiding premise for this entire exercise, as we testified earlier as well, is really minimizing or making sure that there are no service impacts to the public.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And so these contract renegotiations are also happening through that lens, and so there will be no service impacts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm sorry, can you, I don't know if you answered this part, but what type of contracts?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, we're looking across the board.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, as you know, mental hygiene has an enormous portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: A handful at least that we're looking at specifically.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Obviously, we can't talk about them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could let us know and follow up, that would be awesome.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Happy to.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it also includes, the executive plan also includes reduction of $1.2 million starting in FY27 as part of a renegotiation of the naloxone contract.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are the naloxone contract renegotiations also related to...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Reduction of services due to underutilization or why was that scheduled reduction baseline?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, so this is an incredibly important topic and one that I have to say, you know, the data is moving in the right direction when it comes to overdose deaths.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're down 28% as, you know, in terms of our last recorded

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: year on file.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: A lot of that is due to programs like this one.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I just want to plug one more time the program that I mentioned in my testimony, nyc.gov slash naloxone.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Any New Yorker can get online, put their information in, and get multiple units of naloxone shipped to them completely for free.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We were able to distribute about 285,000 kits over the course of the last year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron to share a little bit more about the budget piece.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So this, I think, is actually a great example of how you can be creative when doing these savings exercises that are often pretty painful.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So the story with this one is just that naloxone has

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: come down in cost considerably over the past few years and it continues to and so we've seen it's already come down in our world quite a bit but we've seen other states California for example it's sort of hit record lows

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: and so we're watching very carefully what other states are doing around the country what other municipalities are doing and I think we're very confident that we're gonna see

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: additional savings here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, good to know.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: It's a bit of good news, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $4M baseline for clubhouses, which addresses a funding cliff for existing DOHMH-funded clubhouses rather than smaller ones. Aaron confirms the total FY27 clubhouse budget is $30.7M. Lee advocates for smaller clubhouses funded by the Council.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on clubhouse funding and smaller clubhouse reprocurement

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, moving quickly to the clubhouses, which is a topic that is very near and dear to me.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So when we saw that there was a $4 million baseline in the FY27, I was like, yes, it includes the smaller clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I realized, no, that's not what it's for.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But it's to address a stimulus funding cliff for existing DOHMH-funded clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what's the total budget for the DOHMH-funded clubhouses for FY27?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So thank you for that question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I have to say I agree with you in terms of how miraculous these programs are.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I visited my first clubhouse just a couple weeks ago.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I was at Phoenix House.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And the idea that individuals there are getting care and support, but also dignity by being a member of the clubhouse.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Being involved in the daily work of taking care of the clubhouse is just a beautiful experience.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So let me hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit to talk a little bit more about the model.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, again, thank you, Commissioner and Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So clubhouses are truly an incredible.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: system of care that exists within our city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: They are member-led.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: They're really about creating community.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: They've been a mainstay for the last 50-plus years, really being able to offer employment, education, socialization, and just a sense of purpose and belonging.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We had not looked at this portfolio for over 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So in 2024, we really underwent a serious consideration, got a lot of input from the community and from stakeholders to re-procure the whole portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So now we have 13 clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Membership has been growing, which is what we wanted.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We really want to expand the capacity, and it's growing month over month, and there still is capacity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So this is one of these programs that we have where there is room to continue to grow.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So we certainly encourage any of you, no constituents that need these types of services, to send them our way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We really want to continue to increase capacity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So again, it's an incredibly effective model.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: It's part of this continuum.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: It serves, obviously, a particular population.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We're looking at continued expansion.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: The state office of OASAS is looking at procuring for youth clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We support one of the...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: The LGBT Center's Youth Clubhouse that serves youth, which is really important, ages 13 to 22, and we provide a lot of technical assistance and support to them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So there's definitely an expansion that's underway.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, so what's the total budget amount for FY27?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron on that.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, it's $30.7 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And starting in FY20.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so around the same.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I will say, and my next question is around the reprocurement to include some of the smaller clubhouses that was funded by the city council because we put extra funding for that in.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And at the time when the concept paper for the new RFP was out, there were a lot of us, including myself, that spoke to the former commissioner about the fact that

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: a lot of these smaller clubhouses, and I know I'm preaching to the choir, you know, even if they're smaller, like a lot of times people have been going there 15, 20 years, it's a family, it's their support network, their system.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so to disrupt that and say, hey, now you have to go here and this one's not going to be open, that's why I think on the council side, we felt very strongly we want to support with our funding to make them whole.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But, you know, and ideas like, you know,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: hub and spoke model or subcontracting or allowing them to exist and then maybe be part of the larger clubhouse organizations and maybe include those numbers in there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So there were a lot of suggestions we had made, I think, at the time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wanting to know if there is a consideration to do the reprocurement for the smaller ones.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I can start this off.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As you referenced, I wasn't here when those conversations were happening.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I absolutely am committed to exploring this and figuring out if there are ways to do this in coordination with you and with city councilors across.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: City Hall to make sure we get this right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will you be able to pick up the funding?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's the thing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's probably a question for you.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, I think we're always in conversations with our partners at City Hall and OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we gotta advocate to do, well, I mean, obviously we'll do our part, and then I know Councilmember Brewer is very passionate about this as well, so we'll continue to advocate on our part.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the Office of Community Safety's role with clubhouses. Martin says there's no plan to shift current mental health services to OCS, though he's begun conversations with the OCS commissioner. Lee also asks about state youth clubhouses; Martin says DOHMH likely won't oversee them.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Office of Community Safety and youth clubhouses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will the Office of Community Safety have any role in the oversight of the mental health clubhouses?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If so, what will that role be?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've been at the work of community mental health for the last several decades here at the department.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And from our standpoint, we're eager to work with anyone who's come into the table with solutions and resources.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're eager to work with the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I just spoke with the commissioner this week, and we've begun the process of beginning to lay the foundation of that work that we will share.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At present, there's no plan to shift any of our current mental health services to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll go back to that in one second.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But to your point, the state, going really quickly to the youth clubhouses, because the state budget proposed, as you mentioned, the 15 additional youth clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you going to be overseeing or managing any of those 15 youth clubhouses?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Under DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We may have to circle back.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We typically do not.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have the clubhouses that are sort of our responsibility, but let's circle back to what the details on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $269.6M and 126 positions for OCS. Martin explains the Office of Community Mental Health is moving to OCS—$3.2M and 17 positions as of FY28—but no current mental health services will shift. Anderson confirms the numbers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Office of Community Safety funding transfers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just staying on the topic of Office of Community Safety, it's a total funding of $269.6 million and 126 budgeted positions were added starting FY28 for the newly created Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you anticipating any further funding transfers and program shifts?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because my understanding is that these are not new monies, so to speak, but it's already existing programs that are just sort of being restructured and reorganized.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So if you could speak to the budget for that, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I can speak to the component that is reflected within our work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So the Office of Community Mental Health is moving completely over to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is an office that on paper, the lines in the budget were associated with DOHMH, but we have not controlled those programs or those services at the Office of Community Mental Health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So that whole structure will now shift over to OCS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Aaron and see if he has anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, and just to add, so in terms of numbers, so that means, you know, $3.2 million in 17 positions are moving as of FY28.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: But again, that's just related to the Office of Community.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, OCMH, right?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how is this looking?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I think a lot of us are still, I don't know, we've gotten mixed feedback, but it seems like most people are still sort of not 100% clear on how OCS is going to be sort of

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: coexisting with a lot of the other city agencies that have pieces of the different portfolios that are now under them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so just wondering if you...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sort of anticipate any funding transfers or you know how it would support some of the DOHMH programs and how you would work with them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, how it's going is we're eager to do this work with them and ultimately we see that office and my counterpart commissioner as allies in doing this work together and we're ready to roll up our sleeves and get to work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Specifically with regard to the mental health services, there's no plan to move those services to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That we currently run.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so we'll keep tabs on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if obviously you hear anything, if you could let us know as well, that'd be great.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about school-based mental health clinic budgets. Martin explains these are state-licensed and funded programs. Anderson confirms funding doesn't flow through DOHMH. Lee raises concerns about startup costs and Medicaid impacts; Martin says they'll follow up with the state.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on school-based mental health clinic funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'll stop, I'll have one last question around school-based mental health clinics and then pass it on to our chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For school-based mental health clinics, what was the FY27 and FY26 budgets?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: respectively.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you very much for this question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The reality is that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We are proud of our work that we do to help on technical assistance, quality assurance, helping to do the strategic placement of these schools.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: But ultimately, these are state-licensed and funded programs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we'll have to defer some of the specifics around the funding to the state unless...

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, just to add, as the commissioner said, right, the funding for these does not flow through our budget, but the budgeting contracts for it.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Some of them at least are administered through New York City public schools or independently.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, because I know when it first started, a lot of the feedback we had heard from the community and especially the Article 31 clinics that are partnering with the schools was the startup funding costs was so little.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think it was maybe $25,000 if I remember, which was not enough for them to get started and running.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so if you've heard any feedback from the providers on how it's going,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That would be great if you could give us feedback on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that a lot of it is from Medicaid dollars with the reimbursement system.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So is that how is that going to then be impacted or have you had conversations with OMH on what that's going to look like?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On the school side, because obviously the clinics are operated under the state, but then how that impacts the families if the state Medicaid money starts drying up or if it gets cut.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, or if people lose insurance and lose coverage.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, exactly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We can certainly follow up on that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I personally haven't had those conversations yet with the state, but certainly something that we're happy to partner with them on to try and mitigate the impact of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'm going to pass it on to Chair Cabon.

chapters.summary: Cabán presses on whether DOHMH has asked OMB to fund smaller clubhouses. Martin says conversations are ongoing. Dr. Petit reiterates the reprocurement was based on community feedback and national model standards, with positive quality-of-life and employment outcomes.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on smaller clubhouse funding advocacy with OMB

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to follow up on a couple of the things that you asked about, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I have so many questions, but I'm going to try to be brief.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to ask you all to be succinct where you can.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Going back to the clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: In terms of, you know, I appreciate the really eager and sincere sort of response.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, we want to work with you guys to make sure that we are keeping these smaller clubhouses running.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But I want to know what that means.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, have you guys asked OMB for this?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Because, like, that's probably one of the biggest roles that you're able to do here is saying, hey, we need this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Funding included in the adopted.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So have you asked about that particularly?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, so let me share what we have done and then I'll kick it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit to see if there's anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have re-procured, as you know, the 13 clubhouses to make sure that we raise the standards and expand the capacity.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That was done back in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All 13 of those are now fully operational.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The conversations are ongoing with regard to what we can do with the remaining smaller clubhouses, but I'll pass it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit if he has anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: I do want to reiterate that the whole purpose of the reprocurement was to really take in all of the feedback that we had gotten, some of our own understanding of how these clubhouses had been functioning

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: up until that date, and really committed to this national clubhouse model and really wanting to invest in the types of services that we really believe in, which are really grounded on sort of, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Quality of care and looking at metrics a little bit differently through, you know, some of these outcome measures like quality of life, loneliness, and employment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: And what we are seeing is that we've made a significant impact in some of those measures, and we can definitely find ways of having further conversations.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And totally, I hear that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I get that the model is working.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What I also want to make sure is that it is being, it is participant-driven, and that, you know, probably the most important thing that we could listen to are participants saying that they want to stay where they are.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to move on just because I know that you put this on the record already and colleagues want to ask questions too, but the other follow-up I had, two more follow-ups that I have on the chair's question is on the youth club houses.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about the 15 state youth clubhouses and whether NYC will receive funding. Jamie Nichols explains these are OASIS clubhouses—nine exist in NYC and will be eligible to apply for expansion dollars, though it's a state investment.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on state youth clubhouse funding for NYC

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you tell us, I know it's, what, 13 at the state?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Is that the number that they just added money for?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Oh, for the youth?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Was it 15?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do you know how much money that is?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And also, is the city asking for a portion of that pot of money to come directly to the city?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it to Jamie Nichols to talk about the program a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In terms of the budget, we can circle back on that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it again because Bertie just whispered in my ear.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: These are Oasis clubhouses.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So it's a great model.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So what they've done is across the state, there's nine recovery clubhouses in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That's where the OSF funding went for additional peers.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: They are expanding it so that now clubhouses or recovery centers will be able to serve youth as well.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So really excited about it.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I work really closely with the Oasis folks.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's not a city investment, but it'll definitely benefit so many New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So it's not a city.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We're not going to see any of that added funding for New York City residents.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's going to be from the state, but for all the nine clubhouses in New York City, they're going to be eligible to go for those dollars so that they can expand services.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, I guess that's my question is like how much of that money may we see?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We can follow up with the R.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I mean, it's on the OASIS website, but yeah, we could share.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks what role the Office of Community Mental Health plays if it's just a coordinating agency moving to OCS. Martin clarifies DOHMH is the local governmental unit coordinating mental health activities by state law, and that's not changing. Cabán also raises the absence of harm reduction in OCS discussions.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on Office of Community Safety structure and harm reduction

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then on the Office of Community Safety, you mentioned that the Office of Community Mental Health is going to be under this office.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What I want to know is, I mean, oftentimes we do hearings and the Office of Community Health doesn't want to come because they're like, we're just a coordinating agency and we can't really speak to a lot of these different things.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So if this coordinating office, this coordinating body is coming into the Office of Community Safety, are they going to be tasked with expanding their coordination or something?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, will they be designated?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: the coordinating office for everything that's put under the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We are all still super unclear on what the organizational structure is.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And if the Office of Community Mental Health is just a coordinating agency, what role are they playing?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Being put into, is the coordinating agency going to be coordinating with another coordinating agent?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, what are we doing here?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Well, let me simplify for the parts that we own.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we are the local governmental unit, which is decreed by state law.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That means that we coordinate.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Mental health activities and mental health treatment for the city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So that part is clear and that part is not changing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: In terms of what the Office of Community Safety will do, we're going to have to get back to you with some details on that as things are still very much in the early stages now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So at the moment,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: you can expect us to continue to be the coordinating agency with regard to mental health care in the city.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Another question I have is also, you know, we didn't see this in the briefings that we got on the office, but one glaring absence, I think, in what was being considered as sort of within the scope of work of this office.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Was harm reduction.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And it didn't really make sense to me that that was something that wasn't being included in the office.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I would like to know what those conversations have been like.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Are y'all pushing to have harm reduction work integrated into

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The Office of Community Safety?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, I can maybe just kick things off and hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, from our perspective, you know, the work of harm reduction is a key core function of what we do at the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And that's not going to change.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We don't have any current plans to stop doing that harm reduction work.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That's what I find concerning because it's literally not in the model that has been proposed to us.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So that's, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: I just wanted to put a finer point on sort of our role as a local government unit.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: We have, you know, responsibility for overseeing, planning, and funding mental health, substance use, and developmental disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: It's through state, you know, mental hygiene law 9 and 41.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So our true responsibility is to ensure that in New York City that all those services are coordinated and planned for accordingly based on need.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: So we will continue to do that as the executive deputy commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: That is my primary responsibility to make sure that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I would hope to see some updated sort of metrics and goals that include harm reduction because those metrics and goals have been shared with us and exist across these different areas, but not on that front.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So that's helpful.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The last thing I'll say in the Office of Public Community Safety is I think we're all a little bit concerned about how it's coming together.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We found out, I think, earlier in the week or last week that NYPD had their first conversation with, you know, this division.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: you know, talking to offices around the country, and those, and the conversation with my PDs, those are the first ones.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, it's only successful when they're at the table in the beginning.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, just hoping that there can be more coordination on that front.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I know that that's not you guys, but I want to go to mobile treatment teams.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about mobile treatment team wait lists. Jamie Nichols reports 600 waiting for IMT, 200 for FACT, 367 for ACT, and 87 for SP-SAC, with 80-90 people connected weekly. Cabán presses on resources needed to clear the wait list; Nichols discusses step-down resources.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on mobile treatment team wait lists and capacity

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The exec plan, it increases...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Includes baseline funding for $47.3 million starting in fiscal year 27 to address a funding cliff for mobile treatment teams.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So my understanding, my reading of the proposed exec budget is that they're not operational expansions associated with funding.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's just addressing federal cuts.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So it's not like an investment above what we have been doing.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And obviously, we're interested in seeing the admin expand mobile treatment teams because there's a growing need, but also...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: There has been an existing wait list that we would like to clear.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So is DOHMH planning any operational expansions for mobile treatment teams in FY27?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you again, Chair, for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's incredibly important we get this right on mobile treatment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Since 2016, so about 10 years now, we have doubled the mobile treatment capacity in this city.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We know that that's just the scratching surface.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There's much more work to do on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Aaron to talk a little bit more about the budget here.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, I just wanted to echo Chair Cabana.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Your reading is correct about the 47.3.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And to stay in order,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: For a second, we may have doubled the capacity, but also we're seeing, and this is why there should be coordination, we're also seeing a vast increase in what we call broken windows policing or low-level offenses, and a lot of these folks that are getting picked up

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: on those kinds of offenses and then finding their way to Rikers Island are people living with a mental health diagnosis.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So we have like some cross-competing outcomes where like you may be expanding capacity, but then we're doing harm on the other end.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So yeah, if you could continue.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, no, I'll leave it to others to talk about any potential future plans, but just to say yes, it stabilizes the funding that had been covered by expired federal stimulus dollars.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And are you looking on adding on top of that?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, I think we're always thinking about what the resource needs are for the agency, but from a programmatic perspective, I'll leave it to others.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So what, in terms of the, what is the total funding allocated for IMT and ACT and the executive budget?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then just to break that down a little bit, what's coming in from the state and whatever's coming in from the state, will that get used towards new contracts or that's just to keep the status quo of what we've got right now?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Aaron to talk about the budget here.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, so thanks.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So putting aside the one-time funding from FY26, this is the partnership between the admin and the council.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So the CTL budget is baselined at 20.5.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Which we should probably baseline, I think.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So the current CTL budget, aside from that,

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Anyways, 20.5 for ACT, 4 million for FACT, 44.3 million for IMT.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And how much of that is coming in from the state?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I can get back to you probably shortly with a specific breakdown.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How many people are currently on the citywide ACT and IMT wait lists?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Assistant Commissioner Jamie Nichols for that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we get about 5,000 referrals a year to our adult single point of access.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Everybody who's referred is offered a care coordinator.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I'm happy to say that we have capacity in care coordination.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Those folks go out and visit with people proactively wherever they are in the community, help them find or stay connected to treatment and benefits and address housing and things like that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Some people, that's sufficient.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The care coordination is what they want, and they stay there, and they go to clinics or other places to get their treatment.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I know we got this information on the prelim budget.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Again, I just want to move to what currently is the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I understand also some people qualify for both, and so that kind of complicates the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But, again, I just want to.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm trying to help our team stay on time.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yep, yep.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We've got 600 people waiting for IMT, 200 for FACT, 367 for ACT, and 87 for SPAC.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Those change daily, right?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Assignments are...

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Made people are connected probably you know 80 90 people a week are connected to these programs newly as other people transition off so it's very dynamic yeah

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But still, even with the dynamic nature of it, that's a significant need.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What are the resources needed to clear the wait list?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think, as the Commissioner mentioned, we've doubled mobile treatment capacity over the last 10 years, and yet wait lists have persisted in that time.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So obviously, it's not simply straightforward, just adding teams, eliminating the wait list, because there's other pressures on the system.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're focused on offering additional resources for people to step down from these services into other...

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: longer-term treatment arrangements.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we have our Connect Clinic Enhancement Model, which we're, a demonstration project has been going really well, handling step-downs.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We have Flex Act teams, which provide larger capacity state caseloads.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Those are RFP'd by the state, but those services flow through us.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: And so we think those step-down resources can allow us to use the existing mobile treatment capacity more efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm going to apologize to my, oh wait, he's not here.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm not taking his question.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about $4.5M for the STAPS pilot and $11M for IMT providers in FY26 that haven't been released. Anderson says the administration is continuing conversations with Council and defers to OMB. Cabán emphasizes funding is only useful if it gets out the door.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on unreleased mobile treatment team funding

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But to that point though, right, on the mobile treatment teams, the city approved $4.5 million to create the steps pilot and $11 million for the IMT providers and FY26.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But those funding streams haven't been released.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so I guess my question even to...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That is, you know, like as of now, providers haven't been able to apply for that funding.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So is that funding approval, like when is that approval going to come through so that they can do that?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And then pass to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, so first, just to circle back on your question about the funding.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So for mobile treatment, it's about $12 million from the state.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And just pointing out that IMT is fully city funded.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, that's right, because it's not Medicaid reimbursable.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: In terms of the one-time funding for the 4.5 for Act and the $11 million for IMT, the administration, I know, is continuing the conversations with council.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So on that piece, we have to defer to the call on OMB.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, the funding is only as good as whether it gets out the door or not.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I, you know, I, again, would love to see a push to add.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: There's definitely a need to clear these wait lists again at a time where we're seeing more and more people be criminalized under the same environment administration

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: who are living with mental health diagnoses and don't have like a good continuum of care in place.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will the Office of Community Safety have any role in the oversight of the mobile treatment teams?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do you know?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At present, there are no plans for us to have them oversee our teams.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And also, I just want to go back for a second.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks whether DOHMH would publish wait list data quarterly. Nichols expresses concern that visible wait lists might discourage referrals. Cabán pushes back, arguing organizations already know the wait lists exist and need the data for advocacy.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on publishing mobile treatment wait list data

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: On the waitlist data, would you guys consider publishing the data quarterly?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You said it's dynamic, it changes, but would you guys...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: guys be willing to publish that?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think we're open to having a conversation with you, Chair, and exploring what's possible there.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And, oh, does anybody want to add to that?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, I think it's important to think about that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're open to it.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're also concerned that referral sources may not make referrals if they see the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: And the truth is we're reviewing every application for a variety of clinical risk factors, and so we're prioritizing higher risk applicants, and we don't want to discourage people from making referrals.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I guess, is that a direct conversation you've had with referring organizations?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Because I don't know that that's the approach that they would...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Take and I think just everybody having this information is super helpful.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, it also would make it so that, you know, one of us doesn't have to call you guys and like then get follow up from somebody else to get the information if we know that we can just like access it in a place and talk to our partners about it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I might.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I might suggest maybe rethinking that, especially if organizations haven't said that that's how they would.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: React to having the to being able to see the way they know they know the waitlist is there believe me they know they just want to know how how deep it is um because it helps them in their advocacy right like they want to be able to make

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: accurate asks of us of of you guys and i know that we can just like access it in a in a place and talk to our our partners about it so i i might

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think that that information is helpful.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I might suggest maybe rethinking that, especially if organizations haven't said that that's how they would.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to move on to one more piece here, and then I'll go to Kali.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I have more questions, but I want Kali to be able to ask questions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: React to having the to being able to see the way they know they know the waitlist is there believe me they know they just want to know how how deep it is um because it helps them in their advocacy right like they want to be able to make

Dr. Petit acknowledges the issue is significant and says while they haven't had conversations with OMH, he's eager to engage. Cabán shares a public defender example of someone languishing at Rikers.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on outpatient competency restoration program

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So the campaign to close Rikers, they requested $1.7 million to establish an outpatient competency restoration pilot for 50 to 75 people who might otherwise be waiting at Rikers for transfer to a state hospital.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: accurate asks of us of of you guys and i know that we can just like access it in a in a place and talk to our our partners about it so i i might

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So is DOHMH working with the State Office of Mental Health to develop an outpatient competency restoration program, and has the city committed any funding for a program like that?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I might suggest maybe rethinking that, especially if organizations haven't said that that's how they would.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to pass to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit for more on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Petit
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: React to having the to being able to see the way they know they know the waitlist is there believe me they know they just want to know how how deep it is um because it helps them in their advocacy right like they want to be able to make

utterances.text: So, you know, even though we have not had those conversations, I'm more than eager to, you know, engage and have further deliberations around what or how do we deal with this issue.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán

utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, and again, two months in, so I'm not 100% of everything that we do within the division, but certainly within our Bureau of Justice Impacted Populations, we have a number of initiatives.

utterances.text: We have a health justice network that sort of coordinates peer activities in individuals that are coming out of prisons.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to start with Councilmember Wong, followed by.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: By wrestler.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin

chapters.summary: Councilmember Wong asks about AOT petition data for NYC and Queens. Jamie Nichols says the state OMH has a dynamic dashboard with all petitions since inception, broken down by borough. Wong requests the data be sent to him.
chapters.title: CM Wong on Kendra's Law AOT petition transparency

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: My predecessor, Councilman Robert Holden, spent years asking for basic transparency on Kendra's law petitions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I intend to continue that work.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The question is simple.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How many assisted outpatient treatment petitions, AOT petitions, were filed in New York City in FY25?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: How many were approved?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
