utterances.text: How many were denied?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And what are the numbers of Queens specifically broken down by borough?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You know, there is a Queens AOT team.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I want to know whether it's being used, by whom, how often, and with what results.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: City agencies, NYPD, DHS, HNH can file petitions.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Which agencies are actually doing so and how often?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, absolutely agree.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And the service that we provide with AOT promotes engagement in mental health services for individuals who have had histories of multiple hospitalizations due to harm.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And have had treatment, not adherence.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so AOT provides an extra level of care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: To your question directly, in FY25, we monitored about

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: 2,800, so 2,834 individuals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And to go a little bit deeper, I'm going to hand it over to Assistant Commissioner Jamie Nichols.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We've got a lot of experience in assisted outpatient treatment.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We've been doing it since 1999 and use it, I think, more than every state in the country in terms of the scope of our program here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The lawyers and psychiatrists that do the petitions are really experienced, and very rarely is a petition ever denied.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I don't have the numbers now, but I can tell you it's a very infrequent occurrence.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: This data should not be hard to produce, and I don't understand why it hasn't been consistently reported to the council.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Is this something you could do in the future?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, we can point you.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The State Office of Mental Health has a dynamic dashboard on their website with all of the petitions and court orders that have ever been filed since the program's inception, and we can point you to that information, and it's broken down by borough.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, I'll provide my contact info later, and then you can reach that, send that to me.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we've been joined by Council Members Felder and P.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sanchez on Zoom and wanted to go to pass it on to Council Member Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Restler revisits the 465 vacant supportive housing units. Jamie Nichols clarifies the most recent report shows 104 vacant with no referral, 523 with referral in process, and 376 offline—typically for repairs or sealed units after a tenant death.
chapters.title: CM Restler on supportive housing vacancy data clarification

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Chair Lee and Chair Caban.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, I've been giving Chair Lee shout-outs at every one of these hearings for her endurance, but I feel like Brian doesn't get enough attention and love.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I want to thank Brian for being here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: here at every single one of these hearings too.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's been a marathon.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, Commissioner already heard my first question, but I'm gonna repeat it again.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'll do it briefly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 465 vacant supportive housing units without a referral process that are under DOHMH purview, according to the most recent report that was provided to our office earlier this year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Recognizing that OMH is doing a far worse job, I'll just say that, you're not the worst actor out there, but it's still too many vacant units and it's unacceptable.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So what are, can you give us a breakdown of why these units are vacant without a referral in process?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do they need significant repairs?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What are the investments that we can make that we'll see more of those units activated over the course of FY27?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Assistant Commissioner Jamie Nichols.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so we've got over 13,300 units of permanent affordable supportive housing in our contract portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The last quarterly report from Local 135 showed that we had 104 of those units were vacant with no referral.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So that's a bit lower than the number you cited.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: 523 are vacant with a referral in process.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That means a person has seen the apartment and said, yes, I want to move in, and they're in the process of getting tax credit documentation and the move-in, right, which takes a little bit of time.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So I think our vacancy rate is pretty good.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: There's some offline units, 376 as of that last quarter.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: They report are offline.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Typically, there's two main drivers of that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: One is serious repairs are needed, so those are being done.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The second is that the prior tenant deceased in the unit and it is sealed.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That's a really challenging issue.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're really glad the mayor's housing plan identified a number of initiatives to improve housing and reduce vacancies, and they're really going to be helping us focus on the sealed unit issue.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, so of the 370-odd that are offline, there's a resource need on the repairs to see those units brought back online?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have an estimate of what that would cost to see those units brought back into your portfolio and activated?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I don't have an estimate at this point.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That's changing all the time, right?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: New units, this is every day the numbers are a little bit different.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So a unit that's being repaired today will be available for move-in tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Those are not long-term same units vacant.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The providers do have some money in their budgets for repairs, and they do that, and the landlords as well will pay for those repairs.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So that's not a budget number that's visible to us.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, I'm looking at the last report that I'm seeing, which is listing 465 as either offline plus no referral.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I do want to dig into this data.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We'll follow up with you directly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So we're concerned about just making sure we have the correct

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: understanding of where we are.

chapters.summary: Restler expresses frustration with DOHMH's limited assistance for constituents with severe mental health issues, citing a case of an individual screaming in a park for two years. Martin and Nichols acknowledge the frustration, noting HIPAA limits on sharing information but pledging to problem-solve with OCS.
chapters.title: CM Restler on constituent case management and DOHMH responsiveness

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And then I just want to take a moment to also ask about

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: our challenges in coordinating with the Department of Health and Mental Hygiene regarding constituents with severe mental health issues.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There is, when we reach out, there is, I would describe it as very limited assistance is provided.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're told to call for a mobile crisis team, which maybe tries to visit once, then there's no follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The Department of Health will not engage with us or provide any updates or solutions that may have been provided to the individual who we're calling about.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The individuals continue to endanger their own safety and

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: safety of members of our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It happens again and again and again.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I just, I don't, at this point, I don't even think it's worth calling the Department of Health.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just, it doesn't help.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It makes no difference.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I'm just trying to figure out how can we partner more effectively.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We have an individual with severe mental health challenges screaming in a park for hours every day.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: For now two years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's been a daily, almost probably five days a week, hours on end, often drunk, screams nasty, threatening things at neighbors,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I just like, what are we, is DOHMH able to help on a situation like that?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Because we haven't gotten any help yet.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me ask just a follow-up question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Is this for individuals who are slotted for supportive housing, or are these individuals?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: These are just people who are struggling with mental illness around my community who are causing serious issues.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I get no help.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, I try.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I call everyone I can in the agency.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It doesn't make a difference.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I get no help.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And this has been the case for years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I just am so frustrated and disappointed that I, it's like, it feels like I have to take the health department off as one of the resources to go to because it doesn't yield to outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It doesn't actually make a difference.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I certainly get no updates because we get no, nothing is shared with me.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's just deeply, deeply frustrating.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And is there any, in that example, is there anything the health department can do to help?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, first of all, I hear you, we hear you, and thank you for flagging this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, moving forward, this is the kind of work that we're trying to, in an interagency way with OCS, really be able to

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: address and so we're eager to try to do some problem solving around this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me see if Jamie do you have anything else you want to add?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm sorry you feel that way.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's frustrating on our side too because there's a lot that we do but we can't

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: share a person's protected health information back with you.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we really appreciate the referrals and the situations that you bring to our attention.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We act on them.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I know that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I see the information.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: But we can't talk about a person's treatment plan with a third party.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I totally understand their HIPAA restrictions, but we don't see any improvement on the ground.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Like, we don't see change.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We don't, and without getting into specifics about somebody's exact treatment plan, I don't need to know that information.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I do want to know we're actively engaging with this person, we've been connecting them to help, we're involved, that we're making a difference, that we're in a sustained way continuing to engage here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: anything so that we can come back to our constituents and say, hey, we're trying, the health department's engaged, we're doing something, and I just get a brick wall and I see no difference in the outcomes, so it just feels like nothing's happening and I can't see the result.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So I don't advocate for these things, I don't even call anymore because I honestly feel like what's the point?

chapters.summary: Restler asks if Martin has met with the OCS deputy mayor. Martin confirms his deputy mayors have met and he corresponds with Commissioner Delaney-Breamsfield. Restler emphasizes the need for coordination between agencies and OCS.
chapters.title: CM Restler on Office of Community Safety coordination

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last up, you just spoke to the coordination with the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Have you met with the deputy mayor?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I've met with my counterpart, with the commissioner, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The commissioner, the deputy mayor, you mean?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: There's a deputy mayor, and then there's a newly appointed commissioner, Aisha Delaney-Bramsfield.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Oh, okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Our deputy mayors have met, so at a level higher than mine, my deputy mayor has met with that deputy mayor, but I correspond with the commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think just after the public safety hearing at the beginning of the week, we wanted to make sure that somebody's talking to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I just want to kind of reiterate, Councilmember Ressler's concerns again, as somebody who actively worked navigating the space between like confidentiality, HIPAA and all those things, I think there is information that could be shared about progress

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: that doesn't violate HIPAA that would be helpful to our offices.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I also, Councilmember Ressler, I wanted to give you a quick opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: If you had any other questions on supportive housing vacancies, you can ask them, because I was going to ask them anyway, if you want to take a second to do that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I guess we'll go over to Councilmember Aldeball then.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Aldebol asks about the reduction of 33 vacant positions in mental health. Anderson confirms these were long-term vacancies in back-office functions, spread across the division with no direct service impact. Martin discusses creative recruitment including social media and academic partnerships.
chapters.title: CM Aldebol on mental health headcount reductions and recruitment

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, and thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I do have a question about headcount, your reduction of 33 vacant positions,

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: and how long have these positions been vacant, and are they tied to specific mental health programs?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: All of the headcount targets that we identified were targets that were not highly

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: necessary, were not necessary for executing the functions that we have across our agency.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so those headcount reductions

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: are not affecting any of our ability to do the work that we do in the Division of Mental Hygiene.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll hand it to Aaron to share more.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, as I've said before, these are always difficult exercises to do when you're trying to find savings, as we, as every other city agency, was asked to do.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: But our guiding principle was really that certainly it not impact services, but also that we look at the longest, you know, vacancies that have been out there the longest, vacancies that do not impact direct services.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And, you know, it's hard to do, but, you know, the...

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: the types of positions we ended up looking at for this were things like more back office functions, right?

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Like a data analyst or an office manager.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So it's spread across.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: There's not a single area where they're sort of clustered, but we looked across the whole agency, including within mental hygiene, across the division.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Were any of the positions like hard to fill positions and what has been your strategy or what is your strategy moving forward to fill

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: positions that have been historically hard to fill or where there's high turnover?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I can speak a little bit to that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're doing many different things to try and get folks through the door and also to retain them once they're part of the health department team.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: A couple of things that we're doing are really targeted interventions on social media, believe it or not, to really get some of these job postings in front of individuals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I, in fact, recently was part of a social media push to help people come into the health department to fill some of the hardest to fill roles.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'll tell you another example.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're currently exploring partnerships with academic institutions, with universities, to be able to provide a sort of a foot in the door for some of these

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: hard-to-fill roles so that a student can come into the health department as an intern and then potentially be brought on as a full-time staffer.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we are...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're trying to be creative about this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We also want to continue to shout it from the rooftops.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're hiring, and we want folks to apply.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And how often do you look at your vacancies and your turnover rates?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: You know, how is that impacting the services that you're providing?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, I think we're continually looking at them is the short answer.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: We have, you know, really re-upped recruitment efforts.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I mean, I think all these creative ways, in addition to the usual ways, you know, partnering with DCAS, hosting career fairs, things like that.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: I think one positive statistic is that I think it was two years ago, starting in 2024, we actually was the first time since the height of the pandemic where we actually saw more people coming to the agency than leaving.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And that trend has continued for the last couple of years.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: So I think we're making headway for sure.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: That's good to hear.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: And we're additionally applying these creative ways.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: See the Instagram campaigns, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think that there's long been sort of a reluctance for young folks to apply for or work in city government.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think we're beginning to see a change.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: People are understanding that city services and city government can work for people and make an impact in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And we're hoping to see that the hiring patterns change in accordance with that.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I keep saying Commissioner Kavanaugh, sorry.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Chair Kavanaugh.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about the interagency working group targeting 5% supportive housing vacancy by end of 2026. Martin describes faster unsealing and reduced documentation burden. Nichols confirms three staff are dedicated and the city is on track to meet the 5% goal.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on supportive housing vacancy reduction working group

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to follow up on some of Councilmember Resto's questions.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The mayor recently released the block by block housing report and it details also some things around

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The administration convening an internal working group with DOHMH and DSS and HPD to improve the application and placement process for supportive housing with the goal of reducing that vacancy, the supportive housing vacancy to 5% by the end of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you tell me what DOHMH's contribution to that working group is?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How many employees are dedicated to this group?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What specifics are being discussed to improve and expedite the application and placement process for supportive housing?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for this question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we are indeed part of this interagency working group with us, with HPD, DSS, and you're exactly right, targeting a 5% vacancy rate by the end of this year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The two things that we're trying to do together across these three agencies are, number one, faster unsealing, and then the second is to try to reduce the documentation burden.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We shouldn't let paperwork stand in the way of somebody getting into a home that is available for them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Commissioner Jamie Nichols to share a little bit more about the working group.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, we'd love to know, you know, what the personnel allocation is.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We've got three staff in the work group.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And do you think that the city is on track to, based on what you're seeing so far, to reduce the supportive housing vacancy rate to that 5% by the end of the year?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yes, I do.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will additional funding be allocated to DOHMH for supportive housing in fiscal years 27 to 28?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And if so, how much?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Obviously, we're talking about getting that vacancy rate down to 5% by the end of the year.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But I'm assuming the goal is to get to that zero vacancy rate.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so what are the plans for beyond 2026?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So 27 to 28.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Are you anticipating extra funding there?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so I don't think the vacancy work is necessarily a funding question.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Otherwise, I'd turn it to Erin here.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I wouldn't answer.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think it's really about the things that the commissioner mentioned, right, on sealing apartments, reducing paperwork burden.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: There's no cost associated with that, no new cost.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We are adding supportive housing units all the time.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We added 700 since last year.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Our MMR target is to get up to 14,000 by next fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So the budget is always growing because we're always adding new units thanks to the open RFP.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We do, I just want to make a point about the vacancy rate.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think a healthy system has vacancies as people are moving out and moving in, right?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Not everybody stays forever.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So the goal is not to get to get.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: to zero.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think the goal is to get, I think we're at a housing level.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Or I guess a better way to say it is the goal is to get anybody who is eligible for and needs supportive housing into a unit.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: And I think that's a development question.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I mean, we also want to get use of vacancies, but we need more apartments.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about evictions in supportive housing. Nichols reports 55 eviction-related discharges last fiscal year out of 12,800 units, with new data collection on filings starting in July. Cabán asks about the agency response process; Nichols explains the eviction prevention guidance issued to providers in April.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on evictions in supportive housing

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I want to talk a little bit about evictions in the supportive housing space.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You guys oversee thousands of these units.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you estimate the number of eviction cases brought against supportive housing tenants in DOHMH-funded units every year?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So let me just say a few words at the top there, and that is that we're focused in this agency on helping to address the challenge of eviction in this city and making sure that any individual who

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: is facing an eviction has the right to counsel, as we are a right to counsel city, as we passed in 2017.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We need more money for that, too.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: That's right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we remain committed to the work of managing this process in a fair way and in a comprehensive way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So let me hand it to Jamie to share a little bit more.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think we're committed to limiting, getting as close to zero as possible for evictions from supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The purpose is to house people, and that's our goal.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We track the discharges that are related to evictions.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We've always tracked that number.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's very low.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: There were 55 last fiscal year out of, you know, at that time, 12,800 units at the end of last fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we'll keep an eye on that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We issued some housing stability and eviction prevention guidance in April of this year, and a part of that was adding some new data elements to our data system to track the filing of cases, which is, I think, what you asked about, which we didn't have a data point about that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we've been training providers on that since April, and the data collection will be

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: In July for the next.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And noted that you're ramping up sort of the data collection.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Would you, so there's the 55 units, you know, estimating per year.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do you have any numbers on how many of, like, say that 55 have been non-payments and how many have been holdovers, for example?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so that's a, we don't have that now.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We will have that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: That is one of the pieces of information we're collecting.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Heard, understood.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This is a process question.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So when you find out that a supportive housing tenant is being evicted on a non-payment case, what's the agency response?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What are the steps?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so I think this is outlined in our eviction prevention guidance, right, which is to service providers around the steps we expect them to take to help tenants to understand is this about a benefit issue, a budgeting issue, a behavioral health issue,

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: what are the matters.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: And so our service providers are contracted to help tenants meet their responsibilities and assert their rights as tenants.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So it depends on the individual situation and what's driving the arrears.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So would it be correct to say just like DOHMH's specific role in terms of the guidance is you help put it together and inform kind of what went into the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Obviously, you have, you know, housing lawyers and things also contributing to the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Does that sound?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: More or less accurate.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So our guidance is to the service providers.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're not, there's housing court and we're not.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, but I mean, obviously the guidance includes housing, the basic housing and tenant law in it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm just wondering if like, what's DOHMH's contribution to the guidance?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Is that about sort of the service provisions and what can help somebody get back on track if they're in arrears?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like what's the involvement?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we wrote the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's our guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: It's 100% us and the guidance.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I could have asked that a lot more simply.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We've been doing a lot of these hearings, but thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you for bearing with me.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about the $2M relay program cut eliminating phones and gift cards. Martin and Dr. Walton clarify the cut was to an enhancement that hadn't started—existing gift cards and follow-up practices continue. Cabán argues phones are critical for engagement and likely cheaper than sending staff to find people.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on relay program phone and incentive cuts

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to move to the relay program.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The executive plan would include a baseline reduction of $2 million starting in FY26 for the relay program to eliminate spending on phones and monetary incentives for participation like gift cards and things.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm going to...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm concerned about this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to know what the rationale is choosing this area for savings and why this is a benefit that's being cut off.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm going to give an example again.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I come from the public defense world, so this is the easiest example.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: When they provided clients with cell phones, the warrant rates dropped exponentially by just simply sending a text message to our clients about when they had to come back to court.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like this is really, I think, a really, really critical thing.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You know, cell phones, they keep people engaged.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They allow service providers to be proactively connected with clients.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering what the rationale was for that $2 million.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Cut.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Chair Kavanaugh.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: First of all, let me just say a few words about the Relay Program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This is a program that is a wonder, really.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Myself as an ER physician, having a patient who wakes up from a non-fatal overdose, having a peer there to help them track the course to recovery is just an incredible intervention.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We've been able to contract with over a dozen hospitals and emergency departments to expand this program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The savings that you're seeing is not a cut to the staff or any of the people that are involved.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we thought it was important to make sure that, you know, in order to execute this program, that no staff that provide the services were part of that savings.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But there are tools that are critical.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like, I understand that you're not cutting any staff, but it is eliminating spending on, like I said, phones or other things that have been shown to sort of help with participation and continuity of care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So just, again, hoping for the rationale behind the decision to cut back on that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Maybe I can kick it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Rebecca Lin-Moan.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Sure, yes.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: The cut was to an enhancement to existing.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: We certainly do do gift cards.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: I can, like, we have an amazing storage system that I've gone through in our building with how to find it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: So we certainly absolutely support it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: And so we already do do gift cards.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: We all do, we do work with people on their phone and how to follow up with them and make sure that we do have ways to connect with them, talk to you if they're already talking to like homeless services, how do we engage their case manager there.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: So this was just something that was supposed to start.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: We hadn't yet.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So do you currently spend on phones for participants in the relay program?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: We don't have phones, but we do do the gift card to enhance people being able to follow.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So basically.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: hear you 100% on the evidence.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We haven't, we're not taking away the phones.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We would start the phone program, but we chose to not start it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think we should.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think we should be doing it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And probably, you know.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Giving phones to people is also probably cheaper than having more people to send out to go find them, not quite knowing where to locate them, for example.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I just, yeah, I'm a little concerned about that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But what is...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What's the contracted budget for the relay program?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think that was mentioned, but how many contracts does it fund, and who are the service providers under those contracts?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have a total of 13 emergency departments that we're currently contracting with.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We can get you the full list in a follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about JISH budget and units. Martin reports $10.4M in FY26 and $8.3M in FY27, with 120 operational units across Bronx, Brooklyn, and Manhattan. The goal is 350 units; Nichols explains the amended RFP raised rates, resulting in fewer units overall but higher per-unit funding.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on JISH budget and unit expansion

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to move over to Jish.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The total budget for JISH for FY26 and FY27?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: FY26 total budget is $10.4 million and FY27 is $8.3 million.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, this is an incredibly important program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As you know, Chair, being able to provide this service for someone who's recently incarcerated could literally mean the difference between life and death.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So it's a program that we take very seriously.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: How many GISH units are currently in operation, and do you have the breakdown by borough?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We currently have 120 operational now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it over to Jamie Nichols to share more.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, those 120 units are spread across the Bronx, Brooklyn, and Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: They're all scattered site units, so the exact location may change a little bit.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So I don't have the split between each borough.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We'd love to know what the split is between each borough.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then, you know, the goal that y'all have stated is to reach 350 units eventually.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So what's the timeline of reaching that, do you think?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And how much money do you need to reach it?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Jamie.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yeah, so just this past February, we amended the open RFP to add additional funding.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We're excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The proposals are coming in.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: We opened up the RFP for...

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Scattered as well as congregate.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we won't know the timeline for construction until we've reviewed the proposals, made the awards, and it moves further along in the finance.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: A while from now.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do the contract savings in the exec plan include any GISH contracts?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That you're aware of?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Well, hand it to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Not to my knowledge.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Aaron.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: The question was, are there any cuts to contracts?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, like, you know, there's a bunch of contract savings in the executive budget, and just wondering if any of those include any JISH contracts.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Oh, no, JISH is not a part of our savings.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Just give me a moment here.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I just want to see if I have anything left on this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Oh, so the, you mentioned the GIST RFP, which excited that it was reissued.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about the JISH RFP for 190 units and Just Home project funding. Nichols confirms no funds were redirected—all available funds went to raising rates for existing and new units. Higher rates per unit explain the reduced unit count. No OCS role is planned for JISH.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on JISH RFP and Just Home project funding

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The RFP is for that 190 units.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Some of that will be for the Just Home project, right?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like I think it's 58 of the 83 are for the Just Home.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That's still...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, the math, I guess I'm a little bit confused about the math.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: If we've got that, we understand that some of the just funding will be used for 58 of the 83 units that are part of just home.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That would still seem to leave funding available for 322 units, which is 132 units more than the RFP included.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So can you clarify why the unit count was reduced and was any of the portion of the originally allocated just funding redirected for other purposes?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And a two, Jamie, for more.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: No funds were redirected.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The health department put all of the available funds into raising the rates for the existing 120 units, as well as any new units awarded through the amended RFP.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So the higher rates per unit is the reason why we have fewer units overall.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So that has to do with sort of like the fair market, like that has to do with the market?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Will the Office of Community Safety have any role in the administration of JISH?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That's my last question on that piece.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: At present, there are no plans to have the Office of Community Safety do anything with JISH.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I just want to look here to see.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, I think I am done on the mental health.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer, do you have questions?

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brewer asks about child care center closures and reopening timelines. Martin acknowledges the balance between safety and family disruption, saying they're working to reduce wait times and improve parent communication.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on child care center closure and reopening timelines

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, go for it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I love Ricky Wong.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, my God.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You always call out Ricky Wong.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I like them better than I like everybody else.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We won't take it personally.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You got some fans.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is just on the child care, and I'm late.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I was with DOT on a walking around for the 72nd Street bike lane.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That was lovely.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is, when you close it, you know, for legitimate reasons, a child care center, because of...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Whatever reason, does it depend how long the situation will mandate, I suppose, how long it takes to reopen?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Hopefully they are responsive to the criteria that Department of Health brought to their attention.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But meanwhile, of course, the parents are not able to find other child care, so on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What's a general time frame?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Could it be sped up?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What are some of the hindrances to reopening up a child care center?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Depending, I know, on the issues.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I understand that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you so much for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, it's a balance, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're trying to do two things at once.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: One is keep kids safe.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And if a child care center is out of compliance, we do need to take action.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And the second is not disrupt the community of families that depend on that center.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so this is...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: sometimes more art than science.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we are aware of the fact that sometimes it does take longer than parents would like.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So we're going to work hard on trying to, again, balance those two, you know, needs and get that wait time to be as short as possible.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, that would be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I think communication with the parents is also helpful.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That would perhaps be improved.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm doing it, but I think it would be good for DOH to do it also.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I hear you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You got Ricky, so you got no problems.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Narcisse as well as Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think for round two, quickly, we're going to go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember, did you have a question?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think for round two, quickly, we're going to go with Councilmember Wong, followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Wong asks what the Office of Community Safety has done since its March 19th launch with $270M. Martin defers to OCS on specifics but notes the Be Heard program will be operated by that office. Wong criticizes the lack of a defined operational model.
chapters.title: CM Wong on Office of Community Safety operations and spending

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Regarding the Office of Community Safety, okay, the executive plan includes a baseline transfer of 3.2 million city funding, 17 budgeted positions starting in fiscal 28 from DOHMH to OCS.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: That's clear.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: But my question is,

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The Office of Community Safety launched on March 19th with 260 million and two staffers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And the FY27 executive plan brings that to $270 million.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: So my question is, what has the Office of Community Safety actually done since March 19th?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you answer that?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you very much for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're going to defer questions on the Office of Community Safety with regard to what they've been doing in actions to that office.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: What I can tell you is, from our standpoint, we've already begun doing the work of talking with them and figuring out how to synergize our efforts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we know that they're hard at work trying to figure this out behind the scenes.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, what programs are operating under it today that were not operating before?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And how is it like different from Thrive NYC?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: You know, like so much money was spent and then there's very little got accomplished.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please answer that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Do you mean for Office of Community Mental Health or Office of Community Safety?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yes, Community Safety, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Okay, got it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: You know, the details and sort of the programming, I'm going to have to leave to that office and leadership there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: However, we do know a couple of very important things.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The Be Heard program will be completely, you know, operated by that office, and we look forward to working with the leadership of that office to make sure that program is as successful as it can be.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Yeah, well, I'm not opposed to the concept, but $270 million with no defined operational model is not a mental health program.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: It's just a press release.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Ressler.

chapters.summary: Restler revisits supportive housing vacancies with updated April 30th data showing 486 units offline or with no referral. He presses on repair costs for 381 offline units; Nichols says costs are borne by landlords and providers, not visible to DOHMH. Restler pushes for solutions to bring units online.
chapters.title: CM Restler on offline supportive housing units and repair costs

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great, thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I want to apologize, I was looking at the report from November, and in fact the report, the most recent report is from April 30th, so just to go through it, in November there was a combined 465 units that were offline

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: or online with no referral in process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: As of April 30th, there's 486 units that are either offline, supportive housing units under DOHMH's control, that are either offline or with no referral in process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So let's try again on those.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So for the 381 that are currently offline, do you have an average cost of repair to bring that unit back online?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I recognize it's a fluid dynamic.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I recognize that things are changing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But just so that we could try to ballpark some resources to invest to help bring those units online, because I think we all want to see every one of those units online, right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Jamie.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What would, do you have a ballpark estimate per unit for the renovation costs?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I don't have that information.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Can you provide that to us?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: The repair, the majority, I don't know if I can, the majority of repair costs would be borne by the landlord.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Our relationship is with the service provider.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Sometimes those are the same entity, but oftentimes they're not.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So we're not seeing those costs.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Those are low.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Those are for scatter site units or congregate.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The landlord and the provider are the same, right?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Not always.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So you don't have a contractual model through the provider to be able to do repairs or upgrades when a unit's offline?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: There's a small amount of money in our contracts with the service providers to address minor repairs.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: Could amendments be made if there were resources?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: My guess is that these ones that are offline have much more significant repairs, and that's why they're offline.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: So routine painting and stuff, that's covered in our service contract.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Could amendments be made to those contracts to put additional resources in to fix up the units, or are we just stuck with a few hundred?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Maybe.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I mean, I think this is a, yeah, how can we get...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: All right, maybe ask the question, how do you want to fix up the offline units?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What's your proposal to do it?

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think that's less of a health question.

utterances.speaker: Jamie Nichols
utterances.text: I think we'd really want to talk to the construction people on that.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But these are units within your control that are empty, right, and offline, and that we desperately need people living in.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You know, from April 30th to November 30th, just a six-month period.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But in that period, the number of offline...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: units didn't change from 387 to 381.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We don't want to just be getting the next report in six months that there's 379 offline units and that we haven't done anything either.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So any ideas that you can share with us at this hearing for resources where resources could be brought to bear to bring those units back online?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: So I think, Councilmember, we're eager to do some problem solving with you on this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Clearly, there are tools on the table for us to do this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so, you know, we'd be looking forward to bringing those tools to bear to solve this problem.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The Progressive Caucus is actively...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Discussing with City Hall and with OMB resources that we want to invest in the budget to bring these units online.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It is, in my opinion, disgraceful that we're funding 1,000 city supportive housing units between DOHMH and HRA, so they have even more than you guys, that are not...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Filled with people when we have thousands of folks sleeping on the streets with serious mental illness who desperately need help we know this is the evidence-based approach the cost-effective approach to making a difference in people's lives we want to put resources in this budget that activates every one of these units so that

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: at the next budget hearing a year from now when we're doing this all over again we're talking about how we got that number dramatically down and so

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: there's two weeks left three weeks three weeks and change left before we're voting on a budget there isn't a lot of time so if you have suggestions you want to share with us let us know but we're pushing for resources and we'll push for our best ideas unless you want to share any with us

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Back over to Chair Coburn.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks how opioid settlement funds flow to DOHMH. Dr. Walton explains City Hall determines allocations in four-year increments, with every dollar out the door to 40+ organizations across all boroughs. A listening tour is being launched for community input on spending priorities.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on opioid settlement funding allocation process

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to ask a little bit about harm reduction and the opioid settlement funding.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So how is it determined how much opioid settlement funds flow to DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Just to say right at the top, this is a considerable opportunity for us to redress some of the harms that were caused by the opioid manufacturers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I think we see this as a once-in-a-generation opportunity, really.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We have just a small part of the funding that comes in, as you know.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The other agencies are OCME as well as H&H.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we have control and oversight over the portions that we have access to.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so we can defer questions to sort of the high

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: higher level how much is given to which agency to OMB.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Can you talk a little bit about, I mean, I guess specifically for DOHMH, like how much you're eligible to receive and what the timeline is for receiving it and when you anticipate not receiving it anymore, when does the funding stop?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The timeline and yeah let me hand it over to dr rebecca lynn walton thank you yeah um so how it happens is that city hall

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: determines it's in four-year increments and um as we approach the end then we keep talking about more and so i know i was um

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: part of like the early discussions and so they were looking at sort of a 10 to 20 year timeline for all the funds but um

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: also conversations are ongoing about it so i would say so every dollar we've been given just to be clear is out the door into communities for at least 40 organizations have received funding across the full continuum on every borough

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: and and really focusing on both populations and communities hardest hit by

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: the opioid crisis so all of those dollars are out the door and we just posted yesterday our updated report with all of those organizations and then as they ramp up metrics get out as well so the overarching question you asked was how our decisions made

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: and we regularly meet with City Hall and our other partners and community members we're about to launch a listening tour as well that's what we did last time to figure out from community members

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: How they want dollars spent.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: So at all moments, we're really talking about how to do it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Do we need to speed up?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Do we need to slow down?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Efforts to do it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And will any of the funding, will the Office of Community Safety be eligible to receive any of the opioid settlement funding?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And are you guys planning to transfer any opioid settlement funding to the Office of Community Safety?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We currently are not in any discussions about transferring any of those dollars over to the Office of Community Safety.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about transitioning the syringe redemption pilot to permanent. Martin and Aaron confirm baseline funding has been secured. Cabán also asks about SAMHSA guidance eliminating federal funding for fentanyl test strips; Walton says providers are using DOHMH and state opioid settlement funds for test strips.
chapters.title: Chair Cabán on syringe redemption baselining and SAMHSA harm reduction cuts

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And are you considering, I just want to go to now the syringe redemption pilot for a second, are you considering transitioning it from pilot status into a permanent program?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The Syringe Redemption Pilot Program was, from our standpoint, a huge success.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: As you know, over 1.7 million syringes picked up.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're not just doing community safety in that action.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: We're also paying people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: They get up to $10 a day, and the vast majority of those folks are using that money to pay for food and other basic necessities.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And so from our standpoint, this is a huge, huge success.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Aaron to talk about the funding piece.

utterances.speaker: Aaron
utterances.text: Yeah, this is something we're really excited about from this executive budget, which is baseline funding for the syringe redemption program.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And then I'm assuming that there's no information around whether you're considering transferring that.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Pilot or that program to the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So I'll just put it on the record, but as an outstanding question we have about that office.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: My last question has to do with the SAM HSA updated guidance on funding for harm reduction.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So in this past April, the guidance basically eliminated federal funding eligibility for fentanyl and like different test strips, overdose hotlines, other harm reduction supplies that obviously many providers.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Rely on and use.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Have you guys assessed how much federal funding New York City supported providers stand to lose as a result of those changes and does the exec budget include any city funds to backfill those losses and maintain that part of the overdose prevention service?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Thanks for that question.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I'm going to hand it to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Rebecca Lynn Walton on this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Yeah, so this was one way in which people were somewhat protected that they weren't using SAMHSA funding for this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: I know the state has decided to use opioid settlement funds to fund test strips.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: Our providers are using our funding that we're giving them for test strips.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: And so we've been working with our providers so that we can figure out how to help them shift because we don't want anyone to need a test strip and not have access to it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Lynn Walton
utterances.text: We also provide them as part of our naloxone kits as well.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And thank you, Chair.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Brooks-Powers asks about mental health services for formerly incarcerated individuals. Dr. Petit describes the Bureau of Justice Impacted Populations, health justice network, and co-response teams pairing clinicians with police officers for high-acuity calls.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers on mental health services for formerly incarcerated

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we've also been joined by Council Members Brooks-Powers and Gutierrez, and Council Member Brooks-Powers has questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, and thank you for the testimony.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: How does DOHMH target mental health services for formerly incarcerated individuals?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: In particular, what strategies has the agency found to be effective in reducing recidivism?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And how is the agency prioritizing these strategies and working with the Department of Corrections, CHS, and other agencies to ensure we are able to reduce the incarcerated population in light of the closure of Rikers Island?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And, you know, it's incredibly important that we get this right for this impacted population.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: From our standpoint, it's not only important to treat these individuals with dignity, but it's also important to provide these resources to break the cycle of reincarceration.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And recidivism.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it over to Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Petit to share a little bit more on this.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So certainly this is a critically important population that we want to make sure that we're offering the full array of services across the continuum.
