utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So within the division, there is a bureau specifically focused on initiatives that are aimed at addressing some of these concerns through a health justice network and a lot of interagency collaboration.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We do actually also have what I consider a really remarkable program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's a co-response team, which is pairing clinicians with police officers to be able to address some of the higher acuity calls that come in and make sure that when there is

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: an individual who's in crisis who might have justice involvement or level of...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: violence or agitation that we're addressing in a clinically specific manner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's been incredibly successful in terms of really making sure that folks aren't going back into jail.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And like those, there are multiple programs that we either support or are part of that really are addressing some of the issues around diversion and reentry into the community.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about the $10M reduction in JISH funding and the February RFP for new supportive housing. Martin explains the reduction reflects anticipated underspending, not service cuts. Nichols describes the amended RFP raising rates and adding congregate options.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers on JISH funding reduction and RFP status

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And two final questions.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: One, this executive plan includes a reduction of $10 million into School 26 for supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can the department explain the rationale for this reduction and what impact it will have on the

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: or any upcoming supportive housing projects.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And in February of this year, the department released an updated RFP to fund new supportive housing for people with behavioral health needs who have histories of incarceration and homelessness.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can you provide an update on the status of this RFP and when we can expect to see the expansion of justice-involved supportive housing in our city?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you for that question, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: The $10 million reflects basically a one-time...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Savings from anticipated underspending.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This isn't reduction in any services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: This isn't taking away units.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: It's not taking down housing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: Let me hand it to Assistant Commissioner Jamie Nichols.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Talk about that RFP in particular.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We released an amendment to the Justice Involved Supportive Housing RFP in February that raised the rates that we paid per unit and added a congregate option as well as scattered site.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So those proposals are rolling in and will be reviewed depending on what the proposals are and what's awarded.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That will determine the timeline for those units to become available.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So scattered site becomes available sooner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You're just leasing on the common market, but congregate units take time to construct, and we don't yet know those construction timelines yet.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think that is it for this portion for DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So we thank you so much, Commissioner, and it's great to see all of your team members here and Deputy Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we'll send follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so we are going to roll right into H&H.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're a little bit behind.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So hang tight, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We'll start in a few minutes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: And

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: I know you're waiting.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Alistair Martin
utterances.text: More boots here.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee opens the final hearing of the day for NYC Health + Hospitals, joined by Chair Narcisse. She outlines the $1.75B H&H budget, noting increases for Medicaid cash flow, federal reimbursement, COVID-19 costs, correctional health, and savings measures. Headcount was 43,580.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opening remarks on FY27 H&H budget

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome to the final FY27 budget hearing for today, focusing on New York City health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and I'm proud to be joined by my colleague, Councilmember Mercedes Narcisse, who is the chair of the Committee on Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember, okay, they're in and out, I promise.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmembers Wong, Brewer, Ressler, Aldebal, Brooks-Powers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think that's it for now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So welcome, President, CEO, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Ketz, and your team, and thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive plan for FY27 to 2030 with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: proposed FY27 budget of $1.75 billion represents.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 1.5% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan, and this is an increase of $7.1 million or 0.4% from the $1.74 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, mostly involving a Medicaid initiative adjustment to support the system's cash flow, federal reimbursement,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: funding for costs incurred during COVID-19 pandemic, increased correctional health services expenses, and an array of new savings measures.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of FY26 quarter three, health and hospitals headcount was 43,580.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in the council's preliminary budget response, we called on the mayor to add $3.6 million to H&H to invest in the mental health continuum.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we also raised concerns regarding Be Heard restructuring, Medicaid restrictions, and state-directed plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The state's directive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Although our request was not included in the plan, I look forward to hearing from the director CEO today about your commitment to strengthening H&H's mental health services,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: addressing the challenges posed by Be Heard restructuring and Medicaid restrictions, and ensuring these programs remain a reliable source for resource for New Yorkers who rely on them.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Councilmember and Chair Narcisse for her opening statement.

chapters.summary: Chair Narcisse delivers her opening statement, noting the $1.75B H&H budget and $2.9B capital plan. She outlines focus areas including Bridge to Home, correctional health, medical malpractice, the Maimonides merger, maternal health, headcount, Elmhurst ER renovation, and B-Heard.
chapters.title: Chair Narcisse opening remarks on H&H budget priorities

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And thank you for being here, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We're happy to have you here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I am Council Member Mercedes Narcisse, Chair of the Committee on Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you for attending today's hearing on the City's Fiscal 2027 Executive Budget for New York City Health and Hospitals Corporation, or HNH.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I would like to thank Chair Lee to holding this executive budget hearing with me.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And of course, thank you to all the leadership and for coming here to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: H&H proposed operating budget for fiscal 2027 executive budget totals $1.75 billion and is a $7.1 million higher than the fiscal 2027 preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The main changes include an increase of $32.6 million to support correctional health services expenses,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: saving of $25.8 million to reduce reliance of temporary staffing, reducing overtime and improving revenue collections and contract rebates.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And lastly, a reduction of $4.8 million for Beard transfer of funding to FDNY.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We will also review H&H Executive Capital Commitment Plan of $2.9 billion, which includes funding for the Correctional Health Outpost Unit and critical health care expansion on the RAC.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Peninsula.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: My top line topics for questions today hearing will focus on bridge to home and show, correctional health services, medical malpractice defense contracts, Mamanis health

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: merger, H&H maternal health services, H&H headcount changes,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: and the Elmhurst Hospital Emergency Renovation Capital Project, and be heard.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Before we begin, I would like to thank the financial analysts, Aman Mahadevan, Assistant Director Florentine Carberry, and Deputy Director Isha White for preparing this hearing, as well as my staff committee.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Ria Ogazarara, and of course Manu, but for their support.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Finally, I would also like to thank my staff, Frank Shea, Jushina Jarlott, and Courtney Lee, and all my staff for keeping doing the work in the district office.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I will now turn it over back to my colleague, Shirley.

chapters.summary: Chair Lee announces the June 10th public testimony hearing and welcomes the Public Advocate. Committee counsel Brian Sarfo swears in CEO Dr. Mitch Katz, Senior Vice President Olberg, and Senior Vice President Dr. Yang.
chapters.title: Public testimony announcement and swearing-in of H&H witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I've been announcing this at every hearing, which is that we're doing one full long day, it should be fun, of public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th, starting at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So please make sure to sign up Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30 for our public testimony session.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by our public advocate, who is very appropriately dressed for tonight's game.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Appropriately.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So while we're trying to do that, let me just make sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hold on one second, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, you're good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I will hand it off to our awesome committee counsel, Brian Sarfo, to administer the oath for witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: CEO, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Senior Vice President Olberg.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Senior Vice President Dr.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Yang.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Dr. Katz testifies on H&H's strong financial position with $784M cash balance, 22 days cash on hand, and $134M positive operating margin. He highlights $60M higher patient care revenue, $472M in financial initiatives, 10,800 nurses, and reduced registry usage. He warns of HR1 headwinds and operating losses in outer years.
chapters.title: Dr. Katz opening testimony on H&H financial performance

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Narcisse, Chair Lee, and members of the Committee on Hospitals and Finance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Mitch Katz.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm a primary care doctor, and I'm the proud president and CEO of New York City Health and Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I have my great CFO here, John Ulberg, and Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yang, who does an amazing job for correctional health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Our executive plan builds on the January plan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm happy to say we are doing very well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're going to close the fiscal year with the closing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: cash balance of $784 million, or 22 days of cash on hand, and a positive operating margin of $134 million, all in line with our historic performance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Some financial good news, our

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: patient care revenue, which comes strictly from insurance, $60 million higher than in the same period in fiscal 25.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Our financial initiatives remain on track to achieve $472 million of gain.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Pump all that money into hiring nurses, which I know our chair is a huge supporter of.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The nurses keep our patients safe and take care of them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're now up to 10,800 full and part-time nurses, and we've reduced our use of registry really to only what it's meant to be when someone has a

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: maternity leave, a paternity leave, a disability leave, and you're holding their job, but not using temporary as a staffing method.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're grateful that the executive budget gave us funding for correctional health and maternal morbidity and mortality.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: A council knows that there are headwinds ahead, that HR1, it's a very mean...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: spirited piece of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm sure you discussed the parts of it with the DOHMH and DSS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're all involved in different ways that people are going to be pushed off

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: services, whether it's for food, whether it's for health insurance, and it certainly will affect our bottom line because we will still take care of them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Whether or not we get paid, that's not going to change whether or not we are going to take care of them, which explains why we do show operating losses in fiscal 28 and 30.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm an optimist.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I hope that there will be changes after the next election.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But we are going to make sure that we do everything possible to maintain the care to our patients, regardless of whether or not they have health insurance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I look forward to your suggestions, your comments, your questions, and appreciate the amazing support that we've enjoyed from the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $14.1M savings from reducing temporary staffing. Katz confirms no service decrease, as successful hiring has eliminated temporary lines. Lee asks about physician overtime; Katz says successful physician hiring has decreased overtime dramatically.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on H&H savings and temporary staffing reductions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we've also been joined by Councilmember Areola on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'm gonna try to zip through my questions and then pass it off to Chair Narcisse.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In terms of the executive plan and the savings on the executive plan, it included $14.1 million starting in FY26 and baseline to reach $27 million by FY28.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we were told that these savings would be achieved by reducing reliance on temporary staffing, which you just mentioned, reducing overtime.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How has this maybe impacted or hopefully not impact the reliance on temporary staffing as part of the savings exercise?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm happy to say that it has not affected us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: These were cuts that we could do without, in part because we've been so successful now in hiring, we have been able to eliminate the temporary lines.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so there's no decrease in service associated with that decrease in $14 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as we have been saying on the council, definitely great to hear that services are not going to be impacted because obviously that's not what we want to see.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And were H&H medical staff part of the discussions on reducing overtime?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We always work to try to make sure they are too.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Physician overtime.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: is a symptom of not being able to hire enough full-time physicians.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we've had a very successful year now of hiring physicians.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so the amount of overtime we need to use has decreased dramatically.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about revenue collection improvements. Katz explains the eight-year journey of learning to bill insurance effectively, improving documentation, prior authorization, and contract negotiations, resulting in annual increases above inflation.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on H&H revenue collection and insurance billing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of the revenue collection, because I know it's probably a combination of the insurance reimbursements along with miscellaneous revenue, philanthropy grants, and that such combination.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have the revenue collection improvements, if you could break down, if it's a combination of those or if it's, you know, how it breaks down in terms of those categories?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The revenue improvements has really been starting since eight years ago when we sort of first began this journey of learning how to bill insurance, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're not interested in billing people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're interested in billing insurance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're not interested in giving for...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: profit insurance a free ride by providing care for free and not billing them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so each year we have gotten better at it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's a complicated issue.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It involves things like making sure the documentation supports the bill, making sure that the prior authorization is done, making sure some of the contracts that I inherited when I came were poorly negotiated.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So they might give an overall amount per visit that was less than the vaccination that you gave during the visit because the overall fee included the vaccination.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So each year we look at the contracts, we improve the contracts, we improve our documentation is much better, and all of those things contribute to year to year

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: our increases are higher than inflation, which is how I view it, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: If it's just 3% more, well, then that would just say I was just getting inflation.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But the fact that each year we've done better than 3% says that we are getting better and better at building insurance.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know that just obviously on a much, much, much, much smaller scale, when I was running our Article 31 clinic, it almost felt like we were constantly trying to justify and get funding and reimbursements from the insurance companies, including Medicaid.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I appreciate your efforts in that area for sure.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about contract savings and in-house versus external services. CFO Olberg explains they frequently renegotiate contracts with vendors, leveraging H&H's nearly $13B enterprise scale, and are targeting $100M in additional savings. AI is being explored for denials and efficiency.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on H&H contract renegotiations and technology

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then speaking of contracts, which contracts were part of the savings exercises?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in general, how often does H&A?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: H look into the contracts for funding efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: John?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, this is also, as Mitch had mentioned, just part of our normal course of business to make sure that the contracts that we have with a wide variety of vendors are giving us a fair price.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we frequently, as the contracts expire, will go back.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that could be a host of supplies and services that are delivered at health and hospitals by outside vendors.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then how often do you look at them, and are there areas of where maybe it would make more sense to bring them in-house versus doing it externally?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we're always looking for the cost benefit of doing something in-house versus hiring it out.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also take advantage of, you know, we're almost a $13 billion enterprise.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think as we learned over the years is that we have a certain amount of leverage in terms of how we purchase, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we take advantage of that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Contracts are different.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Some, when they expire, give us the opportunity to go back and renegotiate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There could be certain situations where we may say we're not satisfied with this price.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it's an ongoing effort, especially with our larger vendors, and we've been pretty successful at that, and we're actually trying to line up.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Perhaps we're looking forward to this year and the following year, because as Mitch mentioned, we have pretty sizable deficits in those out years, but we've targeted a $100 million savings target on top of what we've

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: you know, provided here.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's amazing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, because I've brought this up in other hearings as well, where as a city we have tremendous buying power, and so if we can leverage that in any way, we should definitely look into that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And has technology helped at all with that in terms of streamlining, cutting costs, and those types of things?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Certainly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're always looking for that opportunity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And certainly the world of AI is a whole other new environment for us, but we look at that in terms of ways to maybe more effectively deliver care.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as Mitch had mentioned, with denials, we're starting to explore perhaps using AI technology with that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know on the clinical notes and all of those things, it's helped a lot of folks be more efficient.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So that's great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about federal funding risks and Medicaid eligibility changes. Katz uses a frog-in-boiling-water analogy, describing incremental changes designed to avoid a single moment of alarm. He emphasizes H&H will care for everyone regardless of insurance status and explores AI and care model efficiencies.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on H&H preparation for federal Medicaid cuts

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just in terms of the federal funding risks, obviously, you know, that are set to take effect this summer and fall, what steps are you taking to prepare for some of these changes to Medicaid eligibility?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, so the analogy I always think of with the government changes, and it's why it's so dangerous, it's the true scientific experiment that if you have boiling water and you drop a frog into it, the frog will jump out.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But if you put the frog in the water and you just slowly heat it, the frog will die.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I feel this bill was really created with that in mind, that the changes are very incremental so that you never have

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: have that moment when you say, oh my God, but as they accumulate, right, and now we have the first people, for example, not getting food, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But so far everybody is still insured, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But then there's going to be the people who are falling off and they've arranged for there to be accommodation for the first shift, so they won't all fall off at once.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, the most important thing that we do is to constantly remind people, we're here whatever insurance or no insurance you have.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, that that does not affect your care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we are the only provider who can do full range services for people who do not have insurance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The FQHCs are great, but don't have the specialty services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So, you know, when someone needs the MRI or they need the breast biopsy, they're going to come to us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So beyond that, we just need to think about how, given that there will be less dollars, because more of the people that we care for will be uninsured.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we have to figure out the model of how to do it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You had already asked, John, we do think that in the coming months there are going to be opportunities for AI to help us to do certain administrative tasks more efficiently.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That may help a bit.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're constantly looking to see how we can be, you know, what care models are the most efficient, deliver the greatest benefit.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We will work with you and share any information.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're certainly working hard to re-enroll everybody we can and to help people learn that there are exemptions, including for volunteer work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We welcome, I know, the public health department has a great initiative for volunteering to help other people to get benefits.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're also always trying to get people to work in our skilled nursing facilities where the only skill you need is the willingness to sit next to somebody

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: next to a baseball game and talk to them about it or sit with someone and play cards.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They're always looking for a fourth hand, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that would qualify as, you know, volunteer experience, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There's nothing wrong with work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There's something wrong with taking away people's fundamental health care, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we want to try to figure out as much as possible how we maintain people's benefits.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, definitely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think that was brought up in...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Quite a few of the hearings we've had so far, especially with HRA as well as DOHMH before this, when it comes to making sure that the health benefits and also just in general, like SNAP, food stamps, the resource navigator,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: remain funded.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you have those staff that are going to be impacted by this within the hospital setting or within some of your other program areas?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So far, no.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, nobody is affected yet.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Again, that's this very slow moving thing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, again, even to the end of this year, we're fiscally okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know 22 days of cash on hand is very great.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yeah, it's only as you get into the outer years when you start adding up all the incremental things.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But again, we have reason to hope that there is an election before there are the final cuts and that maybe there is some opportunity to protect the benefits that people need.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of that and the subsidy amount that could potentially increase as the eligibility gets stricter in the long term, do you have any sort of,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you're saying this year it should be okay, but in the out years, have you done calculations of how much extra funding you would need?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, in a sense, the shortfall is the loss.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The question is how much can we make up without harming services?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, so whatever that delta is.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, and if it all happens, we will need to come up with alternative models, but maybe sometimes bad times cause you to have creative thoughts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Come up with new ways, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: city learned a whole new set of things through COVID that we never knew before, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We never used before.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You know, I hope it's not going to come to that, but we will certainly look for other opportunities to figure out how to protect services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That will be our bottom line.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, definitely.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $5M Mental Health Continuum program with DOE and DOHMH. Katz confirms discussions with OMB are ongoing and he feels hopeful, praising the model's teacher mentoring component. There are 16 clinics with no new openings planned.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on Mental Health Continuum school program funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Mental Health Continuum, obviously, we are huge fans of.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it's an interagency partnership between H&H, DOHMH, and DOE to provide mental health care in the schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the program is typically funded for $5 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been pushing every year to see if it could be baseline.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But will the $5 million for the Mental Health Continuum be allocated at adoption?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have you been having these conversations with OMB?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have been talking with them and it is in process.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Like you, I've actually visited them and they are amazing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I particularly like that part of the model is to mentor teachers on how to care for someone in the classroom because most of the time is going to be spent in the classroom, not with the therapist.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the model of saying, yes, we provide therapy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But we also teach the teachers how to take care of kids who are in distress, because that's where most of the time they're going to be in the classroom.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So those discussions are ongoing, and I feel very hopeful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And does the funding for $5 million keep programming at current levels?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Current level.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Current levels, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how many centers have been opened since this initiative began, and will there be any new clinics opening in 2026?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There are no new clinics opening.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Do you know the number?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think it's 16 clinics.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 16, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's right, same, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yep.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $3.3M transfer to OCS and whether other H&H programs will move. Katz says no current plans. On the Hospital-Based Violence Interruption Program, Katz describes programs at all hospitals and a recent all-H&H retreat for peer learning.
chapters.title: Chair Lee on OCS transfers and hospital violence interruption program

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then just really quickly, because this also has come up, a topic of Office of Community Safety with a few of the hearings we've had so far.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The executive plan includes a baseline transfer of NGBV funding of $3.3 million to the Office of Community Safety starting in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is the administration planning to transfer any other H&H programs or services that you know of to OCS?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Not at the current time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And H&H's Hospital-Based Violence Interruption Program, HVIP, seeks to break cycles of violence by providing interruption prevention community engagement services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I assume that it would make sense for the Office of Community Safety to be involved with this program if the program is effective.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is this something that the administration is looking into that you know of?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They have not said anything.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yesterday I was at a really great retreat for all of the programs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's one of our initiatives the last year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: These programs all have a sort of historical background.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There was Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Gorder in Brooklyn at Kings County had started a program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There was a Jacoby program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And what we have now done is we have programs at all of the hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: As we said, you know, you can have different brands, but we all want you to learn from one another.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So let's figure out a strategy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And yesterday was their first all H&H retreat together where they spent the whole day figuring out how they learn from one another.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we're very excited.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We want to respect the traditions, but we also want to figure out what we can learn from each other.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, that's perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I feel like that's the best way to learn sometimes is just from your own peers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what is the total budget for the HVAP program for FY26 and FY27, as well as the total headcount?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I will have to get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Let me pass it off to Chair Narcisse.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about billing staffing increases. Katz confirms they hired up the billing department, then eliminated the contractor once staff were trained. Narcisse praises Katz's leadership turnaround at H&H.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H billing department staffing and praise for Katz

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: One of the interesting things I love about that billing, like, did you actually increase the staffing in the billing department, or you used technology to help you build?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Because without good billing, you can collapse.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We definitely hired up.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We haven't had to hire recently.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We got to a certain level that was appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But when we first learned how to do it using a contractor, and then once we learned how to do it, we said, bye-bye contractor, and we hired all the people to do it, and it's been working very effectively.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah, I like that because, and I have to

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: to give you credit because ever since you took over, we have not been really in depth in H&H.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And if anybody tell you that, that's one of the reasons I appreciate you a lot because I know we've been struggling with H&H in the city of New York and you have shown us a new vision and that's the reason I want to say thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you, I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You're quite welcome.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about essential plan eligibility loss impact. Olberg estimates 50-60,000 H&H patients affected, with disproportionate share hospital funding covering about 50% of uncompensated care costs. Katz emphasizes H&H will continue serving all patients regardless of insurance.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on essential plan eligibility loss and uncompensated care

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Based on your testimony, roughly about 450,000 New Yorkers losing their essential plan eligibility by July 1st.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many are current H&H patients and what is the projected

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: uncompensated care increase.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, it's, you know, we're talking about the, we call them, you know, EP5 group.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's the group of about 425, 450,000 people statewide that are in the process of losing their coverage.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For us, that number is about 50 to 60,000 people.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think in state, New York City-wide is somewhere around 200,000.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So it is pretty devastating.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But we are prepared to still, obviously, still serve those individuals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we will use disproportionate share of funding as the vehicle to pay for their services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We would tell them all.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Continue to come for care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're here for you whether you have insurance or don't have insurance.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Narcisse questions whether 22 days cash on hand is sufficient. Katz explains H&H operates differently from private systems due to city and state support, and that in a worst-case scenario, services might need to be consolidated at fewer sites rather than cut entirely.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H cash on hand and financial cushion

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: 20 days cash on hand in line with historical performance.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But 2022 days appears thin for a system about to absorb a federal shock.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: A healthy system targets a far larger cushion.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What's the floor before it, you know, threatens our operations?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, I think the reason we don't operate the same as people do in the private sector is that we have the advantage of the city and the state.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So how much cash we have on hand has some relationship to how much the state owes us at any one moment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And sometimes how much the feds owe us through the state, how much the city owes us for different things, the ability to move payments.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we're always around, and that's why it's always around that number, 28 days.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Ultimately, if there were markedly less insured people and we were providing the same level of services, that will open a hole.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And then that hole can only be filled either by the city, by the state, or us figuring out more.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: efficient models.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we will never back off on providing services, but we could, in a bad scenario, have to look at, for example, maybe we can't provide every service at every hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That could happen, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Would the service still be available?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Would it be available at every hospital?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Potentially no.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, that's, I'm very firm in all of the government systems that I've run.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You get to spend what your leaders give you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You don't get to spend more than that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That's how it works.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's like our checking accounts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You might want to buy something more, but if you don't have the money, you don't.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We will always prioritize services over administration.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We don't have the high salaries that exist in the private sector.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I think what we would have to do is we'd have to look at

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: the places we provide services and probably decrease it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: If you look at the other systems, they don't do the large systems, whether we're talking NYU, Northwell, Mount Sinai, for example, they don't do OBGYN at every hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, they do GYN at every hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They don't do OB at every hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They have a hospital for birthing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that's where you have to go.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Health and hospitals has a different tradition because our hospitals really grew up around taking care of the neighborhoods.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So Harlem takes care of the world of Harlem, and that's more our tradition.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so all our hospitals do OB, and that's a good thing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Pretty much all our hospitals do a pretty full spectrum of services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think in a scenario where there was less dollars and you were prioritizing services, you'd probably have to provide the services at fewer sites, and people would have to travel for those services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But at least the service would be available.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It wouldn't be cut.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You're talking about enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I still, I know we're going to have some complex moment to get people re-enrolled because the fact that we're dealing with population,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: elderlies, people that don't have no support, that live alone.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So as we're talking about enrollment, I know we have strategy to recruit volunteers and stuff.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I pray God since New York City is a great place, despite everything going over our head.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I'm hoping that a lot of people will be recruiting to help address that enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And you said you're going to have kind of hub-like to do that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So thank you in advance for doing that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Great news that we have hired 200 nurses.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know that brings me joy, right?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm very happy.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about projected operating losses in FY28-30 from HR1. Olberg notes the state budget added over $1B for hospitals, and the five-year operating deficit is $350M. The average commercial rate enhancement generates about $2B, serving as the first line of defense.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H operating losses and state budget impact

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We forecast operating losses in...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: FY28, FY29, and FY30 from the HR1 ramp up.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How optimistic are we that the staffing increase won't get squeezed to make ends meet at the end of the day?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: When I look at our system, I'm not cutting nurses because we're structured so that five medical surgical patients equals one nurse.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that is the appropriate model.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't think there's any value in running hospitals that are understaffed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So you have to maintain the basic staff levels.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Again, that's why I was going to, if one had to do something, it would probably be to look at the 11 hospitals and say, what is each borough going to have?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And maybe each borough is not going to have every service at every hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I don't want to ever run hospitals with too few nurses or doctors.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't see any valor in that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That will only lead to bad care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't want to ever provide bad care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I would rather say something is not available than do something poorly.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz, your testimony knows that you are still evaluating the impact of the newly enacted state budget.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And that you anticipate operating losses in out years from HR1.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Can you walk the committee through the challenges you see ahead, both from the state budget and the federal cuts, and how H&H is planning to meet them?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think I did the planning to meet them, but John can do a better job than I on the cuts themselves.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think first on the state budget, right, we think for health and hospitals it should be very favorable.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're still trying to understand the details.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But for hospitals in general, the governor and the legislature added over a billion dollars.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that was $600 million for hospitals, $100 million for outpatient, and then there was a quality pool we're still trying to better understand.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The value of those additional resources are not yet reflected in our plan, right, because we didn't have enough time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The budget was late.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that should improve our out-year situation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, as Mitch had said, you know, we're always forecasting years ahead.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The five-year operating deficit is $350 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will need to attack that deficit, and we've seen to have, you know, good success, you know, thus far in that approach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was a year ago when we were here together, and HR1 had just...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, been released.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And our plan today is about $500 million stronger than it was then.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it's because we try to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: find opportunity in the midst of the turmoil.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we've managed to do that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You've heard us talk about the average commercial rate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's a state direct, it's a rate enhancement.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we take advantage of that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that's benefited us, you know, rather significantly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But yeah, we are today trying to figure out solutions for, you know, the fifth year of the plan to avoid those cuts that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Mitch had mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And like I said, again, I trust Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz is going to do the best he can.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I'm comfortable with a time like this to have Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz, you know, doing the CEO of the H&H.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the $5M emergency weather services budget for warming centers. Olberg confirms OMB will cover expenses, and Katz describes the innovative approach of using vans to find and bring people in from the cold. No separate funding for cooling centers, which are part of the city's overall response.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on emergency weather services and warming centers

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: H&H's fiscal 2026 budget for emergency weather services is $5 million.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The full $5 million was allocated in the preliminary plan to retroactively cover H&H emergency warming center services during extreme cold stretches with this past winter.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Will $5 million be H&H's full fiscal 2026 budget for emergency weather services, or do you anticipate additional funding for being added at the adoption?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we're very grateful, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, the warming center, you know, was a significant problem, and we had to come up with a very rapid solution.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the arrangement that we have with OMB when, you know, health and hospitals, you know,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: tries to address the situation is that they will cover those expenses for us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we anticipate in the future, if we're unfortunately met with a similar situation, that they will help us financially.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is the...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does H&H offer services during extreme heat emergencies as well?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If so, how much do you anticipate H&H spending on these services this coming summer?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So yes, we do offer cooling at all of our facilities for people who don't have it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think when it comes to cooling, the city has more resources and we are just one part of the resource.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think in the cold spell, everybody was staying home and we needed the big thing that Health and Hospital was able to do was go and find the people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think that's what changed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And the city has always had warming centers, but people weren't necessarily going to them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We then took vans and went out and found the people and brought them in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I think on the keeping people cool through the summer, the city has an overall response that we're just part of.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You don't have separate funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No separate funding.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, no separate funding.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, the Rackaway trauma, I'm going to leave it for my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's exciting to go through that because you know she's excited to see you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If she does not ask all the questions, I'm going to come back to that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, I have one of the question I want to ask.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the Office of Healthcare Accountability. Katz says H&H provides cost data showing it often provides the same care at a third of the price. H&H has not been involved in NYU Presbyterian-Emblem Health reimbursement negotiations, which are handled by OLR.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H role with Office of Healthcare Accountability

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The Office of Healthcare Accountability, the office of, was established in June 2024 through the Healthcare Accountability and Consumer Protection Act, Local Law 78.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: This is a legislation that Speaker Menin was proud to spearhead while serving as chair of the Committee on Consumer and Workers'Protection.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The office was created to examine health care costs across New York City, improve

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: transparency in health care, pricing and spending, and identify strategies to reduce costs for patients and the health care system overall.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is H&H doing any work with this office?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If so, what role is H&H playing in improving transparency in their course for care?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We provide data to the office on all our

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: costs and that's where you can learn that health and hospitals often provides the same

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: care but for a third of the price because we are a public system that devotes ourselves to services and not to other things.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Giving ongoing reimbursement rate.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You're very good at it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You master the billing.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Giving ongoing reimbursement rate negotiations between New York Presbyterian and Emblem Health.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What role has H&H and the Office of Healthcare Accountability played in assessing the impact of provider reimbursement rate on the city healthcare course?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If that have delays in reaching an agreement affected the city's anticipating health plan saving.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If so, by how much?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have not been involved in those negotiations.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Those are all done by OLR.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, there's many questions, but I'm trying to wrap it up, I mean, as fast as I can.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Some interesting one I had to ask.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Saving in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The executive plan includes saving of 14.1 million, starting in fiscal 2026, and baseline to reach 27 million by fiscal 2028.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We were told that the savings will be achieved by reducing reliance.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You had to.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: My chair just took that one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm going to go to the next.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I was repeating because I was minding my business, I guess.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I was talking.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Did you do the federal one?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I don't hear the federal one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, the federal restriction on Medicaid.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You did my question, Nene.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Let me see.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The federal...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So we have a conflict here.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Apparently somebody was not paying attention.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It was probably me.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Cheer.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Or the finish.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm the one that's not paying attention.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Sorry, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All good.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: All good.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You did this one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You did that one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You did the mental health.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You mixed my questions with yours.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We have a few questions that we are interested.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: A few questions she's interested.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I don't want you to repeat things.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Just give me a second.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, so let's move on because I have some questions in there on the side too.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the $30.9M asylum seeker budget with no FY27 funding. Katz explains the money is closeout costs for hotels and shelters now transitioned to DSS. All employees were absorbed into other jobs with no layoffs.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H asylum seeker budget wind-down

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Asylum seekers.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: H&H has an asylum seeker budget of $30.9 million in fiscal 2026 with no funding in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How is the remaining asylum seeker funding in H&H budget being spent?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the money that is showing in the fiscal 2026.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Fiscal year 26 is all the closeout of the hotels and shelters that we were running for people who were seeking asylum.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And now all of those services have been transitioned to DSS within the existing shelter system because we're no longer receiving the buses.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: As you remember, there were days when 200 people would arrive to the city without any place to be sheltered.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so in order to help our sister agency, we created another system.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But now that other system is no longer needed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So there's no ongoing funding for the program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's all within DSS.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So I'll go to DSS.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And the money that is there was already, that's already spent, that was money that was spent at the beginning of the fiscal year before the programs closed down.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does H&H have any employees who are fully dedicated to asylum seeker operations?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: If so, what will happen to them when H&H fully winds down in asylum seeker operations?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Everybody was absorbed in other jobs.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They were all absorbed.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Nobody lost their job.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Nobody lost their job.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Good news, especially in this climate.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know I love bridge to home, so I'm going to have to tax over to it.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the $20.1M Bridge to Home program with three sites. Katz passionately describes the program's success—high retention, four people placed in permanent housing, zero neighbor complaints due to 24-hour staffing, and monthly injectable medications. The second site opens in Brooklyn in September.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on Bridge to Home program expansion and outcomes

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Follow up on bridge to home.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Funding was added to the bridge to home program in the preliminary budget, bringing the program's budget to $20.1 million in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: This additional funding covers a new site for a total of three sites.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: During the preliminary budget hearing, you stated that one bridge to home site is operational and located in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Have the locations for the other two sites been chosen yet?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And is there a timeline for the opening of the two last sites?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I just want to take a minute to invite everybody.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I know you've been there, but if you have not been to Bridge to Home, I mean, I think in my 25-year public health career, I don't know that I'm prouder of anything more than this Bridge to Home because

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I always felt that sending people out of the acute care psychiatric hospital who were homeless made absolutely no sense.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that all of the good work that the nurses and the doctors had done during that period of time to stabilize the person would all be lost when two days later their medicines would be lost and they'd be in the subway.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so we created this program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's volunteer, but it's a good deal for the people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the participants have been very willing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We've had very high retention rates.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Very few people have left the existing one in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we've now already placed four people in permanent supportive housing, which was another part of the idea that, yes, we all are in favor of supportive housing, but you cannot actually go from the psychiatric

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: ward.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: To supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There are all these steps.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You have to find an apartment, you have to sign a lease, you have to get furniture, there has to be a first month, and you have to be ready to live in that environment on your own.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But we're doing injectables.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: As a nurse chair, you would appreciate we're giving people monthly injections so that they keep their mental health.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We've seen tremendous improvement in people's abilities to have a positive life outlook.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Oh, with that, the city has generously agreed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The second one will be in Brooklyn and will be opened in September.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And the third one, I don't, it will, do you know if it's Queens or Bronx?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Because I know we're going to do in each borough.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, I'm not sure if we know exactly yet.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think we haven't yet chosen whether, the idea is that there'll be one in each borough, but I'm not sure whether number three is going to be Queens or Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It'll probably depend on finding the right site.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We need another positive thing that I'd say for all of us who've lived through this.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The neighbors, they love us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You don't get a single, you haven't heard a single complaint.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They actually have pointed out how the neighborhood is better.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We took down something that people didn't like that was right in front of the building.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But the key thing is it's 24-hour staffing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So you don't have people sort of hanging out on the outside, the kinds of things that are upsetting neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So it's been incredibly smooth.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, normally when we have a program, I get email complaints, you know, noise or zero.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Zero complaints about the Bridge to Home, and I think it's directly related to the fact that there's 24-hour staffing at the program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we are talking to our sister agency, DSS, THS, about are there other shelters for the mentally ill that we could increase the clinical level?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: of services.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
