utterances.text: If we do that, it does make the shelter more expensive, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You can't provide nursing without some added expense.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I think it might actually save the city money because people revolve through these shelters without actually getting better.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And it could be that if we would provide enough structure, the people don't need a hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But they need something more than a case manager seeing them once a month.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They need some structure, they need some activities, they need some nursing, they need someone to hold on to the medicines so they're not lost.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: People can look remarkably different after three months of good nutrition, positive socialization, and medications.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: People who you would have thought, oh, this person could never go to supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You give them three months of those things, they look completely different.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But they don't look different after two weeks in the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Two weeks in a hospital gets you from psychosis to not psychosis.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I am so sold to that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You know that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I love it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And one of the things, we said boroughs, you know.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We have five boroughs, but I'm hearing three.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So when is the plan to expand?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, this is, we hope that the city will continue to be generous and we will continue to have them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are we having the conversation?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Because you sold me in this, the minute I walked in, you know.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So are we going to continue?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We think that there is tremendous support for us to do this.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'll advocate with you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the $13.1M SHOW program with three new units. Katz says they're not yet operational—funding opens with the July budget—and H&H is working with DHS and CBOs on locations. The program successfully reaches people who won't initially come to a hospital clinic.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on SHOW program new units and timeline

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The show program was funded at $13.1 million in the fiscal 2027 preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are the three new show units in operation?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Where are the three new units located or where will be located?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we just got the funding, so they're not yet operational.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The funding is for fiscal 27, so the money opens with the July budget.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So once we do that, we're working closely with DHS and community-based organizations to figure out what is the best location for them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But they have been very successful at reaching people who are not going to necessarily come first time to a hospital

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: clinic, but who might come after we've engaged with them.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about correctional health contracts. Dr. Yang explains the primary affiliate is PAGNI for frontline providers, with two small contracts for dental and emergency medicine. Contracts are evaluated annually, and some have been terminated for performance issues.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on correctional health services contracts and providers

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Correctional health services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The executive plan includes a baseline of $32.2 million in additional funding starting in fiscal 2026 and increasing to nearly $40 million by fiscal 2030.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The funding will support correctional health services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Spending related to affiliated costs and pharmaceutical drugs.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We know how expensive that can be.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many providers does H&H contract with, I mean, with further provision of correctional health services?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How were these providers chosen?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correctional Health Services has one major affiliate, professional affiliate, as do our sister facilities in the system.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And our affiliate, just like some of the other facilities, is PAGNI, the Physician Affiliate Group of New York.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And they hire, we select individuals who are frontline providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: These are doctors, physicians, physician assistants, mental health clinicians, mental health treatment aides, you know, 1199 and doctor's council, primarily because when we came over to health and hospitals,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: a bulk of the staff who were vetted and interviewed and selected from what had been Corizon were already in doctor's council in 1199.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And for those individuals whom we selected to join our new team, we wanted to preserve their benefits and their seniority and their retirement benefits.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that's how we ended up with PAGNI.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are a professional affiliate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's only frontline providers, patient care providers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: All the supervisors and managers are on health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is not a contractual, typical or traditional contractual relationship.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that is our main contractor.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have two other small professional contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: One is an 18-staff dental, carceral dental provider.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They're dentists and dental hygienists.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and oral surgeons, and then we have eight emergency medicine physicians who are in a group, and we contract with them to be on island.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So those are private groups?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What's the name of the group?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The dental is Correctional Dental Associates.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And Urgent Medical is a small PC.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They are from New York City or out of state?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correctional Dental is in New Jersey and New York City.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And urgent medical is in New York City, and I think they might do some work in Westchester, but they're New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We have a lot of doctors in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So anyway, let's go forward with that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We can always explore that deeper, dive into it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What are the length of H&H correctional health services contracts?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How long?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So our contracts, depending on what kind of contracts we're talking about, as part of health and hospitals, we use the health and hospitals contracts as much as possible.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those are basically for laboratory or pharmaceutical and medical supplies like Cardinal Health, and those contracts generally run three to five years depending on the need.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many years?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You have them?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How long usually have the contract, the cycle of the contract?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The system contracts run three to five years.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Three to five years.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And they're renewed at Correctional Health Services annually, re-evaluate the performance of each of our contracts as to whether we're satisfied or not.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You're re-evaluating them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Annually.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: It's not automatically like a year cycle like.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Annually we do this with our senior staff.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To evaluate whether we're getting what we pay for.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How, I mean, the evaluation that you do with your contractors, based on the responses of the staffing, people that are dealing with their actual contractors?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have taken up corrective steps with some contractors.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have terminated contracts.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have performance measures, including turnaround time, quality of responses and results.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I have some colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: There's different things going on.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I would love to ask a question, but my colleagues, they have to run Selvena Brooks-Bower.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I know she came directly.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: She dived into it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, Selvena Brooks-Bower.

chapters.summary: Brooks-Powers asks about the Far Rockaway Trauma Center, noting the 40-minute travel time and $200M estimated capital cost. Katz strongly supports the project, citing the peninsula's geography and comparing coverage favorably to other boroughs. He confirms the $200M estimate is still accurate.
chapters.title: CM Brooks-Powers on Far Rockaway Trauma Center need and cost

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs, and thank you for the testimony today.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: I'm going to just go ahead in the interest of time and ask the questions, and then I can repeat any question I need to.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: But always great to see you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Cass.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Great to see you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And the full H&H and CHS team.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: So first, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Cass, you have been a strong partner in the Far Rockaway Trauma Center efforts and have heard the needs of our community.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Based on previous conversations, we have estimated that a level one or level two trauma center would cost approximately $200 million in capital to construct.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: As far as you are aware, is this still a reasonable cost estimate for this facility?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Next.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: The executive budget includes additional funding in the next few fiscal years for CHS operations and pharmaceutical operations.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Can health and hospitals explain the rationale for these increases and how they will improve services for CHS patients?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And the last two, how does New York City Health and Housing Authority

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Hospitals track and measure trauma cases across the city.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And what is the average travel distance and transport time for trauma patients to reach a level one or a level two trauma center citywide?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And how does that compare with the residents of the Rockaways?

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And I know, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Katz, you had shared in an interview a while back a very...

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Profound statistic in terms of the number of trauma centers in the different boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And if you could just on the record share based on the boroughs where our trauma centers are located and how many they have, I'd appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'll do my best.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Let's just deal with the CHS question because that's very straightforward.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The increase really reflects the increase in volume of the number of detainees on Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So Rikers has been much more crowded than before.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so some of it is just the per person cost of their medical care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And then we're very proud of providing the same level of care at Rikers that we would provide to anyone.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so that means many more injectable drugs that are expensive, but that we believe ultimately provide good care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So it's a combination of more people, more expensive pharmaceuticals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I remember.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Maine with you, a big supporter of the Rockaway Trauma Center.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I feel that the fact that it is a 40-minute trip to the nearest

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: level one or level two trauma center, assuming reasonable traffic, which as we know is not always the correct assumption.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There are, as you know best, there are only two ways off the peninsula and both of them can be very congested.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And it is a very long and thin peninsula, which means that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: added time depending upon where exactly on the peninsula it is.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I think that there is a very strong argument that it is wrong for people to have to wait the 40 minutes for definitive care and that lives are lost at that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: In terms, I can only say that other parts of the city have better coverage.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't have the exact numbers, but I can certainly give a sense.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Staten Island has two trauma centers, and while it's, if you will, a fatter island, it's not nearly as long.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, so in terms of travel distance, it would be significantly shorter.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Manhattan is certainly the place where there are the most trauma centers in a very small area.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: people would have access to a trauma center.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: In most cases in Manhattan, I would say, you know, no more than 10 minutes would be like the furthest within Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Brooklyn has Kings County, Maimonides,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: and NYU Lutheran, I don't know, I may be missing, I feel like I'm missing one.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But again, for the size, much shorter travel times.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That really, when you look at the city as a whole, the Rockaways stand out in terms of the time people would have to travel.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that's why I support it with you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: And in terms of the cost, as far as you're aware?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Oh, yes, it's correct.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes, thank you for reminding me.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes, that is still inaccurate.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I recently verified the number with a group that builds hospitals, and they agreed that $200 million is the capital cost.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Selvena N. Brooks-Powers
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Caban, followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about gender-affirming care for children under 13. Katz commits to continuing care, noting H&H has no volume cap and has accommodated families from ended programs. He promises to look into care coordinator capacity and says H&H has not received a federal subpoena.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on gender-affirming care for youth at H&H

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'd like to start with a couple of questions about gender affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I asked this question earlier, but it's probably more appropriate for you all.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Obviously, we're seeing the federal assault on gender affirming care continue.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: escalated.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, now we're seeing these private hospitals get subpoenas.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So is there a plan for increasing access to gender affirming care within H&H specifically for younger children?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like there's so, I was mentioning, but there are so few places that provide care for children under the age of 13.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So could you speak on that a little bit?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, with you, we remain committed to this population and we are not going to, you know, stop taking care of them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We believe that this is a health issue that is best dealt with by the person, by their family, and definitely not by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have already accommodated families who were in other programs that ended and will continue to do so.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We don't maintain any volume cap, so we will care for as many children as need to be cared for.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I think one thing that has come up is that, you know, we've asked about this before, obviously, because this has been a long-term attack on that particular community.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And we haven't necessarily seen an addition of patients coming to H&H.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But what I will say is that I am hearing from more parents that they're...

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: You know, having a hard time figuring out where to take their children.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so I'm wondering also if there are dedicated referral pathways, care coordinators, expedited appointments.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: at H&H available for patients whose treatment has been interrupted.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I want to emphasize the care coordinator piece because I think that's what parents have at least indicated would be the most helpful because, you know, different stage, different age and different development stages require different kinds of care and not every

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: doctor in every even H&H facility has the ability to provide that care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yeah, we certainly do try to have people come to a place where we have specialized physicians, because as you say,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: not all physicians, even all good physicians, are culturally competent to deal with the issues of transgender children.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I will go back and see more about whether or not we have a sufficient number of care coordinators,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: and we'll certainly prioritize that as things get harder and harder, as other providers stop doing this work.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We want to still stay firm and keep providing it to the children.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: My last question on this piece is NYU Langone has received a federal subpoena.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Mount Sinai has, except they're lying about it.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Has the administration received?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have not.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And thank you.

chapters.summary: Cabán asks about B-Heard eligibility and structure. Katz clarifies H&H trains and hires social workers but doesn't run operations—currently FDNY does. He describes the staffing model problem of using two paramedics per call and supports adding peers to B-Heard teams.
chapters.title: CM Cabán on B-Heard program structure and peer involvement

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Chairs, may I ask just a few questions on Be Heard?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Wait, can we, oh.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Just because it hits the mental health portion, and I had to wait for health and hospitals to ask those.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: On Be Heard,

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Is there a plan, like we saw on the website that there is a drop in eligible calls for FY25.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Do you have a plan to increase eligibility for those calls?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Like as one example, open to removing the limitation of EDPM only.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We're seeing other responders around the city have a much larger like scope of

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: call classifications that are eligible for their alternate responders.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So you have to remember that we, Health and Hospitals, doesn't run Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We are proud to hire and train social workers, and we think our social workers do a great job.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But we don't do the operational part of Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's certainly a model that I believe in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that if you want to de-escalate a situation, you want to send a mental health professional, and therefore I would want to send a mental health professional as often as possible.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: To these situations and I do think that it could be a much larger group, but that requires operational changes across the city.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So, and according to you, so, and there's been confusion about this.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This is Beard moving to health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Is it going into the department?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So it's not going to be.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So at one time there was the proposal to move it to health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And, you know, that was not a negative on anyone.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That was just recognizing that it was challenging for fire to hire enough EMTs and paramedics.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And in the absence of that, there were not very many calls.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But that plan has changed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think now the administration is looking at a variety of options.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think there are a variety of options.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Totally.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that really which department is not really the issue.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The issue is how do you efficiently get people who are not law enforcement frightening.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But are mental health professionals to situations to de-escalate them as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Here's my very last question, because it's not even just mental health professionals, but H&H uses peers in a myriad of programs, correct?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And that's because you find that peers provide something that nobody else can.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: There has been an increase in peers in a lot of different places, H&H, DOHMH.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It really gets celebrated.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: What do you think about peers on Be Heard teams?

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I mean, but people who live with mental illness and are part of those organizations are saying, we want peers on these teams.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think it'd be great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: That's all I've got.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about correctional health at Rikers. Dr. Yang reports a vacancy rate of about 8%, improved from prior years, and describes the outposted unit at Bellevue with 95 people currently housed. Brewer also asks about NYC Care enrollment, now at 130,000—10,000 higher than last year.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on correctional health staffing and NYC Care enrollment

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: On correctional health, I have a question.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: on New York Cares bike crashes, because you know more about it, and then just mental health on the street.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So correctional health, congratulations finally.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I understand there's 96 people.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: When Stanley Richards was here the other day, he said it was 96, which is phenomenal.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's 95 today.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: 95 today.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so that's going well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But then at Rikers or other locations, I don't know, what's the cost, where it's a budget hearing, what else do you need?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's still challenging on Rikers as opposed to at Bellevue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I'm just wondering if you could update us on the cost, update us on any other needs that you might have in terms of correctional health.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I appreciate so much the additional funding, which is going to make correctional health services whole again.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that really covers the increase in cost for pharmaceuticals.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As Dr.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Katz noted, we have more patients and we have sicker patients.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that's really the issue there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it will allow us to make up for the salaries for the people who are good providers and providing the kind of services that we expect them to provide.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The outposted project continues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They are funded.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are working very closely with the Department of Correction under Commissioner Richards.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's wonderful.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And with the State Commission on Correction to finalize the design.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: For the Bronx and the Brooklyn sites.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Really excited about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Fantastic.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The issue to those at Rikers, how much staff is there?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it every day?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: When I'm there, and I'm there pretty often, it never seems like there's a lot of folks in the clinic, but that's just because maybe I'm there at the wrong time.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I got it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But it's not hard to recruit.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get a sense because people get sick, obviously, and I was just trying to understand if that is still an issue or you think you're totally copacetic with correctional health at Rikers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We can always do better, and it is a challenging place to work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have been much more successful in recent years, certainly since COVID, in recruiting and retaining staff.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our vacancy rate is about 8% at this point in time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Part of the reason why we needed to come to you for the budget leveling is because we've basically run out of vacancy money, dollars that were not spent because of vacancies because we've reduced that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Which is a good thing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have staff on.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I live with people who are asylum seekers and they love New York Cares.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I'm not sure they use it very much, but they know about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering, what's the cost?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Are you increasing those who are participating, et cetera?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Just get a sense of it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, remember when NYC Cares was funded, basically it was a single funding which covers our having an 800 number, an additional fund.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: pharmaceuticals, and the infrastructure to run the program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But the program really just takes advantage of health and hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We don't cap the number, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We've always taken care of everybody, regardless of their immigration status.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The difference is there was never an organized system to enable people to know how to do it or to get a primary care appointment in two weeks and have that be the center of it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I'm sure the actual costs exceed the amount of money that the city is giving, but that's okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: These are people we've always wanted to take care of.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So how many people are taking it?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And that's pretty steady?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's about 10,000 higher than it was this time last year.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Congratulations.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about bike crashes and e-bike accidents. Katz, as a manual bicycle rider, blames e-bikes and scooters for increased accidents due to speed, noting many wrist fractures at Bellevue. He emphasizes everyone needs to follow traffic laws.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on bike and e-bike accident trends at H&H hospitals

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: All right, now about this, I have to deal with bike.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Pass and bike.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know I saw the helmet.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Oh, wow.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: All right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Go ahead.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I have to deal with people like bikes and people don't like bikes.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's how it works.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so when you try to build a new...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I would say infrastructure, all hell breaks loose.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: My question is, in the papers, mostly the post, there's a lot of concern upping the number of, I would say, crashes, accidents, or not.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In other words, do you see more at Bellevue, or is it just other?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Hospitals that are racking up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Give me a sense of what you think we are with this industry in terms of...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Accidents are up, but I would personally, as a manual bicycle rider, I blame the e-bikes and these electronic scooters because they just go way faster.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that's what causes the accidents, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's the fact that people are coming at you really quickly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so we've seen a lot more accidents.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: At Bellevue too, or at all your hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: All hospitals indefinitely at Bellevue and a lot of scooters, a lot of wrist fractures.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And, you know, I think the issue of the bike lanes, again, as someone who uses them all the time, is that you're still...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: supposed to follow the law.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Like when the light turns red, you stop.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: A bike lane is not a, you know, welcoming to go through a red light.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so everybody needs to, you know, pay attention.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, sorry, one quick thing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can we take a quick pause because our public advocate has to go to the airport, and then we'll go back to Councilmember Brewer right after.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about B-Heard funding and coordination. Katz says the $6M goes to FDNY, not H&H, and the city hasn't decided on the ultimate operational home. He describes the staffing model as unsustainable and suggests the issue is staffing, not which department runs it.
chapters.title: CM Restler on B-Heard funding flow and operational model

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am sorry, Councilman Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: My wife will be considerably mad if I don't get there, so I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you for giving me an opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I do have to say let's go Knicks.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That's really important.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Wait, one second.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You have a very well-known fear of flying.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What are we talking about here?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: No, I just said I'm picking up my wife.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Oh, okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yes, I made that very clear, yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And she'll be pretty upset.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm going to be late anyway, so she'll probably be upset.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I may have to call you all for some help.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But thank you, Commissioners.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So really quick, I know I had a question about Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think you said you're not prepared to answer questions on Be Heard?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: No, just that we don't do the operations of Be Heard.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So our role is to train the social workers, hire them, but the operations of Be Heard is right now in the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, so the $6 million for BHA,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Going to you?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's going to the fire department?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Fire department.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so I have, you know, I've been concerned about some of the things I've heard.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The commissioner basically said there's not been much conversation done with OCS coordination.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The fire department's kind of said similar.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is it ultimately going to OCS?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do we know?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We don't.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't think that decision has been made.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that there's, I think everybody agrees that the goal is to send Be Heard to more calls.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that's well established.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But how to do that?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Is the city does not seem to have come.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There was a proposal in the fall that it would come to health and hospitals and we would do it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That proposal is no longer part of the city's idea, but I don't think anybody has articulated yet what it's going to be.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, because I know there was a...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: A report that's something that discussed the bottleneck, because NYPD answers are called FDNY handles EMS, but H&H employs the social workers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And so that coordination can be difficult.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It sounds like it's something that could be difficult.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't think anybody objects to us hiring and training the social workers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think everybody's good with that part.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I think there is an issue that right now many of these calls the police are...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Prioritize them and go rapidly to them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: and the fire department because of shortages of paramedics take longer to get there.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so then in general, you're not achieving the goal of getting the mental health professional in the lead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the police are there first.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You probably can't answer this, but I'm going to ask anyway.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What do you think should happen?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't think the issue is the department.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think this could run in a variety of ways.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think the issue is the staffing and that the current model of staffing is not sustainable.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The current model is that two paramedics or EMTs go out with a social worker in a non-transporting ambulance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And then if the person needs transport, they have to send two more EMTs in a different ambulance.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So on a crowded New York City street, you have two ambulances, four EMTs, one social worker.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And meanwhile, we have an EMT paramedic shortage.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So then the calls with medical illness don't get handled as quickly.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Also, these calls take a long time.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You don't de-escalate somebody by tossing them into the back of your ambulance and driving them to the hospital.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I think that the model, whether it's by us, by the fire department, by the office, community safety, has to come up with a staffing model that there are enough.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: of them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right now there are not enough paramedics or EMTs to respond to the number of calls people want.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, I'd like to offline talk about this a lot further.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about Local Law 42 and prolonged placement in the contagious disease unit at Rikers. Dr. Yang explains the CDU is for medical isolation, and while CHS places patients for medical reasons, DOC may place others for non-medical reasons. Restler asks about the outposted unit timeline; Yang confirms 2029.
chapters.title: CM Restler on correctional health and contagious disease unit

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Just really question really quickly for corrections, we met with the Department of Corrections about Local Law 42.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We brought up a Board of Corrections memo in December that talked about prolonged placement.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It was found in a contagious disease unit at West Facility in Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It also showed that CHS repeatedly raised serious concerns regarding the deteriorating mental health of individuals on supervision and did not support continued placement.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: What can be done to support your efforts to avoid these instances and be in compliance with the board rules?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thanks.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Hi.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The communicable disease unit is unique in the New York City system, in the national system, and it's negative pressure rooms for respiratory isolation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: When Correctional Health Services puts a patient in the CDU, it is for medical isolation or quarantine to rule out or to get past a period of infectivity of a communicable disease.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those individuals, even when they are in medical isolation, when they have a diagnosis of a communicable disease, are not...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Restricted in that setting.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: They can still go outside, they can have court.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What they can't do is

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: be with people physically without precautions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: That is for a short period of time, just until they've passed the period of infectivity, or until we've got the lab results back that says they do or they don't have the disease.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's other people who are placed in that area because of the physical layout of the area by the Department of Correction for non-medical reasons.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those are not...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: People who we put there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay, we'll probably follow up offline on that as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Tell your wife I said hi.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I will, I have details, so I'll maybe be protected when I'm late, but we'll see how that goes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Purina Sanchez on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry, Purina.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And continuation with Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So on the bicycles, it's nothing to decide today to know that the accidents and crashes are up and that we have to do something about it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not your problem per se, but since you are a bike rider, as we all are, your input would be helpful.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There is a bill apparently that Councilmember Hudson has to limit speed, but they still go through the red light, so I don't know.

chapters.summary: Brewer raises concerns about people with mental illness on the streets. Katz argues most upsetting behavior stems from drug addiction, not mental illness per se, and advocates for mandatory substance treatment after repeated low-level arrests as an alternative to Rikers or leaving people on the street.
chapters.title: CM Brewer on street mental health and addiction treatment

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Mental health.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my office is swamped with people who see people and are experiencing, I guess, abuse from people who are mentally ill on the street.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so it's the same story.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's nothing new.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But do you think the city has some remedies for this or is it just the same?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Oh, we just have to deal with it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It does seem to be increasing, obviously to the credit of Jessica Tisch, the numbers are down in terms of violent acts, but mental illness seems to be up.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I've seen it, I know I got 10 calls today even, just, you know,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What do you suggest?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Okay, well, this is Mitch's doctor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know, I like Mitch's doctor.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So I believe that a lot of the people that you're getting calls about are heavily addicted.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I believe that some portion is mental illness.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But people with mental illness are generally not violent.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They are generally the victims.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They are not the perpetrators.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There is the rare person whose psychosis says, the devil says I need to hurt you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But that is by far the minority.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The people with schizophrenia typically become withdrawn.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They are not the people who are upsetting to people.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I believe that the vast majority of people who are upsetting are using drugs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They are addicted to the drugs.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They may also have a mental illness.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right, I understand.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But it's the drug use that is affecting their behavior.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I personally, Mitch, the doctor, thinks that they need treatment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that there needs to be, I don't think sending them to Rikers is going to help them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That's going to make things worse.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I think leaving them on the street is also not helping them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think that we, that judges in our current system, lack alternatives.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: True.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So that it's either do you release the person who has just done this crime, but maybe it was not a horrible crime.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Do you send them to Rikers?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I agree, don't send them to Rikers, they'll only learn worse behavior.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But on the other hand, I also don't, someone has multiple incidents of these kinds of crimes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They're cycling.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Good things are not going to happen to them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Bad things are going to happen to them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And to the people in the neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And why can't we as a society say, you know, this is the third time you have been arrested, the first time you were stealing potato chips, the second time you pushed somebody, you know,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: clearly drug use is hurting you and something bad is going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And therefore, we are saying, you, as a judge, you got to go to this substance treatment program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And ideally, you go voluntarily, but if you don't go, you got to go.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But that's how I view it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The law is not consistent with that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The law says, as you know well, that you can only be held against your will if you are in imminent danger of hurting yourself or someone else.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And it doesn't address how addiction kills people's lives and all the people around.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But do you feel there are enough programs, because my friends who are judges feel there aren't necessarily the right program for that person to be able to deal with his or her addiction.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Do you think that there are enough places?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that if we could all agree that some people are not going to go voluntarily.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But that there should be not a first strike, but like the third time you were brought before a judge that you have to go, that that would be a positive step forward.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we do that with mental illness.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So why can't we say on the third time you're arrested for some, again, not a huge, you know, these kind of stealing things, pushing people, getting into a fight.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Why can't we say, you know, this is not turning out well for you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We as a society feel you're not following through on the societal contract and you are going to this program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Will it work all the time?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: No, but at least...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're trying, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think the frustration that you've expressed to me is the same as what my friends say is, well, but you can't even call the police because the police say there's nothing they can do about it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: My daughter rides the subway home from her supermarket job, and she all the time talks about how frightened she feels as a short Asian girl in the subway, and she feels intimidated, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I hate that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is a good offline discussion to continue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, we have Council Members Wong, Aldebal, and Ressler.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Won asks about the Elmhurst Extended Care Unit opened in December 2024. Olberg confirms the ECUs operate at a loss but provide longer-term savings through avoided future admissions. Won asks about FY27 funding and expansion beyond 20 beds; Olberg promises specific numbers.
chapters.title: CM Won on Elmhurst Extended Care Unit funding and expansion

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I want to start by acknowledging something positive.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The extended care unit that opened at Elmhurst in December 2024 is exactly the kind of investment this community needed.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: 20 beds, up to 120-day stays, and 50% patients connecting to permanent housing on discharge.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: That is a real result.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: My question is about sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What is the annual operating cost of the Elmhurst ECU?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is it fully funded in FY27?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And is there any plan to expand capacity beyond 20 beds?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Can you answer that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think currently for the extended care units, we do operate those beds at a loss, right, because we understand how valuable they are.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, again, it's a loss measured by the stay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But there is a return, I think, on the person's care in terms of avoiding future admissions and future costs, and that's why we operate the program at a loss because we think there's a longer-term savings to be had.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can get you those specific numbers, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Please do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Mine are across all the ECUs, but we can certainly provide you the numbers.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Aldebar, followed by Ressler.

chapters.summary: Aldebol raises the issue of out-of-service pediatric X-ray and fluoroscopy rooms at Jacobi Hospital, leaving zero dedicated pediatric radiology rooms. Katz acknowledges equipment is past useful life and promises to follow up on the replacement plan, noting fluoroscopy has been partially replaced by CT scans.
chapters.title: CM Aldebol on Jacobi Hospital pediatric radiology equipment failures

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you, Dr.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Thank you, cheers.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: So I have a question about Jacoby Hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: They're all the dedicated pediatric x-ray and fluoroscopy rooms.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I practiced that all morning today.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Good job.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I could never have done that.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: At Jacoby are completely out of service.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: The primary pediatric x-ray room has been out of order for nearly three years.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And the fluoroscopy unit is now completely offline.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And that's, you know, obviously leaving pediatric patients at both Jacoby and North Central Bronx with zero dedicated pediatric radiology rooms.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And this is obviously a problem.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: We need pediatric specific equipment to adhere to pediatric radiation safety protocols, especially for premature babies.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And our office and our Bronx delegation, we've been advocating for these capital fixes to be prioritized in the budget.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: However, the urgency and effect on patient care for these needs is

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: is very high.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Do you have any insight into why this situation has been allowed to get so critical and what plans are in place to prevent harm to patients in the short term?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And what can be done to make these fixes for Jacoby Hospital a priority for H&H?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So just acknowledge that much of our equipment is past what people would say is the useful life.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We are a system that prioritizes services, and sometimes the equipment doesn't make it to the top of the list because we're so busy trying to take care of everybody.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Our overall capital needs are huge and would swamp the city's budget, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have 11 acute care skilled nursing facilities, many of them quite old buildings with leaking roofs and boilers that need to be fixed and chillers, and we try to do our best

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: to figure out what is the next priority.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Fluoroscopy, thankfully, is not as much of a tool as it was in my generation as a doctor, which I suspect may have been why it didn't get the same level of priority.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But there are certain things you can only do with fluoroscopy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You can't do it with a CT scan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, mostly the CT scans have replaced fluoroscopy, which we can't do.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: which is good for diagnosis, which is a good thing because less radiation to the kid.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But there are certain procedures that can only be done by fluoroscopy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: My understanding is that they were transferring the kids right now until they got a new fluoroscopy.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't know the equipment schedule.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Do you know it, John?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we will get, I'll go back, I'll call Chris Mastromano.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I feel he told me what the plan is, but I don't remember it at this moment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you for raising it.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I can sit down with him and figure that out as well.

chapters.summary: Aldebol asks about the violence interruption program at Jacobi. Katz describes the Guns Down, Life Up program expansion to all hospitals including new programs at Bellevue and Woodhull, with no current plans to transfer to OCS but willingness to coordinate.
chapters.title: CM Aldebol on hospital violence interruption program expansion

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And I have a question about the violence interruption program.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: It's been a phenomenal, successful program.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: I've had the opportunity to meet with dedicated staff at the SUV program at Jacoby Hospital.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: Are there plans to expand these programs?

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: You know, protect the funding for these programs.

utterances.speaker: Shirley Aldebol
utterances.text: And is this program going to go under Office of Community Safety?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So guns down, life up, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we're going to continue to expand them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We've now both expand them at the hospitals, as in the case of Jacoby, but also we're now creating one at Bellevue and one

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There's another new one, Woodhall, we did last year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I'm blocking on.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Maybe South Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I have not heard any intention to take them to a different office of community safety, but we would want to coordinate with them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, that's what they are, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, they're ways of preventing violence using peers and violence prevention specialists.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So, you know, I'm very supportive of working with other people, and that's their tradition anyway.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about the financial impact of 783,000 New Yorkers losing health insurance. Katz says DSH dollars cover about 50% of uncompensated care, with the average commercial rate generating $2B as the first line of defense. Olberg confirms they've factored EP5 losses and 10-13% Medicaid disruption into their model.
chapters.title: CM Restler on H&H financial modeling of health insurance losses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Ressler.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much to the chairs, and congratulations to Chair Lee for making it to your last hearing of an exceedingly long week.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And President Katz, sorry?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last one for the week, not in general.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Next week will be even more fun.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And President Katz, always good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you for your exceptional public service.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think you're now the one person who's been appointed by three mayors in a row, is that right?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is correct.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is a hat you wear unto yourself.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So congratulations on that well-deserved reappointment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have a variety of questions for you, but I'll do Patsy first for a moment.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 2029 is the date that Commissioner Richards gave for when DOC anticipates Woodhull and NCB opening their facilities.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is that consistent with your timeline as well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It currently still is 2029, but we are all looking at ways that

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We could push that up.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we now fully have teams at DOC and DDC, and our partners are pushing ahead as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Everyone is on the same page and pulling the same.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The wind's at our back, finally.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Good.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And if there are obstacles or barriers, you will be sure to let us know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Katz.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So the health department testified earlier that their modeling has us losing, has about 783,000 New Yorkers losing health insurance as a result of H.R.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's mind-boggling to think about one of 11 New Yorkers losing their health care, but that's what we're imminently facing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we know that those folks end up on your doorstep.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have a financial model of kind of uncompensated care that we'll now need to provide to these three-quarters of a million New Yorkers who've just lost their health insurance?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, the only real financial model we have is that we can access disproportionate share hospital dollars, which would provide about 50% of the cost of the uncompensated care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So, I mean, that will be, you know, a major source.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: 50% is better than zero.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And the modeling I've seen is that we're anticipating a $2 billion increase in hospital uncompensated care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So you're saying that that would be a, we'd get a billion in federal funding through DISH and be able to cover half.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But looking at an additional billion.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And expense is rough estimates at this point.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Big picture.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You got it right.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And forgive my ignorance, but when do you think that funding will need to be in H&H's budget?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, again, you know, I do wonder how the mid-year elections will affect this, since that's when you'd start to get some of the biggest cuts.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I still remain hopeful that the worst-case scenario will not happen.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And a lot depends also on how successful the city is in keeping people on insurance, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There's certain people who you can't keep on insurance, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Like the people in the essential care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Between 200 and 250%.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're just going to lose.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're done.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They're done.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But how we recertify.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But the other people, potentially, if you consider that almost everybody could, you know, either be working or be taking care of children or be in school or volunteer.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Or be disabled, if you could imagine getting everybody through those hoops, you could have relatively few people falling off.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But the recertification process every six months is intended to create a bureaucratic nightmare that people will not be able to comply with.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And to that, I would love, do you mind if I keep going with a few more questions?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Fast?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Are you guys rushing?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Quickly.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: All right, fast.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I have a few more questions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just on that and then a couple more things.

chapters.summary: Restler asks about Medicaid recertification preparation. Katz says H&H is increasing navigators and NYC will need to invest in large numbers of people to help with enrollment. He notes food stamp outcomes will predict health insurance outcomes.
chapters.title: CM Restler on Medicaid recertification and navigator investment

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have any suggested models for how, I know we don't yet have rules or policies from the state on exactly what the certification process is going to look like, but any suggestions of things we should be doing now to plan for and invest to help ensure that we're recertifying people

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: and providing that hand-holding?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're already increasing the use of navigators.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, I think that's the key thing is that you can't just assume that people are going to, you know, be able to fax things or scan things or send things to portals or even get the right documentation.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But if we had enough people doing that work, that we could, in fact, do it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I think that New York City will need to invest in a large number of people to help.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: People to enroll.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I don't see any other way around it.
