utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we'll need that over the course of this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, how well or badly we do in the food will tell you a lot about the health insurance.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: A couple other questions.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Does H&H have an estimate of how much you'll be losing from federal state-directed payment cuts?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And do you believe that those cuts could have an impact on the financial sustainability of the H&H Maimonides merger?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, the state, in a sense, the directed payment is the one place we've done better, right, because we got the average commercial rate.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And even when that caps, it's still so much better than anything that we had before.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So, I mean, I think that were it not for the average commercial rate, we would be in a very different picture already.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But it's the average commercial rate that's protecting us.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And do you all have an estimate of how large the financial impact will be from Medicaid and the essential plan coverage loss?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is there a model that you can share on that?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And will the city need to contribute?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I mean, this is what I was getting at before, but I'm just asking it again.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Will the city need to contribute more to maintain services, or are we just waiting on the elections before we make that determination?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, we have factored into our model in this budget, right, that there will be the loss and the impact of EP5, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've also factored in maybe 10% to 13%.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Of our current patients around Medicaid may be disrupted, right, because of these barriers that we'll put in place.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we factor that into our model.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as Dr.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Cass mentioned, the average commercial rate, which was part of HR1 and it was approved by CMS, and we have our third year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: year right before CMS for approval that's really our first line of defense because it throws off a lot of money about two billion dollars for us

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: and as people are being disenrolled from Medicaid our plan right anticipates that they can then draw from the federal dish dollars okay the last question before I get in trouble because I'm getting a dirty look

chapters.summary: Restler asks about NYC Care enrollment trends. Katz reports 10,000 more enrollees than last year, with anecdotal evidence some immigrants have left the program or the country due to federal climate. Restler emphasizes the need for primary and secondary care capacity.
chapters.title: CM Restler on NYC Care enrollment and immigrant community fears

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Any update on NYC care enrollment?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Have the federal attacks on immigrants suppressed enrollment?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And do we anticipate, are we modeling increased enrollment because of the 750,000 New Yorkers that we think are going to lose their health care?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: 10,000 more than previous.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes, we're assuming more people will come in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think anecdotally, all of us, including me, note that there are some people who are, who,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're in the program who just don't seem to be around anymore.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we take that to mean that they've gotten scared of the climate and have returned to their country of origin.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Or are no longer coming in for care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Or just no longer coming in for care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think that the, you know,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: with the anticipated expansion, making sure that we have the primary and secondary care capacity in place, which costs money for you all, is going to be critically important, because this is the only way that an increasing number of New Yorkers are going to have any access to care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you for your work, and thank you, chairs, for your latitude.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I greatly appreciate it.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about correctional health insurance reimbursement—Yang confirms they're federally precluded. Narcisse asks about the CHS budget breakdown; Yang says they don't budget by medical versus mental health, with 95% for patient care and 84% of that for personnel.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on correctional health budget and insurance reimbursement

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Coming back to correctional health care.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does H&H receive any insurance reimbursement?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: When providing care to individual incarcerated individuals?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, we're federally precluded.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah, you're being bad.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: In the executive plan, H&H has a total correctional health services budget of $3.44.7 million in fiscal 2026 and

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: $3.29.8 million in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What specific services are supported with the funding and how is the funding divided between medical services and mental health services?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We don't budget by medical or mental health services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Many of our patients have needs on all sides.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We provide care on an individual basis, and we're staffed for whoever needs whatever they need.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Most of our funding is for, I think it's 95% was...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 95% is for patient care.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And 84% of that is personnel.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about incarcerated individuals served and common mental health issues. Yang reports a 15% increase in custody population from 6,000 to over 7,000, with schizophrenia and schizoaffective disorders most common among seriously mentally ill patients. Wait times are 24-72 hours depending on priority.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on correctional health population and mental health needs

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many incarcerated individuals received services in 2026?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Has the number of the people served increased or decreased in recent years?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What are the most common mental health issues incarcerated people deal with?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, excuse me, the number of patients that we have on any given day has definitely increased steadily and dramatically since COVID, which is where we hit the lowest number.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think just in the last year, it increased about 15% in terms of the number of people in custody from 6,000 to over 7,000.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the most common...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Reasons why people in the mental health service come to us are really adjustment disorders.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The most common diagnosis among serious mentally ill patients is schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorders.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How long is the wait time for incarcerated individuals to access mental health care services?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, we have at least two levels of priority.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: One is stat immediate, which is within 24 hours, and the other is priority, which is within 72 hours of any referral.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will see that patient and rely on the Department of Correction to bring that patient to us or to connect us in some other way.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On an ongoing follow-up basis for patients who are in our therapeutic housing.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: areas we see they're seen on a weekly basis we had been joined online with P Sanchez Purina Sanchez since we have two Sanchez

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: sorry because she's been waiting okay

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How much of correctional health services budget is allocated to families on Rackers Island?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And how does spending differ across other correctional facilities?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm sorry, could you repeat the first part?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The first part is families, but we wouldn't take care of families, but you,

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Your social workers do meet with family members.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our social workers and all our staff will meet with family members regarding their loved one in jail under our care.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And our budget is spread out over all facilities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're always moving staff around to wherever we need.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So you cannot be specific because you're something that you don't.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We don't by jail.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, gotcha.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does the current funding level at the, it's a decorated support continuing for the care for the individual in reentry of our community?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Certainly for the reentry work that we do day one on admission to jail and for what we provide on Rikers Island and really appreciate the making us whole this time.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Like you said, the funding is the same complexity.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: As the city plans for the transition to more base jails, how do you anticipate correctional health services funding needs will change in the future fiscal years?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can't really predict that far ahead in years, but we will go wherever our patients are.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And the prediction at that point in time, and a big focus on the part of the city under this administration, is to see how people can be safely not in jail.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm in agreement.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: People should not be.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: jail, especially those that don't need to be in jail.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I think Dr.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Katz has been talking about it, and I'm totally in agreement.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I was hearing you when you were talking about it with my colleagues.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the Bellevue Outposted Therapeutic Unit opened in April. Yang reports 95 people currently housed at over 90% occupancy, with strong staff and patient responses. Katz notes cost savings from eliminated transport and encourages Council members to visit.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on Bellevue outposted therapeutic unit operations

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The Bellevue Outpost Therapeutic Unit opened this past April.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: We spoke about it earlier.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What update information do you have to share on operation of the new unit since its opening?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's going really well, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Going well?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: April 8th, we brought our first group of patients in.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The first group were the people who had mental health and medical issues.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Again, we're over 90% at this point in time in occupancy, and people still are coming in.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The staff are responding really well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The patients are really responding very, very well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's made a big difference in all fronts.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's a sidebar.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Do you save money?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I think you save more money by actually putting people where they're supposed to.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That's in my head.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: There are definitely, we think, savings because, for one thing, you don't have the transport costs for people who need specialty care, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's very, they have to come on a bus, right?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They have to, you need deputies to escort them.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But I think and I hope all members of the city council will come and visit the outposted unit because it really shows you that you can have correctional health done in a humane setting.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, that having an inhumane setting is not needed.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The point of correctional settings is either to, well, ideally to rehab people, but or to if you're making a decision that this person at this moment has to be punished in this way.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But that doesn't mean that they have to be in cages.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right, and you can really see the difference in the outposted unit, and I think that difference has affected the behavior of the detainees as well as the staff.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many individuals are currently being housed in the unit, at the unit right now?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As of this moment, 95 people.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The budget for the unit is $7.7 million in fiscal 2026 and $8 million in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Do you anticipate any funding increase for the unit?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we have some funding, discrete funding for Bellevue where we reimburse the hospital

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: for services, basic services, some of the custodial, laundry, food, things like that that we buy from Bellevue, as well as to reimburse Bellevue for the specialty services that they provide on unit.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To us.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So that the hospital is not affected just badly.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about medical malpractice defense contracts. Katz confirms 17 firms contracted for specialized cases, with contracts lasting 5-6 years. The budget is $21.3M in FY26 and $21.4M in FY27, covering only legal fees not payouts. About half of cases settle before trial.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on medical malpractice defense contracts and costs

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: In medical malpractice, the executive plan includes additional baseline funding of $4.3 million starting in fiscal 2026,

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: increasing to $5 million by fiscal 2030 for expenses associated with medical malpractice defense contracts.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is it correct that H&H contracts with 12, 15 different law firms for medical malpractice defense?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes, so the budget just corrects to what the existing use has been.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And the $20 million represents a major decrease from what it historically was before we started working so hard to handle these cases well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We actually work with 17 firms, and it's because medical malpractice is very specialized.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So you need a different firm if it's an...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: orthopedic injury versus a pediatric injury or in an obstetric case.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So what's the total budget for FY 2026 and 2027?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Essentially the same.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Does this funding only cover payment to law firms or does it cover payouts and settlement?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Only to lawyers, law firms, it's not the payouts.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So there's other money for the payout?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That is correct.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Are the contracts with these law firms IFPs?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What is the timeline for a contract?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How did H&H choose which firms to contract with?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So contracts are five to six years.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We do bids, but we also pay attention to how responsive the law firm is to us.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And often you can do a better settlement if you're very responsive as opposed to leaving it for a long period of time.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we also try to make sure that we have the right firms for the right kinds of cases because they're so different.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm assuming they are from our city?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes, yes, they're 100%.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How do you evaluate the slow firms and how often do you, yes, I think you just lead to that six on bid.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: You got that already.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: In finding savings, did you assess the medical malpractice contract to find efficiencies?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, the biggest efficiency is that we have our own lawyers, and so that's made a huge difference.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And then we only have to gain help when we need specialty help in an area that is unusual or requires a lot of expert witnesses.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How many medical malpractice suits were filed against H&H in 2025?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How does the number?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: compared to the past years, which H&H location or service areas received the most medical malpractice complaint?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the closeout cases is 450.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Most of the med mal, as you would expect, come from the large hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: The highest number involved the emergency department.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Lowest number come from the long-term care settings.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And about half of the cases we settled before trial.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: They're settled before trial?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: That's the goal, actually, because otherwise you just rack up lawyer fees and it's not going to the person.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And many years.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Which H&H?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, got that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Have you looked into the possibility of hiring in-house counsel instead of relying on outside firms for medical malpractice?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We mostly have in-house.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We only have in-house.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We only use contracts for the very specific kinds of cases that require that kind of expertise.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And they're very good, the inside counsel.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Yeah.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the Maimonides merger delay. Katz says it will go through eventually but requires an equity assessment before going to the state's PHPC committee. He notes $2.4B in state money is waiting and $9M per month in upper payment limit funds could flow once the merger completes.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on Maimonides merger status and delays

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Mamanis, we started talking earlier about it.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Mamanis Health was set to merge with H&H on April 1st.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But right now it's still not going through.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Am I correct?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It will go through eventually, but yes, it's taken a lot longer than any of us had hoped.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So what step are you taking to push the merger through?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is there a new date set?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have not set a date because it's all dependent on the going before the states.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: FIPAC committee, which is the Public Health Planning Committee.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we learned just yesterday that in order to go before that committee, there needs to be another kind of assessment, an equity assessment.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we're now working on the equity assessment, and when the equity assessment is over, then we will go to the committee.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we believe that the committee will support this because everybody recognizes that this is a critical hospital and there is no other.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: choice.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: No one else has offered to help.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So 660 bed hospital with the trauma center, the only children's hospital in Brooklyn, the hospital needs to survive.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have always said throughout this process, somebody else can do it, we're fine with that.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We did not get involved to take on more hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We got involved to preserve a critical resource.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And that's what we're doing.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: If somebody else has another idea and they want, they can put together a proposal, that would be fine.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But this has now gone on months and while we have some opposition, there's no other proposal.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I keep saying it's fine to oppose something, but you need to tell us what you're going to suggest instead.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so far, we haven't made any progress on that conversation.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And that's what I've been asking folks.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What's the solution?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Come up with a plan if you have a better plan.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And I have not heard any.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: There's no money there.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is there a possibility that the term of the merger will change after the resolution of the process that we're going on?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's always possible, but we're, I mean, the state has provided generous support once it goes through.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We have $2.4 billion of state money.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Help save that hospital, but that money cannot flow to the hospital unless this is actually happening.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And it's not, it's a tick-tock moment now because the more we're not doing it, the more money is being, we're losing now?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Nine, we could be giving them through the upper payment limit $9 million more per month for doing exactly the same thing they're doing now.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But as long as there's opposition, the process will be slow.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm going to leave that alone.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I had more questions, but since we got to go somewhere because I don't understand why all this.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about maternal health services and the $513K baseline addition. Katz describes funding for two social workers, fetal heart monitoring, car seats, and portable cribs. He discusses joint mom-baby visits and the importance of postpartum support, with no caps on visit frequency.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H maternal health programs and funding

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: But anyway, maternal health services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: There is a 3.5 million in H&H fiscal 2027 executive budget for maternal medical home services, including an additional of 513,000 baseline

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: added to this plan for maternal mobility and mortality reduction.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: What specific maternity-related program does the additional $513 funding support, and which H&H location does?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I mean, it's going to be operated out.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we're very excited about this funding and very appreciative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It will pay for two additional social workers and a fetal heart monitoring program.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And also car seats.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And portable cribs for new mothers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So really valuable, valuable services.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And portable crib, that's good.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: That can help some parents.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Which other maternal health-related programs are successful at H&H?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Well, I think, I mean, in general, our overall numbers are better than the overall New York City numbers.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So, you know, we do well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: I mean, OB is a very different issue than what I do, internal medicine, because with OB, each time you expect a healthy baby.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: When you're taking care of people who are sick, you understand that sometimes things may not go well.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: But OB is meant to be a happy event.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so anytime either the mother or the baby is hurt, for us, it's very serious and very negative.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: On the other hand, it's always known that a certain number of outcomes will not be what we want it to be.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Our job is to make it as good as we possibly can.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: A lot of the morbidity, we've been teaching people, people hear maternal morbidity and mortality and they think that means the woman

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: is hurt or loses her life while delivering, when really it's in the year after.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's a very complicated dynamic.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: undoubtedly has to do with depression and stress and the difficulties of being a new mom.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And in some cases, there's also mental health issues or substance use issues.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's a complicated, difficult time.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And our job is to do as good a job supporting those moms and those families as we possibly can.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Well, we added, we say the year after delivery is the most crucial moment that you should have support.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So what kind of programs that H&H have to offer to help those process diminish?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So we do everything from, you know, follow-up visits with social workers through mental health practice.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We are also doing a lot of work on trying to arrange visits together with the mom and the baby

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: because we know mothers prioritize the health care of their babies but don't always prioritize their own health care.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And so we're creating at all the hospitals joint visits to do a better job of bringing the women in.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Very nice.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: And how often do they receive those visits?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: As often as necessary.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We are always, as a system, because it's come up in several of the questions, we never cap volume.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Our view is...

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: that there are things we do, there might be things we don't do, but if we do them, we do them for everybody who needs it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: If we provide mental health services, we'll see you as often as you need it.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: All services should be guided by clinical need, not by protocol.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, so there's no cap to the visit.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Have you identified a need to increase resources for maternal health and mental health programs at H&H?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yeah, well, again, this new funding will really help us quite a lot.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And it's going to enable us, we're going to have a new obstetric program that looks at the baby, at the fetal monitors and alerts us when there's an issue with the baby.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And I think that's going to make a big difference for the actual deliveries.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Appreciate that.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about nurse and physician headcount changes. Katz confirms 21 physician positions moved to affiliate payroll, not eliminated. Registered nurses increased by 223 across acute care hospitals. New residencies include psychiatry at Kings County and family practice at Metropolitan.
chapters.title: CM Narcisse on H&H headcount changes and residency expansion

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Headcount changes.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The number of licensed private nurses at H&H has continued to decrease since the first quarter of fiscal 2025

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: from 3.53 to now 3.36 in the third quarter of fiscal 2026.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: I'm pleased, doctor, to see H&H is working on reducing its reliance on private nurses.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: However, the number of physicians continued to decrease from 199 in Q4 of the fiscal 2025 to 178 in Q3 of fiscal 2025.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Have these 21 positions been moved to an affiliated payroll or were they simply eliminated?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: You have it right.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: They moved to an affiliate payroll.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So the physicians were not eliminated.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: It's just a better way for them and us for us to employ them.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: So, and the...

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The registered nurse, let me go to that one.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: The registered nurse headcount increased by 223 in Q3 of fiscal 2026 compared to Q4 of fiscal 2025.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Which H&H locations are the newly hired nurses staff at?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: So it's throughout the acute care hospitals.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Throughout.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Is there any plan to increase the number of residents going forward?

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We've opened up an additional psychiatry residency at Kings County.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: We're going to open a family practice residency at Metropolitan.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz
utterances.text: And we're looking right now at growing some of the other residencies as well.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Very good.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about the Elmhurst ER renovation. Katz confirms the $2.6M project is currently in procurement with a March 2029 completion timeline. Narcisse, who works at Elmhurst ER, celebrates the expansion plans.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.text: I am so happy for them.

utterances.text: All right.

chapters.summary: Narcisse asks about B-Heard team structure and dispatch. Katz reiterates that H&H trains social workers but FDNY handles operations and dispatch. He says the city is interested in expanding B-Heard to more calls but hasn't articulated the model. Specific criteria for call qualification are a fire department question.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Let me calm myself down.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Where will this 10 new Be Heard teams be located?

utterances.text: But you don't know, even though you're not interested in it?

utterances.text: Okay, I love that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Respect that.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz

utterances.text: I think the city is genuinely interested in trying to figure out a way for Be Her to respond to more calls.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay, okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: How are be heard calls currently dispatched and triaged?

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.text: Right now they're running through the FDNY dispatch.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse
utterances.text: Dr.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz

utterances.speaker: Mercedes Narcisse

utterances.text: Well, and actually, we've always tried to put capital money in and push the teacher and everyone to put capital money in.

utterances.speaker: Dr. Mitch Katz

chapters.summary: Chair Narcisse and Chair Lee thank Dr. Katz and the H&H team. Narcisse expresses appreciation for Katz's leadership and optimism about NYC's future. The hearing is officially concluded.
chapters.title: Hearing concludes with thanks from Chairs Narcisse and Lee

summary: FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for DOHMH Public Health, DOHMH Mental Hygiene, and NYC Health + Hospitals, covering budget priorities, federal funding risks, and program investments across the health system.

Key topics include disease outbreak resilience, childhood asthma in the Bronx, sexual health clinics, gender-affirming care, supportive housing, mobile treatment teams, correctional health, the B-Heard program, and the Maimonides merger.
Council members pressed agency leaders on staffing vacancies, federal Medicaid cuts under HR1, harm reduction funding, and plans for the newly created Office of Community Safety.
title: Committee on Mental Health and Substance Use
topic: Executive Budget Hearings - Mental Health and Substance Use
